搜神后记 - Later Records of In Search of the Spirits

    Home

    搜神后记 晋　陶潜 撰

    　●卷一
    　　丁令威，本辽东人，学道于灵虚山。后化鹤归辽，集城门华表柱。时有少年，举弓欲射之。鹤乃飞，徘徊空中而言曰：“有鸟有鸟丁令威，去家千年今始归。城郭如故人民非，何不学仙冢垒垒。”遂高上冲天。今辽东诸丁云其先世有升仙者，但不知名字耳。
    　　嵩高山北有大穴，莫测其深，百姓岁时游观。晋初，尝有一人误堕穴中。同辈冀其傥不死，投食于穴中。坠者得之，为寻穴而行。计可十余曰，忽然见明。又有草屋，中有二人，对坐围棋。局下有一杯白饮。坠者告以饥渴，棋者曰：“可饮此。”遂饮之，气力十倍。棋者曰：“汝欲停此否”坠者不愿停。棋者曰：“从此西行，有天井，其中多蛟龙。但投身入井，自当出。若饿，取井中物食。”坠者如言，半年许，乃出蜀中。归洛下，问张华，华曰：“此仙馆大夫。所饮者，玉浆也；所食者，龙穴石髓也。” [1]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [1]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 搜神后记 - Title meaning 'Supplementary Records of the Supernatural,' indicating a collection of tales about gods, spirits, and the supernatural.
    • 本 - Means 'originally' or 'by birth,' used here to indicate origin or ancestry.
    • 学道 - Refers to the practice of Daoist cultivation or the pursuit of the Dao (Way).
    • 灵虚山 - A specific mountain name, likely a Daoist sacred site, where the protagonist studied.
    • 化鹤 - A metaphor for transformation into a crane, symbolizing immortality or spiritual transcendence.
    • 徘徊空中而言 - Describes the crane circling in the air and speaking, a common motif in Daoist and Buddhist tales of immortals.
    • 城郭如故人民非 - A phrase expressing the idea that while the physical structures of a city remain unchanged, the people have changed over time.
    • 高上冲天 - Means 'soaring high into the sky,' a metaphor for ascending to heaven or achieving immortality.
    • 冢垒垒 - Refers to grave mounds or burial sites, often used to symbolize the passage of time and the mortality of humans.
    • 有鸟有鸟丁令威 - A poetic refrain, repeating the name 'Ding Lingwei' to emphasize the immortality and return of the protagonist.
    • 嵩高山 - The Songshan Mountains, a famous range in central China, often associated with Daoist and Buddhist traditions.
    • 大穴 - Refers to a large cave or pit, possibly a natural formation or a Daoist hermitage.
    • 莫测其深 - Means 'impossible to measure its depth,' emphasizing the mystery and vastness of the cave.
    • 百姓岁时游观 - Means 'common people visit and observe during festivals,' indicating the cave's cultural significance.
    • 投食于穴中 - Describes the act of throwing food into the cave, a gesture of goodwill or curiosity.
    • 玉浆 - Refers to a mythical drink of jade essence, often associated with immortality in Daoist texts.
    • 龙穴石髓 - Refers to a mythical substance found in dragon caves, often associated with longevity and immortality.
    • 天井 - Refers to a natural or artificial well, often used in Daoist texts to symbolize a portal to the underworld or a hidden realm.
    • 蛟龙 - Refers to water dragons, mythical creatures often associated with the depths of rivers and lakes.
    • 投身入井 - Means 'to throw oneself into the well,' a metaphor for a leap of faith or a desperate act.
    • 蜀中 - Refers to the region of Sichuan, known for its rugged terrain and rich cultural history.
    • 归洛下 - Means 'returning to Luoyang,' indicating the protagonist's journey back to a familiar place.
    • 博学求之 - Means 'to seek broadly and deeply,' emphasizing the pursuit of knowledge and wisdom.
    • 无不利于事也 - Means 'nothing is unhelpful in matters,' suggesting that all efforts and experiences are valuable.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 丁令威 - A legendary figure in Chinese folklore, known for his transformation into a crane and his return to his hometown after a long absence.
    • 辽东 - A historical region in northeastern China, known for its rich cultural and natural heritage.
    • 灵虚山 - A specific mountain, likely a Daoist sacred site, where the protagonist studied.
    • 嵩高山 - The Songshan Mountains, a famous range in central China, often associated with Daoist and Buddhist traditions.
    • 张华 - A historical figure, a scholar and official during the Western Jin dynasty, known for his knowledge and wisdom.
    • 晋 - Refers to the Western Jin dynasty, a period of Chinese history marked by political instability and cultural flourishing.
    • 陶潜 - The author of the text, a renowned poet and writer from the Eastern Jin dynasty, known for his works on Daoist themes and his reclusive lifestyle.

    Proper Nouns

    • 搜神后记 - The title of the text.
    • 晋 - The Western Jin dynasty.
    • 陶潜 - The author of the text.
    • 丁令威 - The legendary figure in the first story.
    • 辽东 - The historical region where the first story takes place.
    • 灵虚山 - The mountain where the protagonist studied.
    • 嵩高山 - The Songshan Mountains.
    • 张华 - A historical figure and scholar.
    • 蜀中 - The region of Sichuan.
    • 洛下 - Luoyang, the capital city.
    • 仙馆大夫 - A term for high-ranking immortals in the celestial bureaucracy.
    • 玉浆 - A mythical drink of jade essence.
    • 龙穴石髓 - A mythical substance found in dragon caves.

    Supplementary Records of the Supernatural
    Compiled by Tao Qian of the Jin Dynasty

    Chapter One

    Ding Lingwei, originally from Liaodong, studied Daoist cultivation on Lingxu Mountain. Later, he transformed into a crane and returned to Liaodong, where he alighted on the ornamental pillars of the city gate. At that time, a young man raised his bow to shoot the crane. The crane then flew, circling in the air and speaking: "There is a bird, a bird named Ding Lingwei. After a thousand years, I return home. The city remains the same, but the people have changed. Why did you not learn the Dao and become an immortal, leaving behind only grave mounds?" The crane then soared high into the sky. Today, the people of Liaodong say that their ancestors had ascended to immortality, but they do not know the names of these immortals.

    To the north of Songshan Mountain, there is a large cave whose depth cannot be measured. Common people visit and observe it during festivals. In the early Jin dynasty, there was once a man who accidentally fell into the cave. His companions hoped he might not die and threw food into the cave. The man who fell found the food and began to explore the cave. After about ten days, he suddenly saw light. He then came upon a small thatched house, where two people were sitting across from each other playing chess. On the chessboard lay a cup of white liquid. The man who fell told them of his hunger and thirst. One of the chess players said, "You may drink this." He drank it, and his strength increased tenfold. The chess player asked, "Do you wish to stay here?" The man did not want to stay. The chess player said, "Continue to the west, where there is a natural well filled with many water dragons. Simply throw yourself into the well, and you will emerge. If you are hungry, take something from the well to eat." The man followed their instructions and, after about six months, emerged in Sichuan. He returned to Luoyang and asked Zhang Hua about his experience. Zhang Hua said, "These were officials of the celestial bureaucracy. The drink you consumed was jade essence, and the food you ate was the stone marrow from a dragon cave." [1]

    会稽剡县民袁相、根硕二人猎，经深山重岭甚多，见一群山羊六七头，逐之。经一石桥，甚狭而峻。羊去，根等亦随渡，向绝崖。崖正赤，壁立，名曰赤城。上有水流下，广狭如匹布，剡人谓之瀑布。羊径有山穴如门，豁然而过。既入，内甚平敞，草木皆香。有一小屋，二女子住其中，年皆十五六，容色甚美，著青衣。一名莹珠，一名洁玉。见二人至，忻然云：“早望汝来。”遂为室家。忽二女出行，云复有得婿者，往庆之。曳履于绝岩上行，琅琅然。二人思归，潜去归路。二女已知，追还，乃谓曰：“自可去。”乃以一腕囊与根等，语曰：“慎勿开也。”于是乃归。后出行，家人开视其囊，囊如莲花，一重去，一重复，至五盖，中有小青鸟，飞去。根还知此，怅然而已。后根于田中耕，家依常饷之，见在田中不动，就视，但有壳如蝉蜕也。 [2]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [2]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 深山重岭 - Describes a region with deep mountains and multiple ridges, emphasizing the rugged and remote setting.
    • 石桥 - A stone bridge, which is a common feature in ancient Chinese literature, often symbolizing a threshold or boundary.
    • 绝崖 - A sheer cliff, indicating a dramatic and dangerous landscape.
    • 赤城 - A mountain in Zhejiang, known for its red cliffs and scenic beauty. The name '赤城' (Red City) is a poetic reference to the mountain's appearance.
    • 瀑布 - A waterfall, which is a common natural feature in Chinese literature, often associated with beauty and tranquility.
    • 山穴 - A mountain cave, which can be a place of mystery and transformation.
    • 豁然 - Describes a sudden and clear opening, often used to convey a sense of discovery or revelation.
    • 草木皆香 - All plants and trees emit a fragrance, suggesting a paradisiacal environment.
    • 小屋 - A small house, which in this context is likely a simple dwelling.
    • 二女子 - Two women, who are described as having names (莹珠 and 洁玉), indicating they are significant characters.
    • 室家 - A household or family, suggesting a marital relationship.
    • 得婿者 - Someone who has found a husband, implying a social event or celebration.
    • 绝岩 - A sheer cliff, emphasizing the dangerous and inaccessible nature of the landscape.
    • 琅琅然 - Describes a clear and resonant sound, often used to describe the sound of footsteps or other actions.
    • 慎勿开也 - Be careful not to open it, indicating a warning or prohibition.
    • 囊 - A bag or pouch, which in this context is a magical or mystical object.
    • 一重去，一重复 - One layer after another, describing the structure of the bag.
    • 小青鸟 - A small green bird, which is a common symbol in Chinese literature, often representing freedom or a messenger.
    • 田中耕 - Working in the fields, indicating agricultural labor.
    • 壳如蝉蜕 - A shell like a cicada's molt, suggesting a transformation or shedding of an old form.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 会稽剡县 - Yandian County in Kuaiji, a region known for its natural beauty and historical significance.
    • 赤城 - A famous mountain in Zhejiang, known for its red cliffs and scenic beauty.
    • 瀑布 - Waterfalls are often associated with natural beauty and spiritual significance in Chinese literature.
    • 山穴 - Caves are often depicted as places of mystery and transformation, sometimes leading to otherworldly realms.
    • 小青鸟 - In Chinese mythology, small green birds are often associated with messengers or symbols of freedom and transformation.

    Proper Nouns

    • 袁相 - A name, likely a character in the story.
    • 根硕 - A name, likely a character in the story.
    • 莹珠 - A name, likely a character in the story.
    • 洁玉 - A name, likely a character in the story.
    • 赤城 - A mountain in Zhejiang, known for its red cliffs and scenic beauty.
    • 瀑布 - A waterfall, which is a common natural feature in Chinese literature.

    In Yandian County of Kuaiji, there were two men named Yuan Xiang and Gen Shuo who went hunting. They traversed many deep mountains and ridges, and saw a group of six or seven wild goats. They chased the goats and crossed a narrow and steep stone bridge. As the goats ran, Gen and his companions followed, heading towards a sheer cliff. The cliff was bright red and stood upright, known as the Red City. Above it, water flowed down, wide and narrow like a piece of cloth, which the people of Yandian called a waterfall. There was a mountain cave like a door, and they passed through it suddenly. Inside, it was very flat and spacious, with fragrant grass and trees. There was a small house where two young women lived, both around fifteen or sixteen years old, with beautiful appearances, dressed in green clothes. One was named Yingzhu, and the other was named Jieyu. When the two men arrived, the women happily said, "We have been expecting you." They then became husband and wife. Suddenly, the two women went out, saying that someone else had also found a husband and went to congratulate them. They walked on the sheer cliff, making a clear and resonant sound. The two men wanted to return home, secretly leaving the way back. The two women already knew, and they chased them back, saying, "You can go." They then gave a small bag to Gen and his companions, telling them, "Be careful not to open it." So they returned home. Later, when they went out, their family opened the bag to look inside. The bag was like a lotus, with one layer after another, up to five layers, and inside was a small green bird that flew away. Gen returned and knew this, feeling sad. Later, when Gen was working in the fields, his family brought him food as usual, but he was seen standing still in the field. They approached to see, and there was only a shell like a cicada's molt. [2]

    荥阳人，姓何，忘其名，有名闻士也。荆州辟为别驾，不就，隐遁养志。常至田舍，人收获在场上。忽有一人，长丈余，萧疏单衣，角巾，来诣之。翩翩举其两手，并舞而来，语何云：“君曾见‘韶舞’不此是‘韶舞’。”且舞且去。何寻逐，径向一山，山有穴，才容一人。其人即入穴，何亦随之入。初甚急，前辄闲旷，便失人，见有良田数十顷。何遂垦作，以为世业。子孙至今赖之。 [3]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [3]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 隐遁养志 - A lifestyle of withdrawal and self-cultivation, common among scholars and recluses.
    • 世业 - Family legacy, indicating the long-term impact on the family.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 荥阳 - A place name, indicating the person's origin.
    • 荆州 - An ancient Chinese province, indicating the location of the appointment.
    • 别驾 - A high-ranking official position, indicating the importance of the offer.
    • 韶舞 - An ancient dance, often associated with the Yellow Emperor and the Five Emperors, symbolizing harmony and grace.
    • 良田数十顷 - Several hundred acres of good land, indicating a significant amount of fertile land.
    • 子孙至今赖之 - Descendants still rely on it, indicating the lasting benefit.

    Proper Nouns

    • 荥阳 - A place name.
    • 荆州 - An ancient Chinese province.
    • 别驾 - A high-ranking official position.
    • 韶舞 - An ancient dance.
    • 良田数十顷 - Several hundred acres of good land.
    • 世业 - Family legacy.
    • 子孙至今赖之 - Descendants still rely on it.

    A man from Xingyang, whose name is forgotten, was a well-known scholar. The Governor of Jingzhou appointed him as a vice governor, but he refused the appointment and retreated to seclude himself and cultivate his aspirations. One day, he went to a farm where people were harvesting their crops on the field. Suddenly, a person appeared, over a zhang tall, wearing a loose, simple robe and a headband, and came towards him. Gracefully raising both hands, the person danced towards him and said, "Have you ever seen the 'Shao Dance'? This is the 'Shao Dance.'" Dancing and leaving, the person departed. He followed, directly towards a mountain. The mountain had a cave, just big enough for one person. The person immediately entered the cave, and he also followed in. At first, it was very narrow, then it became spacious, and he lost sight of the person. He saw several hundred acres of good land. He then cultivated it as his family's legacy, and his descendants still rely on it. [3]

    晋太元中，武陵人捕鱼为业。缘溪行，忘路之远近，忽逢桃花林，夹岸数百步，中无杂树，芳华鲜美，落英缤纷。渔人甚异之（渔人姓黄名道真）。复前行，欲穷其林。林尽水源，便得一山。山有小口，彷佛若有光。便舍舟，从口入。初极狭，才通人。复行数十步，豁然开朗，土地旷空，屋舍俨然。有良田、美池、桑竹之属。阡陌交通，鸡犬相闻。男女衣著，悉如外人。黄发垂髫，并怡然自乐。见渔人，大惊，问所从来，具答之。便要还家，为设酒杀鸡作食。村中人闻有此人，咸来问讯。自云先世避秦难，率妻子邑人至此绝境，不复出焉，遂与外隔。问今是何世，乃不知有汉，无论魏晋。此人一一具言所闻，皆为叹惋。余人各复延至其家，皆出酒食。停数曰，辞去。此中人语云：“不足为外人道也。”既出，得其船，便扶向路，处处志之。及郡，乃诣太守，说如此。太守刘歆，即遣人随之往，寻向所志，不复得也。 [4]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [4]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 晋太元中 - Refers to the reign period of Emperor Hui of Jin (376–386 CE).
    • 捕鱼为业 - Means 'engaged in fishing as a profession.'
    • 缘溪行 - Means 'following the stream.'
    • 忘路之远近 - Means 'forgetting how far or near the road was.'
    • 忽逢桃花林 - Means 'suddenly came upon a peach blossom forest.'
    • 夹岸数百步 - Means 'on both banks for several hundred steps.'
    • 中无杂树 - Means 'no mixed trees in between.'
    • 芳华鲜美，落英缤纷 - Describes the beautiful flowers and the falling petals.
    • 渔人甚异之 - Means 'the fisherman found it very strange.'
    • 复前行，欲穷其林 - Means 'went forward again, wanting to explore the entire forest.'
    • 林尽水源 - Means 'the forest ended at the water source.'
    • 便得一山 - Means 'and found a mountain.'
    • 山有小口，彷佛若有光 - Means 'there was a small opening in the mountain, as if there was light inside.'
    • 便舍舟，从口入 - Means 'left his boat and entered through the opening.'
    • 初极狭，才通人 - Means 'it was very narrow at first, barely wide enough for a person to pass through.'
    • 复行数十步，豁然开朗 - Means 'after walking several dozen steps, it suddenly opened up into an open space.'
    • 土地旷空，屋舍俨然 - Describes the open land and neatly arranged houses.
    • 有良田、美池、桑竹之属 - Means 'there were good fields, beautiful ponds, mulberry trees, and bamboo.'
    • 阡陌交通，鸡犬相闻 - Describes the well-connected paths and the sounds of chickens and dogs.
    • 男女衣著，悉如外人 - Means 'the men and women were dressed just like those outside.'
    • 黄发垂髫，并怡然自乐 - Means 'the elderly and children were all happy and content.'
    • 见渔人，大惊，问所从来，具答之 - Means 'they saw the fisherman and were greatly surprised, asking where he came from, and he answered them in detail.'
    • 便要还家，为设酒杀鸡作食 - Means 'they invited him to return home, preparing wine, killing a chicken, and cooking food for him.'
    • 村中人闻有此人，咸来问讯 - Means 'the villagers heard about this person and all came to inquire.'
    • 自云先世避秦难，率妻子邑人至此绝境，不复出焉，遂与外隔 - Means 'they said their ancestors fled from the Qin troubles, leading their wives, children, and fellow townspeople to this isolated place, never leaving again, thus being cut off from the outside world.'
    • 问今是何世，乃不知有汉，无论魏晋 - Means 'they asked what era it was now, and they did not know about the Han dynasty, let alone the Wei and Jin dynasties.'
    • 此人一一具言所闻，皆为叹惋 - Means 'he told them everything he had heard, and they all sighed in amazement.'
    • 余人各复延至其家，皆出酒食 - Means 'the others also invited him to their homes, all offering wine and food.'
    • 停数曰，辞去 - Means 'he stayed for several days before taking his leave.'
    • 此中人语云：“不足为外人道也” - Means 'the people here told him, 'It is not worth telling outsiders about.''
    • 既出，得其船，便扶向路，处处志之 - Means 'after leaving, he found his boat, retraced his steps, and marked the path everywhere.'
    • 及郡，乃诣太守，说如此 - Means 'when he reached the county, he went to the governor and told him about it.'
    • 太守刘歆，即遣人随之往，寻向所志，不复得也 - Means 'Governor Liu Xun immediately sent people to follow him, searching for the marks he had made, but they could not find the way again.'

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 晋太元中 - Refers to the reign period of Emperor Hui of Jin, a time of relative peace and prosperity.
    • 桃花源 - The name of the idyllic village, which has become a symbol of an idealized utopia in Chinese literature.
    • 秦难 - Refers to the Qin dynasty's harsh rule, often associated with the tyranny of Qin Shi Huang.
    • 汉、魏、晋 - Refers to the Han, Wei, and Jin dynasties, significant periods in Chinese history.

    Proper Nouns

    • 晋太元中 - The reign period of Emperor Hui of Jin.
    • 武陵人 - The fisherman from Wuling.
    • 桃花源 - The idyllic village.
    • 太守刘歆 - The governor Liu Xun.

    During the reign of Emperor Hui of Jin, a man from Wuling made a living by fishing. Following the stream, he forgot how far he had traveled, and suddenly came upon a peach blossom forest. On both banks for several hundred steps, there were no mixed trees, only beautiful flowers and falling petals. The fisherman found it very strange. He went forward again, wanting to explore the entire forest. The forest ended at the water source, and he found a mountain. There was a small opening in the mountain, as if there was light inside. He left his boat and entered through the opening. It was very narrow at first, barely wide enough for a person to pass through. After walking several dozen steps, it suddenly opened up into an open space. The land was open, and the houses were neatly arranged. There were good fields, beautiful ponds, mulberry trees, and bamboo. The paths were well-connected, and the sounds of chickens and dogs were heard. The men and women were dressed just like those outside. The elderly and children were all happy and content. They saw the fisherman and were greatly surprised, asking where he came from, and he answered them in detail. They invited him to return home, preparing wine, killing a chicken, and cooking food for him. The villagers heard about this person and all came to inquire. They said their ancestors fled from the Qin troubles, leading their wives, children, and fellow townspeople to this isolated place, never leaving again, thus being cut off from the outside world. They asked what era it was now, and they did not know about the Han dynasty, let alone the Wei and Jin dynasties. He told them everything he had heard, and they all sighed in amazement. The others also invited him to their homes, all offering wine and food. He stayed for several days before taking his leave. The people here told him, "It is not worth telling outsiders about." After leaving, he found his boat, retraced his steps, and marked the path everywhere. When he reached the county, he went to the governor and told him about it. Governor Liu Xun immediately sent people to follow him, searching for the marks he had made, but they could not find the way again. [4]

    南阳刘 之，字子骥，好游山水。尝采药至衡山，深入忘反。见有一涧水，水南有二石，一闭一开。水深广，不得渡。欲还，失道，遇伐薪人，问径，仅得还家。或说中皆仙方、灵药及诸杂物。 之欲更寻索，不复知处矣。
    　　长沙醴陵县有小水，有二人乘船取樵，见岸下士穴中水逐流出，有新砍木片逐流下，深山中有人迹，异之。乃相谓曰：“可试如水中看何由尔”一人便以笠自障，入穴，穴才容人。行数十步，便开明朗，然不异世间。
    　　平乐县有山临水，岩间有两目，如人眼，极大，瞳子白黑分明，名为“目岩”。
    　　始兴机山东有两岩，相向如鸱尾。石室数十所。经过皆闻有金石、丝竹之响。 [5]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [5]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 好游山水 - Classical expression meaning 'to enjoy traveling and exploring mountains and rivers.'
    • 深入忘反 - Literally 'delve deeply and forget to return,' meaning to get so engrossed in exploration that one loses track of time.
    • 石 - Refers to stone chambers or caves; the character  (likely a typographical error or variant of 窟) means 'cave' or 'hole.'
    • 水南有二石，一闭一开 - Describes a scene where two stone caves on the south side of the river are either closed or open.
    • 欲还，失道 - Means 'intending to return, lost the way,' indicating that the person got disoriented.
    • 或说中皆仙方、灵药及诸杂物 - A statement about someone claiming that the caves contain magical formulas, elixirs, and various objects.
    • 欲更寻索，不复知处矣 - Means 'wanting to search again, but no longer knowing the location,' indicating that the person cannot find the place anymore.
    • 尝采药至衡山 - Means 'once gathered herbs to Hengshan Mountain.'
    • 水深广，不得渡 - Describes a deep and wide river that cannot be crossed.
    • 仅得还家 - Means 'barely managed to return home.'
    • 伐薪人 - Refers to a woodcutter.
    • 问径 - Means 'asked for the way' or 'inquired about the path.'
    • 可试如水中看何由尔 - Means 'you can try going into the water to see how it is.'
    • 入穴 - Means 'enter the cave.'
    • 穴才容人 - Means 'the cave is just big enough for a person.'
    • 行数十步 - Means 'walked several dozen steps.'
    • 开明朗 - Means 'opened up into a bright and spacious area.'
    • 金石、丝竹之响 - Refers to the sounds of metal, stone, strings, and bamboo, symbolizing musical and harmonious sounds.
    • 目岩 - Refers to a rock formation that resembles eyes.
    • 相向如鸱尾 - Means 'facing each other like the tail of a kite' or 'facing each other like the tail of a mythical bird.'
    • 石室数十所 - Means 'dozens of stone chambers.'
    • 经过皆闻有金石、丝竹之响 - Means 'those who pass by all hear the sounds of metal, stone, strings, and bamboo.'

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 南阳 - Refers to Nanyang, a region in Henan Province.
    • 刘子骥 - A historical figure, Liu Ziji, known for his interest in Daoist practices and exploration.
    • 衡山 - Refers to Mount Heng, one of the Five Sacred Mountains of China.
    • 长沙醴陵县 - Refers to Liling County in Changsha, Hunan Province.
    • 平乐县 - Refers to Pingle County, possibly in Guangxi or Guangdong.
    • 目岩 - A specific rock formation with a unique appearance.
    • 机山东有两岩 - Refers to two rock formations on Jishan Mountain.
    • 石室 - Refers to natural stone chambers or caves.
    • 金石、丝竹之响 - Symbolizes the sounds of nature and music, often associated with serene and harmonious environments.

    Proper Nouns

    • 南阳 - Nanyang, a region in Henan Province.
    • 刘子骥 - Liu Ziji, a historical figure known for his interest in Daoist practices and exploration.
    • 衡山 - Mount Heng, one of the Five Sacred Mountains of China.
    • 长沙醴陵县 - Liling County in Changsha, Hunan Province.
    • 平乐县 - Pingle County, possibly in Guangxi or Guangdong.
    • 目岩 - A specific rock formation with a unique appearance.
    • 机山东有两岩 - Two rock formations on Jishan Mountain.
    • 石室 - Natural stone chambers or caves.
    • 金石、丝竹之响 - The sounds of metal, stone, strings, and bamboo.

    Translation:

    Liu Ziji of Nanyang, also known as Ziji, enjoyed traveling and exploring mountains and rivers. Once, while gathering herbs on Mount Heng, he delved so deeply that he forgot to return. He saw a stream, with two stone caves on the south side, one closed and one open. The stream was deep and wide, making it impossible to cross. Intending to return, he lost his way and encountered a woodcutter. He asked for directions and barely managed to return home. Someone claimed that the caves contained magical formulas, elixirs, and various objects. Liu Ziji wanted to search again but could no longer find the location.

    In Liling County of Changsha, there was a small stream. Two people were rowing a boat to gather firewood when they saw water flowing out of a hole in the bank, with freshly cut wood pieces floating downstream. They were surprised to find human footprints in the deep mountains. They said to each other, "You can try entering the water to see what it is like." One of them put on a hat to cover himself and entered the cave, which was just big enough for a person. After walking several dozen steps, he found himself in an open and bright space, yet it was no different from the world outside.

    In Pingle County, there is a mountain overlooking water, with rock formations resembling eyes, large and clear, with distinct white and black pupils, known as "Eye Rock."

    On Jishan Mountain in Shixing, there are two rock formations facing each other like the tail of a mythical bird. There are dozens of stone chambers. Those who pass by all hear the sounds of metal, stone, strings, and bamboo. [5]

    中宿县有贞女峡。峡西岸水际有石如人影，状似女子。是曰“贞女”。父老相传，秦世有女数人，取螺于此，遇风雨昼昏，而一女化为此石。
    　　临城县南四十里有盖山，百许步有姑舒泉。昔有舒女，与父析薪于此泉。女因坐，牵挽不动，乃还告家。比还，唯见清泉湛然。女母曰：“吾女好音乐。”乃作弦歌，泉涌洄流，有朱鲤一双，今人作乐嬉戏，泉故涌出。

    　　●卷二
    　　吴舍人名猛，字世云，有道术。同县邹惠政迎猛，夜于家中庭烧香。忽有虎抱政儿超篱去。猛语云：“无所苦，须臾当还。”虎去数十步，忽然复送儿归。政遂精进，乞为好道士。猛性至孝，小儿时，在父母傍卧，时夏曰多蚊虫，而终不摇扇。同宿人觉，问其故，答云：“惧蚊虫去啮我父母尔。”及父母终，行伏墓次。蜀贼纵暴，焚烧邑屋，发掘坟垅。民人进窜。猛在墓侧，号恸不去。贼为之感怆，遂不犯。 [6]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [6]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 中宿縣 - A specific county name in China, which should be kept as is in the translation.
    • 貞女 - Can be translated as 'chaste maiden' or 'virtuous girl.'
    • 秦世 - Refers to the Qin dynasty.
    • 螺 - Shellfish, specifically referring to abalone in this context.
    • 風雨昼昏 - A phrase describing a stormy day with heavy rain and darkness.
    • 化為此石 - Means 'transformed into this stone.'
    • 蓋山 - A specific mountain name, which should be kept as is in the translation.
    • 姑舒泉 - A specific spring name, which should be kept as is in the translation.
    • 舒女 - Refers to a girl named Shu.
    • 析薪 - Means 'to chop firewood.'
    • 牽挽不動 - Means 'could not be moved by pulling.'
    • 弦歌 - Refers to playing stringed instruments while singing.
    • 清泉湛然 - Means 'clear spring water, serene and still.'
    • 好音樂 - Means 'likes music.'
    • 朱鲤 - Red carp, often associated with auspiciousness in Chinese culture.
    • 嬉戏 - Means 'play' or 'recreation.'
    • 涌出 - Means 'erupts' or 'pours out.'
    • 吳舍人名猛 - Refers to a person named Wu Meng, who was a scholar-official.
    • 有道術 - Means 'possessed of magical arts.'
    • 同縣 - Means 'in the same county.'
    • 邹惠政 - A specific person's name, which should be kept as is in the translation.
    • 夜于家中庭烧香 - Means 'burned incense in the courtyard at night.'
    • 虎抱政儿超篱去 - Means 'a tiger carried away Zheng's child over the fence.'
    • 无所苦 - Means 'no harm done.'
    • 须臾当还 - Means 'will return shortly.'
    • 数十步 - Means 'several dozen steps.'
    • 号恸不去 - Means 'cried bitterly and did not leave.'
    • 蜀贼 - Refers to bandits from the Shu region.
    • 纵暴 - Means 'committed acts of violence.'
    • 发掘坟垅 - Means 'excavated graves.'
    • 民人进窜 - Means 'people fled.'
    • 号恸 - Means 'cried bitterly.'
    • 感怆 - Means 'moved to sorrow.'
    • 遂不犯 - Means 'therefore did not attack.'

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 貞女 - In Chinese culture, '貞女' refers to a woman who remains chaste and virtuous, often used in legends and stories.
    • 秦世 - The Qin dynasty (221-206 BCE) was known for its strict laws and centralized government.
    • 螺 - Abalone is a type of shellfish that was often consumed by ancient Chinese people.

    In Zhongxu County, there is a Chaste Maiden Gorge. On the western bank of the gorge, near the water's edge, there is a stone that resembles a human shadow, shaped like a woman. This is called "Chaste Maiden." According to local elders, during the Qin dynasty, several girls came here to gather abalone. During a stormy day with heavy rain and darkness, one girl transformed into this stone.

    Forty li south of Lincheng County, there is Gaishan Mountain, and about a hundred steps away is the Gushu Spring. Once, a girl named Shu was gathering firewood with her father at this spring. The girl sat down, and no matter how hard she was pulled, she could not be moved. She then returned home to inform her family. By the time she got back, she only saw serene and still clear spring water. Her mother said, "My daughter loves music." So, they played stringed instruments and sang, causing the spring water to surge and flow in circles, and a pair of red carp appeared. Now, when people play music and engage in recreational activities, the spring water surges out.

    In Volume Two:
    Wu Meng, a scholar-official with magical arts, was invited by his fellow countyman Zou Huizheng. At night, they burned incense in the courtyard. Suddenly, a tiger carried away Zou's child over the fence. Wu Meng said, "There is no harm, he will return shortly." The tiger went several dozen steps and then suddenly returned the child. Zou then became more diligent and asked to become a good Taoist. Wu Meng was extremely filial. When he was a small child, he slept beside his parents during the summer when there were many mosquitoes, but he never fanned himself. His fellow sleeper noticed and asked why, and he replied, "I fear that the mosquitoes will bite my parents." When his parents passed away, he lay by their graves. During a bandit raid, the bandits committed acts of violence, burned houses, and excavated graves. The people fled. Wu Meng lay by the grave and cried bitterly, not leaving. The bandits were moved to sorrow and did not attack.

    Source text:
    中宿县有贞女峡。峡西岸水际有石如人影，状似女子。是曰“贞女”。父老相传，秦世有女数人，取螺于此，遇风雨昼昏，而一女化为此石。
    　　临城县南四十里有盖山，百许步有姑舒泉。昔有舒女，与父析薪于此泉。女因坐，牵挽不动，乃还告家。比还，唯见清泉湛然。女母曰：“吾女好音乐。”乃作弦歌，泉涌洄流，有朱鲤一双，今人作乐嬉戏，泉故涌出。

    　　●卷二
    　　吴舍人名猛，字世云，有道术。同县邹惠政迎猛，夜于家中庭烧香。忽有虎抱政儿超篱去。猛语云：“无所苦，须臾当还。”虎去数十步，忽然复送儿归。政遂精进，乞为好道士。猛性至孝，小儿时，在父母傍卧，时夏曰多蚊虫，而终不摇扇。同宿人觉，问其故，答云：“惧蚊虫去啮我父母尔。”及父母终，行伏墓次。蜀贼纵暴，焚烧邑屋，发掘坟垅。民人进窜。猛在墓侧，号恸不去。贼为之感怆，遂不犯。

    Provide only the final translation with no additional commentary. [6]

    谢允从武当山还，在桓宣武座，有言及左元放为曹公致鲈鱼者，允便云：“此可得尔。”求大瓮盛水，朱书符投水中。俄有一鲤鱼鼓鳍水中。
    　　钱塘杜子恭，有秘术。尝就人借瓜刀，其主求子，子恭曰：“当即相还耳。”既而刀主行至嘉兴，有鱼跃入船中。破鱼腹，得瓜刀。
    　　太兴中，衡阳区纯作鼠市：四方丈余，开四门，门有一木人。纵四五鼠于中，欲出门，木人辄以手推之。
    　　晋大司马桓温，字元子。末年，忽有一比丘尼，失其名，来自远方，投温为檀越。尼才行不恒，愠其敬待，居之门内。尼每浴，必至移时。温疑而窥之。见尼裸身挥刀，破腹出脏，断截身首，支分脔切。温怪骇而还。及至尼出浴室，身形如常。温以实问，尼答曰：“若逐凌君上，形当如之。”时温方谋问鼎，闻之怅然。故以戒惧，终守臣节。尼后辞去，不知所在。 [7]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [7]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 從武當山還 - Formal expression meaning 'returning from Mount Wudang,' indicating the location and context of the events.
    • 有言及左元放為曹公致鲈鱼者 - Phrase meaning 'there was mention of Zuo Yuanfang providing perch to Cao Cao,' where '致' means 'to provide' or 'to send.'
    • 此可得尔 - Colloquial expression meaning 'this is possible,' indicating a response to the previous statement.
    • 求大瓮盛水 - Phrase meaning 'asked for a large jar to hold water,' where '盛' means 'to hold' or 'to contain.'
    • 朱书符投水中 - Phrase meaning 'wrote a red talisman and threw it into the water,' where '朱书' means 'red writing' and '符' means 'talisman.'
    • 俄有一鲤鱼鼓鳍水中 - Phrase meaning 'soon there was a carp flapping its fins in the water,' where '俄' means 'soon' and '鼓鳍' means 'flapping fins.'
    • 有秘术 - Phrase meaning 'has secret techniques,' where '秘术' refers to esoteric or mystical abilities.
    • 尝就人借瓜刀 - Phrase meaning 'once borrowed a melon knife from someone,' where '尝' means 'once' and '借' means 'to borrow.'
    • 其主求子 - Phrase meaning 'the owner asked for the return of the knife,' where '求子' means 'asked for the return.'
    • 当即相还耳 - Phrase meaning 'I will return it immediately,' where '当即' means 'immediately' and '相还' means 'to return.'
    • 既而刀主行至嘉兴 - Phrase meaning 'afterward, the owner of the knife traveled to Jiaxing,' where '既而' means 'afterward' and '行至' means 'traveled to.'
    • 有鱼跃入船中 - Phrase meaning 'a fish jumped into the boat,' where '跃入' means 'jumped into.'
    • 破鱼腹，得瓜刀 - Phrase meaning 'opened the fish's belly and found the melon knife,' where '破' means 'to open' and '得' means 'to find.'
    • 太兴中 - Phrase meaning 'during the Tai Xing period,' referring to a specific era in Chinese history.
    • 衡阳区纯作鼠市 - Phrase meaning 'Hengyang Qu Chun made a mouse market,' where '作' means 'to make' and '鼠市' means 'mouse market.'
    • 四方丈余 - Phrase meaning 'four sides measuring over a zhang,' where '丈余' means 'over a zhang' (a unit of length).
    • 开四门 - Phrase meaning 'with four doors,' where '开' means 'to open' and '门' means 'doors.'
    • 门有一木人 - Phrase meaning 'each door had a wooden figure,' where '木人' means 'wooden figure.'
    • 纵四五鼠于中 - Phrase meaning 'released four or five mice inside,' where '纵' means 'to release' and '于中' means 'inside.'
    • 欲出门，木人辄以手推之 - Phrase meaning 'if they tried to leave, the wooden figure would push them back,' where '辄' means 'always' and '推之' means 'push them back.'
    • 晋大司马桓温，字元子 - Formal introduction meaning 'Duke Huan Wen of Jin, courtesy name Yuanzi,' where '大司马' means 'Grand Marshal' and '字' means 'courtesy name.'
    • 末年 - Phrase meaning 'in his later years,' where '末年' means 'in the final years.'
    • 忽有一比丘尼 - Phrase meaning 'suddenly there was a nun,' where '忽' means 'suddenly' and '比丘尼' means 'nun.'
    • 失其名 - Phrase meaning 'whose name was unknown,' where '失' means 'lost' and '其名' means 'her name.'
    • 来自远方 - Phrase meaning 'from a distant place,' where '来自' means 'from' and '远方' means 'distant place.'
    • 投温为檀越 - Phrase meaning 'offered herself to Huan Wen as a patron,' where '投' means 'to offer' and '檀越' means 'patron.'
    • 尼才行不恒 - Phrase meaning 'the nun's conduct was not constant,' where '才行' means 'conduct' and '不恒' means 'not constant.'
    • 愠其敬待 - Phrase meaning 'resented his respectful treatment,' where '愠' means 'resented' and '敬待' means 'respectful treatment.'
    • 居之门内 - Phrase meaning 'lived in his inner quarters,' where '居' means 'to live' and '门内' means 'inner quarters.'
    • 尼每浴，必至移时 - Phrase meaning 'the nun always bathed for a long time,' where '每' means 'always' and '移时' means 'for a long time.'
    • 温疑而窥之 - Phrase meaning 'Huan Wen suspected and peeked,' where '疑' means 'suspected' and '窥' means 'to peek.'
    • 见尼裸身挥刀，破腹出脏，断截身首，支分脔切 - Phrase meaning 'saw the nun naked, swinging a knife, opening her belly, removing her organs, cutting off her head and body, slicing them into pieces,' where '裸身' means 'naked,' '挥刀' means 'swinging a knife,' '破腹' means 'opening her belly,' '出脏' means 'removing her organs,' '断截' means 'cutting off,' '身首' means 'head and body,' and '支分脔切' means 'slicing into pieces.'
    • 温怪骇而还 - Phrase meaning 'Huan Wen was startled and returned,' where '怪骇' means 'startled' and '还' means 'returned.'
    • 及至尼出浴室，身形如常 - Phrase meaning 'when the nun emerged from the bath, her body was as usual,' where '及至' means 'when' and '身形如常' means 'body as usual.'
    • 温以实问 - Phrase meaning 'Huan Wen asked her about the truth,' where '以实问' means 'asked about the truth.'
    • 尼答曰 - Phrase meaning 'the nun replied,' where '答曰' means 'replied.'
    • 若逐凌君上，形当如之 - Phrase meaning 'if I were to harm your lord, my body would be like this,' where '逐凌' means 'to harm' and '形当如之' means 'my body would be like this.'
    • 时温方谋问鼎 - Phrase meaning 'at that time, Huan Wen was plotting to seize power,' where '方谋' means 'plotting' and '问鼎' means 'to seize power.'
    • 闻之怅然 - Phrase meaning 'he heard this and was greatly disappointed,' where '闻之' means 'heard this' and '怅然' means 'greatly disappointed.'
    • 故以戒惧，终守臣节 - Phrase meaning 'therefore, he was warned and ultimately maintained his loyalty as a minister,' where '故以' means 'therefore' and '终守臣节' means 'ultimately maintained his loyalty as a minister.'
    • 尼后辞去，不知所在 - Phrase meaning 'the nun later left and her whereabouts are unknown,' where '辞去' means 'left' and '不知所在' means 'her whereabouts are unknown.'

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 武当山 - Refers to Mount Wudang, a famous Taoist mountain in China.
    • 左元放 - A historical figure known for his mystical abilities.
    • 曹公 - Refers to Cao Cao, a prominent warlord and statesman during the late Eastern Han dynasty.
    • 鲈鱼 - A type of fish, often associated with literary and cultural significance.
    • 秘术 - Refers to esoteric or mystical techniques.
    • 瓜刀 - A knife used for cutting melons.
    • 嘉兴 - A city in Zhejiang Province, China.
    • 太兴中 - Refers to the Tai Xing period, a specific era in Chinese history.
    • 衡阳 - A city in Hunan Province, China.
    • 区纯 - A historical figure known for his magical abilities.
    • 鼠市 - A magical device or mechanism involving mice.
    • 晋大司马桓温 - Refers to Huan Wen, a prominent military leader and politician during the Jin dynasty.
    • 比丘尼 - Refers to a nun, a female Buddhist monk.
    • 檀越 - Refers to a patron or benefactor.
    • 问鼎 - A metaphor for seizing power or authority.

    Xie Yun returned from Mount Wudang and was seated with Huan Xuanwu. Someone mentioned that Zuo Yuanfang had provided perch to Cao Cao, and Xie Yun remarked, "This is possible." He then asked for a large jar to hold water, wrote a red talisman, and threw it into the water. Soon, a carp began flapping its fins in the water.

    In Qiantang, there was a man named Du Zigong who possessed secret techniques. Once, he borrowed a melon knife from someone, and the owner asked for its return. Du Zigong said, "I will return it immediately." Later, the owner of the knife traveled to Jiaxing and found a fish jump into his boat. When he opened the fish's belly, he discovered the melon knife inside.

    During the Tai Xing period, Qu Chun of Hengyang created a mouse market: it was about four zhang square, with four doors, each having a wooden figure. He released four or five mice inside, and if they tried to leave, the wooden figures would push them back.

    Duke Huan Wen of Jin, courtesy name Yuanzi, in his later years, suddenly encountered a nun whose name was unknown, from a distant place. She offered herself to Huan Wen as a patron. The nun's conduct was not constant, and she resented his respectful treatment, living in his inner quarters. Whenever she bathed, she would do so for a long time. Huan Wen suspected and peeked. He saw the nun naked, swinging a knife, opening her belly, removing her organs, cutting off her head and body, slicing them into pieces. Huan Wen was startled and returned. When the nun emerged from the bath, her body was as usual. Huan Wen asked her about the truth, and the nun replied, "If I were to harm your lord, my body would be like this." At that time, Huan Wen was plotting to seize power, and hearing this, he was greatly disappointed. Therefore, he was warned and ultimately maintained his loyalty as a minister. The nun later left, and her whereabouts are unknown. [7]

    沛国有一士人，姓周，同生三子，年将弱冠，皆有声无言。忽有一客从门过，因乞饮，闻其儿声，问之曰：“此是何声”答曰：“是仆之子，皆不能言。”客曰：“君可还内省过，何以至此”主人异其言，知非常人。良久出云：“都不忆有罪过。”客曰：“试更思幼时事。”入内，食顷，出语客曰：“记小儿时，当床上有燕巢，中有三子，其母从外得食哺，三子皆出口受之，积曰如此。试以指内巢中，燕雏亦出口承受。因取三蔷茨，各与食之。既而皆死。母还，不见子，悲鸣而去。昔有此事，今实悔之。”客闻言，遂变为道人之容，曰：“君既自知悔，罪今除矣。”言讫，便闻其子言语。周亦忽不见此道人。 [8]

    A Man from Pei with Three Sons Who Could Make Sounds but Not Speak

    There was a scholar from the state of Pei, surnamed Zhou, who had three sons who could make sounds but not speak. Suddenly, a guest passed by the door and asked for a drink, hearing the children's sounds. He asked, "What is this sound?" The father replied, "These are my sons, all of whom cannot speak." The guest said, "You should reflect inwardly on your past and consider why this has happened." The host found the guest's words strange and knew he was no ordinary person. After a long time, the host said, "I do not remember any sins." The guest said, "Try to think about your childhood." The host went inside and, after a while, came out and said to the guest, "I remember when I was a child, there was a swallow's nest on the bed, with three chicks inside. The mother brought food from outside to feed them, and the chicks opened their mouths to receive it, happening repeatedly. I then put my finger into the nest, and the chicks also opened their mouths to receive it. I then took three thorns and gave them to each chick to eat. And they all died. The mother returned, did not see her chicks, and mourned and flew away. I remember this event, and I truly regret it now." The guest, upon hearing this, transformed into a Taoist's appearance and said, "Since you know you are sorry, your sins are now forgiven." After he finished speaking, the children could suddenly speak. Zhou also suddenly did not see the Taoist anymore. [8]

    天竺人佛图澄，永嘉四年来洛阳，善诵神咒，役使鬼神。腹傍有孔，常以絮塞之。每夜读书，则拔絮，孔中出光，照于一室。平旦，至流水侧，从孔中引出五脏六腑洗之，讫，还内腹中。
    　　石虎邺中有一胡道人，知咒术。乘驴作估客，于外国深山中行。下有绝涧，然无底。忽有恶鬼，偷牵此道人驴，下入绝涧。道人寻迹咒誓，呼诸鬼王。须臾，即驴、物如故。
    　　昙游道人，清苦沙门也。剡县有一家事蛊，人啖其食饮，无不吐血死。游尝诣之。主人下食，游依常咒愿。双蜈蚣，长尺余，便于盘中跳走。游便饱食而归，安然无他。
    　　高悝家有鬼怪，言词呵叱，投掷内外，不见人形。或器物自行再三发火。巫祝厌劾而不能绝。适值幸灵，乃要之。至门，见符索甚多，并取焚之。惟据轩小坐而去。其夕鬼怪即绝。 [9]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [9]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 天竺人 - A person from Tianzhu, an ancient Chinese name for India.
    • 永嘉四年 - The fourth year of Yongjia (310 AD), a specific year during the Western Jin dynasty.
    • 洛阳 - Luoyang, an important city in Chinese history, particularly during the Han and Jin dynasties.
    • 役使鬼神 - To command spirits and deities, indicating supernatural abilities.
    • 腹傍有孔，常以絮塞之 - He had a hole beside his abdomen, which he often plugged with cotton.
    • 平旦，至流水侧 - At dawn, he would go to the side of a flowing stream.
    • 从孔中引出五脏六腑洗之，讫，还内腹中 - He would pull out his five viscera and six bowels through the hole, wash them, and then put them back.
    • 石虎邺中有一胡道人 - In Ye, there was a foreign Taoist priest named Shi Hu.
    • 知咒术 - He knew spells and incantations, indicating magical knowledge.
    • 乘驴作估客 - He rode a donkey and acted as a merchant.
    • 绝涧 - A deep ravine, a geographical feature.
    • 恶鬼，偷牵此道人驴，下入绝涧 - A wicked ghost stole the priest's donkey and led it down into the deep ravine.
    • 寻迹咒誓，呼诸鬼王 - He followed the trail, recited spells, and summoned the kings of ghosts.
    • 昙游道人 - A Taoist priest named Tan You.
    • 清苦沙门 - A monk who lived a life of austerity.
    • 剡县 - Shanyang County, a place in Zhejiang province.
    • 事蛊 - To practice witchcraft, indicating a form of black magic.
    • 人啖其食饮，无不吐血死 - Anyone who ate his food would die by vomiting blood.
    • 游尝诣之 - Tan You once visited him.
    • 主人下食 - The host placed food on the table.
    • 游依常咒愿 - Tan You followed his usual ritual and made a wish.
    • 双蜈蚣，长尺余 - Two centipedes, each over a foot long.
    • 游便饱食而归，安然无他 - Tan You then ate his fill and returned home safely.
    • 高悝家有鬼怪 - In Gao Kui's house, there were ghosts and strange phenomena.
    • 言词呵叱，投掷内外，不见人形 - They spoke harshly, threw things around, and their forms were not visible.
    • 或器物自行再三发火 - Sometimes objects would move on their own and repeatedly catch fire.
    • 巫祝厌劾而不能绝 - Wizards and shamans tried to exorcise them but could not stop them.
    • 适值幸灵 - Just then, a fortunate spirit arrived.
    • 乃要之 - He invited it.
    • 至门，见符索甚多，并取焚之 - He went to the door and saw many talismans, which he all burned.
    • 惟据轩小坐而去 - He sat on a small platform and left.
    • 其夕鬼怪即绝 - That night, the ghosts and strange phenomena ceased.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 天竺 - An ancient Chinese name for India, indicating the origin of Fotu Cheng.
    • 永嘉四年 - The fourth year of Yongjia (310 AD), a specific year during the Western Jin dynasty.
    • 洛阳 - Luoyang, an important city in Chinese history, particularly during the Han and Jin dynasties.
    • 役使鬼神 - To command spirits and deities, indicating supernatural abilities, a common theme in Buddhist and Taoist traditions.
    • 腹傍有孔，常以絮塞之 - He had a hole beside his abdomen, which he often plugged with cotton, a unique physical characteristic of Fotu Cheng.
    • 平旦，至流水侧 - At dawn, he would go to the side of a flowing stream, a daily ritual.
    • 从孔中引出五脏六腑洗之，讫，还内腹中 - He would pull out his five viscera and six bowels through the hole, wash them, and then put them back, a supernatural ability.
    • 石虎 - Shi Hu, a prominent figure during the Later Zhao dynasty, known for his military and political power.
    • 邺中 - Ye, a city in Hebei province, the capital of the Later Zhao dynasty.
    • 胡道人 - A foreign Taoist priest, indicating a non-Chinese background.
    • 知咒术 - He knew spells and incantations, indicating magical knowledge.
    • 乘驴作估客 - He rode a donkey and acted as a merchant, describing his appearance and role.
    • 绝涧 - A deep ravine, a geographical feature.
    • 恶鬼 - A wicked ghost, a supernatural entity.
    • 寻迹咒誓，呼诸鬼王 - He followed the trail, recited spells, and summoned the kings of ghosts, a ritual to summon supernatural beings.
    • 昙游道人 - A Taoist priest named Tan You, a historical figure.
    • 清苦沙门 - A monk who lived a life of austerity, describing his lifestyle.
    • 剡县 - Shanyang County, a place in Zhejiang province.
    • 事蛊 - To practice witchcraft, indicating a form of black magic.
    • 人啖其食饮，无不吐血死 - Anyone who ate his food would die by vomiting blood, the effects of the witchcraft.
    • 游尝诣之 - Tan You once visited him, describing a visit.
    • 主人下食 - The host placed food on the table, describing a meal.
    • 游依常咒愿 - Tan You followed his usual ritual and made a wish, describing a ritual.
    • 双蜈蚣，长尺余 - Two centipedes, each over a foot long, a supernatural event.
    • 游便饱食而归，安然无他 - Tan You then ate his fill and returned home safely, the outcome.
    • 高悝家有鬼怪 - In Gao Kui's house, there were ghosts and strange phenomena, describing a haunted house.
    • 言词呵叱，投掷内外，不见人形 - They spoke harshly, threw things around, and their forms were not visible, the behavior of the ghosts.
    • 或器物自行再三发火 - Sometimes objects would move on their own and repeatedly catch fire, supernatural events.
    • 巫祝厌劾而不能绝 - Wizards and shamans tried to exorcise them but could not stop them, failed attempts to control the ghosts.
    • 适值幸灵 - Just then, a fortunate spirit arrived, the arrival of a supernatural being.
    • 乃要之 - He invited it, an action.
    • 至门，见符索甚多，并取焚之 - He went to the door and saw many talismans, which he all burned, a ritual.
    • 惟据轩小坐而去 - He sat on a small platform and left, the departure of the spirit.
    • 其夕鬼怪即绝 - That night, the ghosts and strange phenomena ceased, the outcome.

    A Person from Tianzhu Named Fotu Cheng

    In the fourth year of Yongjia (310 AD), a person from Tianzhu named Fotu Cheng came to Luoyang. He was skilled in reciting divine incantations and could command spirits and deities. He had a hole beside his abdomen, which he often plugged with cotton. Every night, when he read books, he would remove the cotton, and light would shine out from the hole, illuminating the entire room. At dawn, he would go to the side of a flowing stream, pull out his five viscera and six bowels through the hole, wash them, and then put them back inside his abdomen.

    In Ye, there was a foreign Taoist priest named Shi Hu, who knew spells and incantations. He rode a donkey and acted as a merchant, traveling through the deep mountains of a foreign land. Below was a deep ravine with no bottom. Suddenly, a wicked ghost stole the priest's donkey and led it down into the deep ravine. The priest followed the trail, recited spells, and summoned the kings of ghosts. In a short while, the donkey and belongings were restored to their original state.

    Tan You, a Taoist priest, lived a life of austerity as a monk. In Shanyang County, there was a family that practiced witchcraft. Anyone who ate their food would die by vomiting blood. Tan You once visited them. The host placed food on the table, and Tan You followed his usual ritual and made a wish. Two centipedes, each over a foot long, appeared and jumped around on the table. Tan You then ate his fill and returned home safely, without any harm.

    In Gao Kui's house, there were ghosts and strange phenomena. They spoke harshly, threw things around, and their forms were not visible. Sometimes objects would move on their own and repeatedly catch fire. Wizards and shamans tried to exorcise them but could not stop them. Just then, a fortunate spirit arrived, and he invited it. He went to the door and saw many talismans, which he all burned. He sat on a small platform and left. That night, the ghosts and strange phenomena ceased. [9]

    赵固常乘一匹赤马以战征，甚所爱重。常系所住斋前，忽腹胀，少时死。郭璞从北过，因往诣之。门吏云：“将军好马，甚爱惜。今死，甚懊惋。”璞便语门吏云：“可入通，道吾能活此马，则必见我。”门吏闻之惊喜，即启固。固踊跃，令门吏走往迎之。始交寒温，便问：“卿能活我马乎”璞曰：“我可活尔。”固欣喜，即问：“须何方术”璞云：“得卿同心健儿二三十人，皆令持竹竿，于此东行三十里，当有邱陵林树，状若社庙。有此者，便当以竹竿搅扰打拍之。当得一物，便急持归。既得此物，马便活矣。”于是左右骁勇之士五十人使去。果如璞言，得大丛林，有一物似猴而飞走。众勇共逐得，便抱持归。此物遥见死马，便跳梁欲往。璞令放之。此物便自走往马头间，嘘吸其鼻。良久，马起，喷奋奔迅，便不见此物。固厚赀给，璞得过江左。 [10]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [10]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 甚所愛重 - Means 'greatly loved and valued,' expressing Zhao Gu's deep affection for the horse.
    • 卿能活我馬乎 - A direct question meaning 'Can you bring my horse back to life?' reflecting Zhao Gu's hope and desperation.
    • 良久，馬起，噴奮奔迅 - Describes the horse standing up, snorting, and running quickly after its recovery, showing the miraculous outcome.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 趙固 - Zhao Gu, a historical figure known for his military prowess and leadership.
    • 郭璞 - Guo Pu, a famous scholar and alchemist of the Jin dynasty, known for his expertise in divination and magic.
    • 赤馬 - A red horse, often symbolizing bravery and nobility in Chinese culture.
    • 社廟 - A local temple or shrine, often associated with community and religious practices.
    • 江左 - The area east of the Yangtze River, historically significant in Chinese politics and culture.

    Proper Nouns

    • 趙固 - Historical figure.
    • 郭璞 - Famous scholar and alchemist.
    • 赤馬 - Red horse.
    • 社廟 - Local temple or shrine.
    • 江左 - Area east of the Yangtze River.

    Zhao Gu often rode a red horse in battle, greatly loving and valuing it. He usually tethered the horse in front of his residence, but one day the horse suddenly bloated and died shortly after. Guo Pu passed by from the north and went to visit Zhao Gu. The gatekeeper said, "General Zhao's horse was very well cared for. Now that it has died, he is very upset." Guo Pu then told the gatekeeper, "Tell Zhao Gu that I can bring the horse back to life, and he will certainly see me." The gatekeeper, hearing this, was surprised and delighted, and immediately informed Zhao Gu. Zhao Gu was overjoyed and ordered the gatekeeper to go fetch Guo Pu. When they first met and exchanged pleasantries, Zhao Gu asked, "Can you bring my horse back to life?" Guo Pu replied, "I can bring it back to life." Zhao Gu was delighted and asked, "What kind of method do you need?" Guo Pu said, "Get about twenty or thirty of your loyal and strong men, all to hold bamboo poles, and travel east for thirty miles from here. There will be a hill, forest, and trees, resembling a temple. If you find such a place, use the bamboo poles to stir and hit it. You will find an object, and you should quickly bring it back. Once you get this object, the horse will come back to life." Zhao Gu then sent fifty brave and valiant men to go. Just as Guo Pu had said, they found a large grove and saw an object that looked like a monkey and could fly and run. The brave men chased it down and brought it back. This object, seeing the dead horse from afar, jumped and wanted to go there. Guo Pu ordered to let it go. This object then went to the horse's head and breathed on its nose. After a long time, the horse stood up, snorted, and ran quickly, and then the object disappeared. Zhao Gu generously rewarded Guo Pu, who then crossed the river to the east. [10]

    王文献曾令郭璞筮己一年吉凶，璞曰：“当有小不吉利，可取广州二大罂，盛水，置床张二角，名曰‘镜好’，以厌之。至某时，撤罂去水。如此其灾可消。”至曰忘之。寻失铜镜，不知所在。后撤去水，乃见所失镜在于罂中。罂口数寸，镜大尺余。王公复令璞筮镜罂之意。璞云：“撤罂违期，故致此妖。邪魅所为，无他故也。”使烧车辖，而镜立出。
    　　中兴初，郭璞每自为卦，知其凶终。尝行经建康栅塘，逢一趋步少年，甚寒，便牵住，脱丝布袍与之。其人辞不受，璞曰：“但取，后自当知。”其人受而去。及当死，果此人行刑。旁人皆为求属，璞曰：“我托之久矣。”此人为之欷哽咽。行刑既毕，此人乃说。 [11]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [11]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 令郭璞筮己一年吉凶 - Guo Pu divined the auspiciousness or inauspiciousness of his own year.
    • 使烧车辖，而镜立出 - Burn the vehicle's axle, and the mirror immediately appeared.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 郭璞 - A famous Chinese scholar, geographer, and diviner of the Jin Dynasty.
    • 建康栅塘 - A place name, possibly referring to a location in Jiankang (modern-day Nanjing).
    • 中兴初 - Refers to the early period of the Eastern Han Dynasty, also known as the 'Restoration' period.

    Proper Nouns

    • 王文献 - A historical figure, likely a specific individual or a title.
    • 广州 - The place name Guangzhou.
    • 建康栅塘 - A place name, possibly referring to a location in Jiankang (modern-day Nanjing).

    Wang Xianmin once ordered Guo Pu to divinate the auspiciousness or inauspiciousness of his own year. Guo Pu said, "There will be a small misfortune. You can take two large jars from Guangzhou, fill them with water, place them on the bed with two corners, name them 'Mirror Good,' and ward off it. At a certain time, remove the jars and the water." Wang Xianmin then found a lost copper mirror, but did not know its location. Later, when he removed the water, he saw the lost mirror inside the jar. The mouth of the jar was several inches, and the mirror was more than a foot in size. He then ordered Guo Pu to divinate the meaning of the mirror and the jar. Guo Pu said, "Removing the jars was delayed, thus causing this misfortune. It was caused by evil spirits, no other reason." He had the vehicle's axle burned, and the mirror immediately appeared.

    In the early period of the Eastern Han Dynasty, Guo Pu often divined for himself and knew his fate would be disastrous. Once passing by Jiankang Gate and Pond, he met a young man who hurriedly walked, very cold. He pulled him, took off his silk robe, and gave it to him. The man refused to take it, and Guo Pu said, "Just take it, you will know later." When he was about to die, it was indeed this person who executed him. Others all tried to plead for him, and Guo Pu said, "I have entrusted him for a long time." This person was choked with sobs. After the execution was completed, this person then explained. [11]

    高平郗超，字嘉宾，年二十余，得重病。庐江杜不愆，少就外祖郭璞学易卜，颇有经验。超令试占之。卦成，不愆曰：“案卦言之，卿所恙寻愈。然宜于东北三十里上官姓家，索其所养雄雉，笼而绊之，置东檐下，却后九曰景午曰午时，必当有野雌雉飞来，与交合。既毕，双飞去。若如此，不出二十曰，病都除。又是休应，年将八十，位极人臣。若但雌逝雄留者，病一周方差。年半八十，名位亦失。”超时正羸笃，虑命在旦夕，笑而答曰：“若保八十之半，便有余矣。一周病差，何足为淹。”然未之信。或劝依其言索雄，果得。至景午曰，超卧南轩之下观之。至曰晏，果有雄雉飞入笼，与雄雉交而去。雄雉不动。超叹息曰：“管、郭之奇，何以尚此！”超病逾年乃起，至四十，卒于中书郎。 [12]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [12]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 案卦言之 - According to the hexagram.
    • 若保八十之半 - If you can live to half of eighty.
    • 何以尚此 - How can they surpass this?

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 高平 - Place name in ancient China, modern-day Shanxi Province.
    • 庐江 - Place name in ancient China, modern-day Anhui Province.
    • 郭璞 - Famous scholar and diviner of the Western Jin Dynasty.
    • 易卜 - Refers to the *I Ching* (Book of Changes) and divination practices.
    • 景午曰午时 - Refers to the traditional Chinese date and time system, specifically the day of the double fifth and the midday hour.
    • 中书郎 - An official position in the imperial bureaucracy, responsible for drafting and managing official documents.

    Proper Nouns

    • 高平 - Place name.
    • 庐江 - Place name.
    • 郭璞 - Scholar and diviner.
    • 中书郎 - Official position.

    Xi Chao, a man from Gaoping, was in his twenties and suffered from a serious illness. Du Buqian, a man from Lüjiang, had learned the I Ching and divination from his maternal grandfather, Guo Pu, and had considerable experience. Chao ordered him to try divination. When the hexagram was cast, Buqian said, "According to the hexagram, your illness will soon be cured. However, you should go to a family named Shanggong, thirty li northeast, and look for the male pheasant they are raising. Put it in a cage and tie it up, and place it under the eastern eaves. Nine days later, on the day of the double fifth at midday, there will definitely be a wild female pheasant flying over. It will mate with it. After mating, they will fly away together. If this happens, your illness will be completely cured within twenty days. This is also a good omen. You will live to be eighty years old and reach the highest rank of a minister. If only the female flies away and the male stays, your illness will take a full year to recover. You will live to be half of eighty years old and lose your position and reputation."

    At the time, Chao was extremely weak and feared his life was in danger. He laughed and replied, "If I can live to half of eighty, that would be enough. A full year of illness, what is worth waiting for?" However, he did not believe it. Someone advised him to follow the advice and look for the male pheasant, and he indeed found it. On the day of the double fifth, Chao lay under the southern veranda to watch. Until dusk, there was indeed a male pheasant that flew into the cage and mated with the male pheasant before flying away. The male pheasant did not move. Chao sighed and said, "The wonders of Guan and Guo, how can they surpass this!" His illness lasted more than a year before he recovered, and he lived to be forty years old, dying as a Lang of the Central Secretariat. [12]

    ●卷三
    　　程咸字咸休。其母始怀咸，梦老公投药与之：“服此当生贵子。”晋武帝时，历位至侍中，有名于世。
    　　袁真在豫州，遣女妓纪陵送阿薛、阿郭、阿马三妓与桓宣武。既至经时，三人半夜共出庭前月下观望，有铜瓮水在其侧。忽见一流星，夜从天直堕瓮中。惊喜共视，忽如二寸火珠，流于水底，炯然明净，乃相谓曰：“此吉祥也，当谁应之。”于是薛郭二人更以瓢杓接取，并不得。阿马最后取，星正入瓢中，便饮之。既而若有感焉，俄而怀桓玄。玄虽篡位不终，而数年之中，荣贵极矣。
    　　临淮公荀序，字休玄。母华夫人，怜爱过常。年十岁，从南临归，经青草湖，时正帆风驶，序出塞郭，忽落水。比得下帆，已行数十里，洪波淼漫，母抚膺远望。少顷，见一掘头船，渔父以楫棹船如飞，载序还之，云：“送府君还。”荀后位至常伯、长沙相，故云府君也。 [13]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [13]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 卷三 - Refers to the third volume or chapter of a text, indicating the location of the content.
    • 程咸字咸休 - Introduces a person by name and courtesy name, a common practice in Classical Chinese biographical writing.
    • 其母始怀咸 - Describes the beginning of a pregnancy in a straightforward manner.
    • 梦老公投药与之 - A dream narrative, where a '老公' (old man) gives medicine, symbolizing a blessing or prophecy.
    • 服此当生贵子 - A prophecy within the dream, indicating that the child will be of high status.
    • 历位至侍中 - Indicates a career progression, reaching the position of '侍中,' an important official role.
    • 有名于世 - Describes fame or reputation, indicating the person's recognition in the world.
    • 袁真在豫州 - Introduces a person by name and location, indicating his place of residence or authority.
    • 遣女妓纪陵送阿薛、阿郭、阿马三妓与桓宣武 - Describes a formal act of sending women, with '女妓' and '妓' referring to female entertainers or courtesans.
    • 既至经时 - Indicates the passage of time after the women arrived.
    • 三人半夜共出庭前月下观望 - Describes a scene of three women observing the moon at night, a common motif in Classical Chinese literature.
    • 忽见一流星 - Describes a sudden and unexpected event, a falling star.
    • 夜从天直堕瓮中 - Describes the trajectory of the star, falling directly into a water container.
    • 惊喜共视 - Describes a shared reaction of surprise and joy.
    • 忽如二寸火珠 - Describes the appearance of the star as a small, glowing object.
    • 炯然明净 - Describes the star's brightness and clarity.
    • 此吉祥也，当谁应之 - A statement of good fortune and a question about who it will benefit.
    • 于是薛郭二人更以瓢杓接取 - Describes an attempt by two of the women to catch the star using a ladle or bowl.
    • 并不得 - Indicates that the attempt was unsuccessful.
    • 阿马最后取，星正入瓢中 - Describes the successful capture of the star by the third woman.
    • 便饮之 - Describes the act of drinking the star, a symbolic act of consuming the good fortune.
    • 既而若有感焉 - Describes a sense of something happening or a feeling of fulfillment.
    • 俄而怀桓玄 - Describes the pregnancy of the woman who drank the star, leading to the birth of '桓玄.'
    • 玄虽篡位不终 - Describes the eventual fate of '桓玄,' who seized power but did not maintain it.
    • 而数年之中，荣贵极矣 - Describes the period of great honor and wealth during '桓玄's' reign.
    • 临淮公荀序，字休玄 - Introduces another person by name and courtesy name.
    • 母华夫人，怜爱过常 - Describes the mother's excessive love for her son.
    • 年十岁，从南临归 - Describes the son's age and a journey he took.
    • 经青草湖 - Describes a specific location, '青草湖,' where the event takes place.
    • 时正帆风驶 - Describes the wind conditions during the journey.
    • 序出塞郭，忽落水 - Describes the son's accidental fall into the water.
    • 比得下帆，已行数十里 - Describes the distance traveled before the son was noticed missing.
    • 洪波淼漫 - Describes the vast and turbulent waters.
    • 母抚膺远望 - Describes the mother's emotional reaction, touching her chest and looking far away.
    • 少顷，见一掘头船 - Describes the appearance of a boat, '掘头船,' which is a type of small, narrow boat.
    • 渔父以楫棹船如飞 - Describes the swift movement of the boat, propelled by a fisherman using a paddle.
    • 载序还之 - Describes the fisherman's act of bringing the son back to safety.
    • 云：'送府君还。' - Describes the fisherman's statement, indicating that he is returning the son to his '府君' (lord or master).
    • 荀后位至常伯、长沙相 - Describes the son's later career, reaching the positions of '常伯' (a high-ranking official) and '长沙相' (governor of Changsha).

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 程咸 - A historical figure, likely a prominent official or scholar.
    • 袁真 - A historical figure, possibly a regional governor or military leader.
    • 桓宣武 - A historical figure, likely a high-ranking official or military leader.
    • 桓玄 - A historical figure, known for seizing power but failing to maintain it.
    • 临淮公荀序 - A historical figure, likely a prominent official or scholar.
    • 青草湖 - A specific location, possibly a well-known lake or body of water in the region.
    • 府君 - A title of respect, often used for high-ranking officials or lords.

    Proper Nouns

    • 程咸 - A historical figure.
    • 咸休 - The courtesy name of '程咸'.
    • 晋武帝 - Emperor Wu of Jin, a ruler of the Jin Dynasty.
    • 侍中 - An important official position.
    • 袁真 - A historical figure.
    • 豫州 - A region or administrative division.
    • 桓宣武 - A historical figure.
    • 桓玄 - A historical figure.
    • 临淮公荀序 - A historical figure.
    • 休玄 - The courtesy name of '荀序'.
    • 华夫人 - The mother of '荀序'.
    • 青草湖 - A specific location.
    • 府君 - A title of respect.
    • 常伯 - An important official position.
    • 长沙相 - The governor of Changsha.

    Translation of the Source Text

    Chapter Three

    Cheng Xian, courtesy name Xianxiu. His mother was pregnant with him when she dreamed of an old man giving her medicine and said, "Take this, and you will bear a noble son." During the reign of Emperor Wu of Jin, Cheng Xian rose through the ranks to become a Shizhong (Attendant-in-Waiting) and gained fame in the world.

    Yuan Zhen was in Yu Prefecture. He sent a female entertainer named Jiling to deliver three courtesans—Xue, Guo, and Ma—to Huan Xuanwu. After they arrived, after some time, the three women came out at midnight to gaze at the moon in the courtyard. There was a bronze jar of water beside them. Suddenly, they saw a shooting star fall directly into the jar from the sky. They were surprised and looked at it together. Suddenly, it appeared as a small, glowing pearl, flowing to the bottom of the water, shining brightly and clearly. They then said to each other, "This is a sign of good fortune. Who will it benefit?" Xue and Guo tried to catch it with a ladle and a bowl, but failed. Ma was the last to try, and the star fell right into her ladle. She drank it immediately. Soon after, she felt something and soon became pregnant with Huan Xuan. Although Huan Xuan eventually seized power but did not maintain it, he enjoyed great honor and wealth for several years.

    Linhuai Gong Xun Xu, courtesy name Xiuxuan. His mother, Lady Hua, loved him excessively. When he was ten years old, he traveled south with his family and passed through Qingcao Lake. At the time, the wind was strong and the sails were full. Xu suddenly fell into the water while leaving the city gate. By the time they lowered the sails, they had already traveled dozens of miles. The waters were vast and turbulent, and his mother touched her chest and looked far away. After a short while, she saw a small boat with a fisherman rowing it like a bird. The fisherman brought Xu back to safety and said, "I am returning the young master to you." Later, Xun Xu rose to the positions of Changbo (a high-ranking official) and Governor of Changsha, hence the term "young master" (府君). [13]

    庐陵巴邱人文晁者，世以田作为业。年常田数十顷，家渐富。晋太元初，秋收已过，刈获都毕，明旦至田，禾悉复满，湛然如初。即便更获，所获盈仓。于此遂为巨富。
    　　上虞魏全，家在县北。忽有一人，著孝子服，皂笠，手巾掩口，来诣全家，诣曰：“君有钱一千万，铜器亦如之，大柳树钱在其下，取钱当得尔。于君家大不吉。仆寻为君取此。”便去。自尔出三十年，遂不复来。全家亦不取钱。
    　　元嘉元年，建安郡山贼百余人破郡治，抄掠百姓资产、子女，遂入佛图，搜掠财宝。先是，诸供养具别封置一室。贼破户，忽有蜜蜂数万头，从衣簏出，同时噬螫。群贼身首肿痛，眼皆盲合，先诸所掠，皆弃而走。 [14]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [14]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 世以...为业 - Commonly used to indicate a long-standing profession.
    • 年常 - Fixed expression meaning 'annually' or 'regularly'.
    • 渐富 - Common expression indicating a gradual increase in wealth.
    • 明旦 - Means 'the next morning'.
    • 悉复 - Means 'fully restored'.
    • 湛然 - Literary term meaning 'clear and unchanged'.
    • 即便 - Means 'immediately'.
    • 盈仓 - Means 'filled the granary'.
    • 遂为 - Means 'thus became'.
    • 忽有 - Means 'suddenly appeared'.
    • 著孝子服 - Refers to traditional mourning attire.
    • 皂笠 - Means 'a black hat'.
    • 手巾 - Means 'handkerchief'.
    • 诣 - Means 'to come to' or 'to visit'.
    • 君 - Respectful form of address.
    • 当得 - Means 'will get'.
    • 大不吉 - Means 'very unlucky'.
    • 仆 - Humble self-reference.
    • 寻 - Means 'soon'.
    • 自尔 - Means 'since then'.
    • 抄掠 - Means 'raided'.
    • 佛图 - Old term for a Buddhist temple.
    • 供养具 - Offerings made to deities or ancestors in religious contexts.
    • 破户 - Means 'broke into the house'.
    • 噬螫 - Means 'bit and stung'.
    • 身首 - Means 'bodies and heads'.
    • 盲合 - Means 'became blind and closed'.
    • 先诸 - Means 'all the things they had plundered'.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 庐陵 - Historical region in present-day Jiangxi Province.
    • 巴邱 - Place name within the historical region of Luzhou.
    • 晋太元初 - The Taiyuan era (376-396) of the Eastern Jin dynasty.
    • 上虞 - Historical county in present-day Zhejiang Province.
    • 元嘉元年 - The first year (424) of the Yuangang era of the Liu Song dynasty.
    • 建安郡 - Historical county in present-day Fujian Province.

    Proper Nouns

    • 庐陵巴邱人文晁者 - Descriptive phrase meaning 'the person named Wen Chao from Baqiu in Luzhou'.
    • 魏全 - Refers to 'Wei Quan from Shangyu'.
    • 孝子服 - Traditional mourning attire worn during funerals.
    • 佛图 - Old term for a Buddhist temple.
    • 供养具 - Offerings made to deities or ancestors in religious contexts.

    Wen Chao of Baqiu in Luzhou, through generations, made farming his occupation. Annually, he cultivated dozens of hectares of land, and the family gradually became wealthy. At the beginning of the Taiyuan era of the Jin dynasty, after the autumn harvest, all the harvesting was completed. The next morning, he went to the fields, and the rice was fully restored, as clear and unchanged as before. Immediately, he harvested again, and the harvest filled the granary. Thus, he became very wealthy.

    Wei Quan of Shangyu lived in the northern part of the county. Suddenly, there was a person wearing mourning clothes, a black hat, and a handkerchief covering his mouth, who came to the entire family. He said, "You have one million coins, and copper objects are also like this. There is a large willow tree with money underneath it. If you take the money, you will get it. It is very unlucky for your family. I will soon take this for you." Then he left. Since then, for thirty years, he did not come back. The whole family also did not take the money.

    In the first year of the Yuangang era of the Liu Song dynasty, more than a hundred bandits from Jian'an County attacked and destroyed the county seat, raided the people's property and children, and then entered the Buddhist temple, searching for and looting treasures. Before that, all the offerings were separately sealed and placed in a room. When the bandits broke into the house, suddenly, there were tens of thousands of bees that came out of the clothes chest, bit and stung simultaneously. The bandits' bodies and heads swelled and hurt, and their eyes all became blind and closed. They abandoned all the things they had plundered and fled. [14]

    蔡裔有勇气，声若雷震。尝有二偷儿入室，裔拊床一呼，二盗俱陨。
    　　昔有一人，与奴同时得腹瘕病，治不能愈。奴既死，乃剖腹视之，得一白鳖，赤眼，甚鲜明。乃试以诸毒药浇灌之，并内药于鳖口，悉无损动，乃系鳖于床脚。忽有一客来看之，乘一白马。既而马尿溅鳖，鳖乃惶骇，欲疾走避尿，因系之不得去，乃缩藏头颈足焉。病者察之，谓其子曰：“吾病或可以救矣。”乃试取白马尿以灌鳖上，须臾便消成数升水。病者乃顿服升余白马尿，病豁然愈。
    　　太尉郗鉴，字道徽，镇丹徒。曾出猎，时二月中，蕨始生。有一甲士，折食一茎，即觉心中淡淡欲吐。因归，乃成心腹疼痛。经半年许，忽大吐，吐出一赤蛇，长尺余，尚活动摇。乃挂著屋檐前，汁稍稍出，蛇渐焦小。经一宿视之，乃是一茎蕨，犹昔之所食。病遂除差。 [15]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [15]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 蔡裔有勇气，声若雷震 - Descriptive phrase meaning 'Cai Yi was courageous, his voice as loud as thunder.'
    • 拊床一呼 - Means 'to clap the bed and shout once,' a dramatic action indicating sudden, loud noise.
    • 二盗俱陨 - Means 'the two thieves both fell down,' likely indicating they were frightened or incapacitated.
    • 昔有一人，与奴同时得腹瘕病 - A narrative opening, meaning 'Once there was a person who, along with his servant, contracted a disease called 腹瘕 (abdominal tumor).'
    • 奴既死，乃剖腹视之 - Means 'After the servant died, he then cut open the abdomen to examine it.'
    • 赤眼，甚鲜明 - Describes the white turtle as having 'red eyes, very bright and vivid.'
    • 试以诸毒药浇灌之 - Means 'he tried to pour various toxic medicines over it,' testing the turtle's resilience.
    • 悉无损动 - Means 'it showed no damage or reaction,' indicating the turtle's resistance to the poisons.
    • 系鳖于床脚 - Means 'he tied the turtle to the bedpost,' a practical action to restrain it.
    • 忽有一客来看之，乘一白马 - Means 'Suddenly, a guest came to visit, riding a white horse,' introducing an unexpected event.
    • 马尿溅鳖 - Means 'the horse's urine splashed on the turtle,' a key moment in the story.
    • 惶骇，欲疾走避尿 - Means 'the turtle became frightened, wanting to quickly run away to avoid the urine,' describing the turtle's reaction.
    • 乃缩藏头颈足焉 - Means 'it then retracted its head, neck, and feet,' a natural defensive response.
    • 病者察之，谓其子曰 - Means 'the patient observed this and said to his son,' indicating a moment of realization.
    • 吾病或可以救矣 - Means 'My illness might be curable,' expressing hope.
    • 乃试取白马尿以灌鳖上 - Means 'he then tried to pour the white horse's urine over the turtle,' testing the idea.
    • 须臾便消成数升水 - Means 'in a short while, it dissolved into several liters of water,' describing the miraculous transformation.
    • 病者乃顿服升余白马尿 - Means 'the patient then drank several liters of the white horse's urine,' a drastic measure.
    • 病豁然愈 - Means 'the illness suddenly healed,' indicating a complete recovery.
    • 太尉郗鉴，字道徽，镇丹徒 - Introduces the historical figure 'Xishi (Xie Jian), a high-ranking official during the Jin Dynasty, stationed in Dantu.'
    • 曾出猎，时二月中，蕨始生 - Means 'Once, while hunting, it was early February when ferns began to grow,' setting the scene.
    • 有一甲士，折食一茎 - Means 'A soldier broke off and ate one stem,' a seemingly harmless action.
    • 即觉心中淡淡欲吐 - Means 'immediately felt a slight nausea in his heart, wanting to vomit,' describing the onset of illness.
    • 因归，乃成心腹疼痛 - Means 'he returned home, and then developed abdominal pain,' indicating the progression of the illness.
    • 经半年许，忽大吐 - Means 'After about half a year, he suddenly vomited,' a turning point in the story.
    • 吐出一赤蛇，长尺余，尚活动摇 - Means 'he vomited out a red snake, over a foot long, still moving,' a shocking revelation.
    • 乃挂著屋檐前，汁稍稍出，蛇渐焦小 - Means 'he hung it on the eaves, and the snake gradually dried and shrank,' describing the snake's transformation.
    • 经一宿视之，乃是一茎蕨，犹昔之所食 - Means 'After one night, it was just a stem of fern, the same one he had eaten,' revealing the truth.
    • 病遂除差 - Means 'the illness was then cured,' concluding the story.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 蔡裔 - A historical figure, likely a warrior or official during the Three Kingdoms period.
    • 腹瘕 - A traditional Chinese medical term for an abdominal tumor or mass.
    • 太尉郗鉴 - A high-ranking official during the Jin Dynasty, known for his military and administrative roles.
    • 丹徒 - A place name, likely referring to a city or region in Jiangsu Province.
    • 蕨 - A type of fern, commonly eaten in traditional Chinese cuisine.

    Syntactic Ambiguities

    • 蔡裔有勇气，声若雷震 - The phrase is straightforward but emphasizes P'ai Yi's courage and the dramatic nature of his voice.
    • 拊床一呼，二盗俱陨 - The action is vivid but requires careful translation to maintain the dramatic impact.
    • 奴既死，乃剖腹视之 - The sequence of events is clear but must be translated to preserve the narrative flow.
    • 赤眼，甚鲜明 - The description of the turtle is vivid and requires careful rendering to maintain the imagery.
    • 试以诸毒药浇灌之，悉无损动 - The testing of the turtle's resilience is a key moment that must be translated clearly.
    • 系鳖于床脚 - The practical action of tying the turtle must be translated to maintain the narrative clarity.
    • 忽有一客来看之，乘一白马 - The introduction of the guest is a turning point that must be translated to maintain the story's momentum.
    • 马尿溅鳖，惶骇，欲疾走避尿 - The turtle's reaction to the urine is a key moment that must be translated to maintain the drama.
    • 乃缩藏头颈足焉 - The turtle's defensive action is a key moment that must be translated to maintain the narrative clarity.
    • 病者察之，谓其子曰 - The patient's realization and subsequent action are key moments that must be translated to maintain the narrative flow.
    • 乃试取白马尿以灌鳖上，须臾便消成数升水 - The miraculous transformation is a key moment that must be translated to maintain the narrative clarity.
    • 病者乃顿服升余白马尿，病豁然愈 - The drastic measure and subsequent recovery are key moments that must be translated to maintain the narrative flow.
    • 太尉郗鉴，字道徽，镇丹徒 - The introduction of the historical figure is a key moment that must be translated to maintain the narrative clarity.
    • 曾出猎，时二月中，蕨始生 - The setting of the scene is a key moment that must be translated to maintain the narrative clarity.
    • 有一甲士，折食一茎 - The soldier's action is a key moment that must be translated to maintain the narrative flow.
    • 即觉心中淡淡欲吐，因归，乃成心腹疼痛 - The progression of the illness is a key moment that must be translated to maintain the narrative clarity.
    • 经半年许，忽大吐，吐出一赤蛇，长尺余，尚活动摇 - The shocking revelation is a key moment that must be translated to maintain the narrative clarity.
    • 乃挂著屋檐前，汁稍稍出，蛇渐焦小 - The snake's transformation is a key moment that must be translated to maintain the narrative clarity.
    • 经一宿视之，乃是一茎蕨，犹昔之所食 - The revelation of the truth is a key moment that must be translated to maintain the narrative clarity.
    • 病遂除差 - The conclusion of the story is a key moment that must be translated to maintain the narrative clarity.

    Proper Nouns

    • 蔡裔 - A historical figure, likely a warrior or official during the Three Kingdoms period.
    • 腹瘕 - A traditional Chinese medical term for an abdominal tumor or mass.
    • 太尉郗鉴 - A high-ranking official during the Jin Dynasty, known for his military and administrative roles.
    • 丹徒 - A place name, likely referring to a city or region in Jiangsu Province.
    • 蕨 - A type of fern, commonly eaten in traditional Chinese cuisine.

    Cai Yi was courageous, his voice as loud as thunder. Once, two thieves entered his room, and Cai Yi clapped the bed and shouted once, causing both thieves to fall down.

    Once there was a man who, along with his servant, contracted a disease called 腹瘕 (abdominal tumor). The servant died, and the man then cut open his abdomen to examine it. He found a white turtle with red eyes, very bright and vivid. He tried to pour various toxic medicines over it, and even put medicine in the turtle's mouth, but it showed no damage or reaction. He then tied the turtle to the bedpost. Suddenly, a guest came to visit, riding a white horse. The horse's urine splashed on the turtle, which became frightened, wanting to quickly run away to avoid the urine. Being tied, it could not move, so it retracted its head, neck, and feet. The patient observed this and said to his son, "My illness might be curable." He then tried to pour the white horse's urine over the turtle, and in a short while, it dissolved into several liters of water. The patient then drank several liters of the white horse's urine, and the illness suddenly healed.

    High-ranking official Xishi (Xie Jian), stationed in Dantu, once went hunting in early February when ferns began to grow. A soldier broke off and ate one stem, immediately feeling a slight nausea in his heart, wanting to vomit. He returned home, and then developed abdominal pain. After about half a year, he suddenly vomited, expelling a red snake over a foot long, still moving. He hung it on the eaves, and the snake gradually dried and shrank. After one night, it was just a stem of fern, the same one he had eaten. The illness was then cured. [15]

    桓宣武时，有一督将，因时行病后虚热，更能饮复茗，必一斛二斗乃饱。才减升合，便以为不足。非复一曰。家贫。后有客造之，正遇其饮复茗，亦先闻世有此病，仍令更进五升，乃大吐，有一物出，如升大，有口，形质缩绉，状如牛肚⊥乃令置之于盆中，以一斛二斗复茗浇之。此物歙之都尽，而止觉小胀。又加五升，便悉混然从口中涌出。既吐此物，其病遂差。或问之：“此何病”答云：“此病名斛二瘕。”
    　　桓哲字明期，居豫章时，梅元龙为太守，先已病矣，哲往省之。语梅云：“吾昨夜忽梦见作卒，迎卿来作泰山府君。”梅闻之愕然，曰：“吾亦梦见卿为卒，著丧衣，来迎我。”经数曰。复同梦如前，云“二十八曰当拜”。至二十七曰晡时，桓忽中恶腹满，就梅索麝香丸。梅闻，便令作凶具。二十七曰，桓便亡；二十八曰而梅卒。 [16]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [16]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 督將 - Refers to a military officer or commander.
    • 因時行病後虛熱 - A medical term describing a condition of lingering fever after an illness.
    • 更能飲復茗 - Means 'can drink even more tea,' where '復茗' refers to tea.
    • 必一斛二斗乃飽 - Means 'must drink one hu and two dou to be full,' where '一斛二斗' is a large volume of liquid.
    • 才減升合，便以為不足 - Means 'if it's reduced even slightly, they feel it's insufficient,' where '升合' refers to smaller units of liquid.
    • 家貧 - Means 'poor family.'
    • 造之 - Means 'visit' or 'pay a visit to.'
    • 聞世有此病 - Means 'heard in the world that there is such a disease.'
    • 令更進五升 - Means 'ordered to drink five sheng more.'
    • 大吐 - Means 'vomited heavily.'
    • 如升大 - Means 'about the size of a sheng.'
    • 有口 - Means 'with a mouth.'
    • 形質縮绉 - Means 'shrunken and wrinkled in form.'
    • 狀如牛肚 - Means 'looks like a cow's stomach.'
