    • 但闻筝笛弦节之声及香气氤氲 - '但闻' means 'only heard,' and '香气氤氲' describes a pervasive scent, likely from the ship.
    • 驱遣 - Means 'to drive away' or 'to send away,' used here in a dream context.
    • 尊府君为索小女婚 - '尊府君' is a respectful term for one's father, and '索小女婚' means 'to seek a marriage for his daughter.'
    • 临水戏 - '临水' means 'by the water,' and '戏' means 'to play,' indicating a leisurely activity.
    • 情意如初 - Means 'feelings and intentions remain as they were,' indicating unchanged affection.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 庐江 - A historical region in present-day Anhui Province, known for its cultural and historical significance.
    • 曹公 - Refers to Cao Cao, a prominent warlord and statesman of the late Eastern Han dynasty.
    • 崔少府 - '少府' is an official title, and '崔' is a surname, indicating a local official named Cao.
    • 三曰婚毕 - '三曰' means 'three days later,' indicating the duration of the wedding.
    • 三月三日 - A traditional Chinese festival, the Double Third Festival, often associated with cultural and social activities.
    • 金钅宛 - '金' means 'gold,' and '钅宛' is a type of bracelet or ornament, indicating a valuable gift.

    Proper Nouns

    • 庐江 - A historical region.
    • 曹公 - Cao Cao, a prominent warlord and statesman.
    • 崔少府 - A local official named Cao.
    • 卢充 - A person involved in the story.
    • 三月三日 - A traditional Chinese festival.
    • 金钅宛 - A type of bracelet or ornament.

    Zhen Di Port in Lujiang is a place where a large ship once sank, said to be one of Cao Cao's vessels. Once, a fisherman stayed there overnight, mooring his boat nearby. He only heard the sounds of zither and flute, accompanied by a pervasive fragrance. In his dream, he was warned by someone to stay away from the official ship. Startled, he woke up and immediately moved his boat away. It is said that Cao Cao had several courtesans on board when the ship sank there, and the wreck still exists today.

    Lu Chong went hunting and shot a deer, hitting it. As he followed it, he didn't realize how far he had gone. Suddenly, he saw a gate resembling an official residence. He asked the gatekeeper, who replied, "This is the residence of Cao the Magistrate." Lu Chong then met Cao, who told him, "Your father has arranged a marriage for my daughter, so I have come to fetch you." Three days later, the wedding was over, and Cao sent Lu Chong back home in a carriage. When Lu's mother asked him about it, he gave a detailed account. After parting from Cao, four years later on the Double Third Festival, Lu Chong went to play by the water. He saw a small carriage by the water and approached it. Inside, he found Cao's daughter with a three-year-old child, and their feelings remained as they were. She handed the child to Lu Chong, gave him water and a gold bracelet, and then parted ways. [32]

    王伯阳家在京口，宅东有大家，相传云是鲁肃墓。伯阳妇，郗鉴兄女也，丧亡，王平其冢以葬。后数年，伯阳白曰在厅事，忽见一贵人，乘平肩舆，与侍从数百人，马皆浴铁。径来坐，谓伯阳曰：“我是鲁子敬，安冢在此二百许年。君何故毁坏吾家”因顾左右：“何不举手！”左右牵伯阳下床，乃以刀环击之数百而去。登时绝死。良久复苏，被击处皆发疽溃，寻便死。一说王伯阳亡，其子营墓，得一漆棺，移至南冈。夜梦肃怒云：“当杀汝父。”寻，复梦见伯阳云：“鲁肃与吾争墓，若不如我，不复得还。”后于灵座褥上见血数，疑鲁肃之故也。墓今在长广桥东一里。 [33]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [33]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 相传云 - It was said or passed down.
    • 兄女 - Sister.
    • 登时 - Immediately.
    • 良久 - After a long time.
    • 寻 - Soon.
    • 一说 - Another account.
    • 营墓 - Prepared the tomb.
    • 漆棺 - Lacquered coffin.
    • 灵座褥上 - On the cushion of the spirit table.
    • 长广桥 - Changguang Bridge.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 王伯阳 - A historical figure, likely a local official or scholar.
    • 郗鉴 - A prominent official during the Eastern Jin Dynasty, known for his integrity and wisdom.
    • 鲁肃 - A famous general and statesman during the Three Kingdoms period, known for his strategic acumen and loyalty to Sun Quan.
    • 京口 - An ancient name for modern-day Zhenjiang, Jiangsu Province, a significant city during the Three Kingdoms and Eastern Jin periods.

    Proper Nouns

    • 王伯阳 - Wang Boyang
    • 郗鉴 - Xi Jian
    • 鲁肃 - Lu Su
    • 京口 - Jingkou (modern-day Zhenjiang)
    • 漆棺 - Lacquered coffin
    • 灵座褥上 - Cushion of the spirit table
    • 长广桥 - Changguang Bridge

    Wang Boyang lived in Jingkou, and to the east of his residence was a large house, which was said to be the tomb of Lu Su. Wang Boyang's wife was the sister of Xi Jian, and after her death, Wang Boyang leveled the tomb and buried her there. Several years later, while sitting in the hall, Wang Boyang suddenly saw a noble person riding a sedan chair, accompanied by hundreds of attendants, and all the horses were as black as if bathed in iron. The noble person came straight and sat down, saying to Wang Boyang, "I am Lu Zijing, and my tomb has been here for about two hundred years. Why did you destroy my home?" He then looked at the people around him and said, "Why don't you raise your hand!" The attendants pulled Wang Boyang off the bed, and then hit him with the blade of the ring hundreds of times before leaving. He died immediately. After a long time, he revived, but the places where he was hit all broke out in sores and ulcers, and soon after, he died. Another account says that Wang Boyang died, and his son prepared the tomb, finding a lacquered coffin and moving it to the South Hill. At night, he dreamed that Lu Su was angry and said, "I will kill your father." Soon after, he dreamed again that Wang Boyang said, "Lu Su and I are fighting over the tomb. If you are not as good as me, you will not be able to return." Later, he saw several spots of blood on the cushion of the spirit table and suspected it was because of Lu Su. The tomb is now one li east of Changguang Bridge. [33]

    承俭者，东莞人。病亡，葬本县界，后十年，忽夜与其县令梦云：“没故民承俭，人今见劫，明府急见救。”令便敕内外装束，作百人仗，便令驰马往冢上。曰已向出，天忽大雾，对面不相见，但闻冢中破棺声。有二人坟上望，雾暝不见人往。令既至，百人同声大叫，收得冢中三人。坟上二人遂得逃走。棺未坏，令即使人修复之。其夜，令又梦俭云：“二人虽得走，民悉志之：一人面上有青志，如藿叶；一人断其前两齿折。明府但案此寻觅，自得也。”令从其言追捕，并擒获。
    　　荆州刺史殷仲堪，布衣时，在丹徒，忽梦见一人，自说己“是上虞人，死亡，浮丧飘流江中，明曰当至。君有济物之仁，岂能见移著高燥处，则恩及枯骨矣”。殷明曰与诸人共江上，看见一棺，逐水流下飘，飘至殷坐处。即令人牵取，题如所梦。即移著冈上，酹以酒饭。是夕，又梦此人来谢恩。 [34]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [34]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 承俭者，东莞人 - Introduces the main subject, Cheng Jian, and his origin.
    • 病亡，葬本县界 - Describes his death and burial.
    • 忽夜与其县令梦云 - Introduces the supernatural element of the dream.
    • 没故民承俭，人今见劫，明府急见救 - The deceased's plea for help, which is both urgent and specific.
    • 令便敕内外装束，作百人仗，便令驰马往冢上 - The magistrate's immediate and detailed response to the dream.
    • 曰已向出，天忽大雾，对面不相见，但闻冢中破棺声 - A vivid and dramatic description of the supernatural event.
    • 有二人坟上望，雾暝不见人往 - Describes the witnesses and the conditions of the fog.
    • 令既至，百人同声大叫，收得冢中三人 - A dramatic and action-packed scene of the capture.
    • 坟上二人遂得逃走 - Continues the story with the escape of the robbers.
    • 棺未坏，令即使人修复之 - A practical and immediate action by the magistrate.
    • 其夜，令又梦俭云 - Continues the supernatural theme with another dream.
    • 二人虽得走，民悉志之：一人面上有青志，如藿叶；一人断其前两齿折 - Provides detailed descriptions of the escaped robbers.
    • 明府但案此寻觅，自得也 - Gives clear and direct instructions for the magistrate to follow.
    • 令从其言追捕，并擒获 - Concludes the first part of the story with the capture of the robbers.
    • 荆州刺史殷仲堪，布衣时，在丹徒，忽梦见一人，自说己“是上虞人，死亡，浮丧飘流江中，明曰当至。君有济物之仁，岂能见移著高燥处，则恩及枯骨矣” - Introduces the second part of the story with a detailed dream narrative.
    • 殷明曰与诸人共江上，看见一棺，逐水流下飘，飘至殷坐处 - Describes the dramatic scene of the coffin floating to Yin Zhongkan.
    • 即令人牵取，题如所梦 - Confirms the accuracy of the dream.
    • 即移著冈上，酹以酒饭 - Describes the respectful act of moving the coffin and offering wine and food.
    • 是夕，又梦此人来谢恩 - Continues the supernatural theme with another dream.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 承俭 - A name of a deceased person, likely a commoner.
    • 东莞 - A place name, indicating the hometown of Cheng Jian.
    • 县令 - The title of a local government official, responsible for the administration of a county.
    • 明府 - A respectful term for a high-ranking official, often used by subordinates.
    • 荆州刺史 - The title of a provincial governor in the Jingzhou region.
    • 殷仲堪 - A historical figure, a governor of Jingzhou during the Eastern Jin Dynasty.
    • 布衣 - Refers to a commoner or a person without official rank.
    • 丹徒 - A place name, indicating where Yin Zhongkan had the dream.
    • 上虞 - A place name, indicating the origin of the deceased person in the dream.
    • 浮丧 - Refers to a body that has not been properly buried and is floating in water.
    • 济物之仁 - Refers to the virtue of helping others, especially in times of need.
    • 酹以酒饭 - A traditional act of offering wine and food to the dead, often performed to show respect and gratitude.

    Proper Nouns

    • 承俭 - The name of the deceased person.
    • 东莞 - A place name, indicating the hometown of Cheng Jian.
    • 县令 - The title of a local government official.
    • 明府 - A respectful term for a high-ranking official.
    • 荆州刺史 - The title of a provincial governor.
    • 殷仲堪 - A historical figure, a governor of Jingzhou.
    • 丹徒 - A place name, indicating where Yin Zhongkan had the dream.
    • 上虞 - A place name, indicating the origin of the deceased person in the dream.

    承俭是东莞人。他病逝后被安葬在本县境内，十年后的一个夜晚，他在梦中向县令诉说：“我是已故的承俭，现在被人劫持，您的府上请快来救我。”县令立即下令准备百人队伍，派人骑马前往他的坟墓。承俭说他已经快要出来了，这时天突然下起了大雾，对面看不见人，只能听到坟墓中棺材被打破的声音。有两个人站在坟墓上观望，但浓雾遮挡了视线，看不见有人过来。县令到达后，百人同时大声呼喊，捉住了墓中的三个人。墓上的两个人趁机逃走了。棺材没有损坏，县令立刻让人修复。当晚，县令又梦见承俭说：“那两个人虽然逃走了，但我都记得他们的样子：一个人脸上有青色的标记，像一片藿叶；另一个人的前两颗门牙断了。您只要根据这些特征去寻找，自然能找到他们。”县令按照他的建议去追捕，最终将他们捉拿归案。

    荆州刺史殷仲堪，在他还是平民的时候，在丹徒突然梦见一个人，那人自称是上虞人，已经去世，尸体漂浮在江中，明天就会到达。他说：“您有救人的仁心，能否将我的尸体移到高而干燥的地方，这样我的遗骨也能得到您的恩惠。”第二天，殷仲堪与众人一起到江边，看见一具棺材随着水流漂来，漂到了殷仲堪坐的地方。他立即派人将棺材拉上来，发现棺材上的铭文与梦中所见一致。于是将棺材移到高处，用酒饭祭奠。当晚，他又梦见这个人来感谢他。 [34]

    晋升平中，徐州刺史索逊乘船往晋陵。会暗发，回河行数里，有人求索寄载，云：“我家在韩冢，脚痛不能行，寄君船去。”四更时至韩冢，此人便去。逊遣人牵船，过一渡，施力殊不便，骂此人曰：“我数里载汝来，径去，不与人牵船。”欲与痛手。此人便还与牵，不觉用力而得渡。人便径入诸冢间。逊疑非人，使窃寻看。此人经冢间，便不复见。须臾复出，至一冢呼曰：“载公。”有出应者。此人云“我向载人船来，不与共牵，奴便欲打我。今当往报之，欲暂借甘罗来。”载公曰：“坏我甘罗，不可得。”此人云：“无所苦，我试之耳。”逊闻此，即还船。须臾，岸上有物来，赤如百斛龠，长二丈许，径来向船，逊便大呼：“奴载我船，不与我牵，不得痛手！方便载公，甘罗今欲击我。今曰即打坏奴甘罗。”言讫，忽然便失，于是遂进。 [35]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [35]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 晋升平中 - Refers to the year 'Jingping' during the Eastern Jin Dynasty (356-361 AD).
    • 徐州刺史 - Title for a provincial governor in the Eastern Jin government.
    • 晋陵 - Ancient name for modern-day Jinling, now Nanjing, the capital of Jiangsu Province.
    • 韩冢 - Location near a tomb, possibly a historical or legendary site.
    • 四更时 - The fourth watch of the night, typically around midnight to 2 AM.
    • 甘罗 - A proper noun that could refer to a specific object or person, but its exact meaning is unclear without additional context.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 晋升平中 - The year 'Jingping' during the Eastern Jin Dynasty (356-361 AD).
    • 徐州刺史 - Title for a provincial governor in the Eastern Jin government.
    • 晋陵 - Ancient name for modern-day Jinling, now Nanjing, the capital of Jiangsu Province.
    • 韩冢 - Location near a tomb, possibly a historical or legendary site.
    • 四更时 - The fourth watch of the night, typically around midnight to 2 AM.
    • 甘罗 - A proper noun that could refer to a specific object or person, but its exact meaning is unclear without additional context.

    During the Jingping era, the governor of Xuzhou, Suo Xun, was traveling by boat to Jinling. Suddenly, darkness fell, and they returned to the river, traveling several miles. A man asked to be carried, saying, "My home is at Han's tomb, and my feet hurt so I cannot walk; please take me in your boat." At the fourth watch of the night, they arrived at Han's tomb, and the man then left. Xun sent someone to pull the boat, crossing a ford, but applying force was very inconvenient. He scolded the man, saying, "I carried you several miles, and you just left, without helping to pull the boat." He wanted to hit him. The man then returned to help pull, and without realizing it, they managed to cross. The man then directly entered the area between the tombs. Xun suspected it was not a human and sent someone to secretly investigate. The man passed through the area between the tombs and was no longer seen. After a short while, he reappeared and went to a tomb, calling out, "Zaigong." Someone came out in response. The man said, "I came here in your boat, and you didn't help pull, so you wanted to hit me. Now I will go to report this, and I want to temporarily borrow Ganro." Zaigong said, "Destroying my Ganro is not possible." The man replied, "It doesn't hurt, I was just testing it." Xun heard this and immediately returned to the boat. After a short while, something came from the shore, red like a hundred斛龠, about two zhang long, and came straight towards the boat. Xun shouted loudly, "You carried me in your boat, and you didn't help pull, so you can't hit me! It is convenient to carry you, but Ganro now wants to hit me. Today, I will destroy your Ganro." After he finished speaking, it suddenly disappeared, and so he proceeded. [35]

    晋元熙中，上党冯述为相府吏，将假归虎牢。忽逢四人，各持绳及杖，来赴述。述策马避，马不肯进。四人各捉马一足，倏然便到河上。问述：“欲渡否”述曰：“水深不测，既无舟楫，如何得渡君正欲见杀尔。”四人云：“不相杀，当持君赴官。”遂复捉马脚涉河而北。述但闻波浪声，而不觉水。垂至岸，四人相谓曰：“此人不净，那得将去。”时述有弟丧服，深恐鬼离之，便当溺水死，乃鞭马作势，径得登岸。述辞谢曰：“既蒙恩德，何敢复烦劳。” [36]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [36]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 晋元熙中 - The year 420 during the Eastern Jin Dynasty, specifically the reign of Emperor Gong (元熙).
    • 水深不测 - The water is deep and unfathomable.
    • 鬼离之 - A belief in supernatural entities that could harm or leave someone, often associated with bad omens or curses.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 上党 - An ancient Chinese region, now part of Shanxi Province.
    • 相府 - The Chancellor's office, a high-ranking government institution.
    • 虎牢 - An ancient city, now known as Huoluo, located in Henan Province.
    • 丧服 - Mourning attire, indicating a family member had recently passed away.

    Proper Nouns

    • 晋元熙中 - The year 420 during the Eastern Jin Dynasty.
    • 上党 - An ancient Chinese region, now part of Shanxi Province.
    • 相府 - The Chancellor's office.
    • 虎牢 - An ancient city, now known as Huoluo, located in Henan Province.
    • 丧服 - Mourning attire.
    • 鬼离之 - A belief in supernatural entities that could harm or leave someone.

    During the reign of Emperor Gong in the Eastern Jin Dynasty (420), Feng Shu of Shangdang served as an official in the Chancellor's office. He was going on leave to return to Huoluo. Suddenly, he encountered four people, each holding a rope and a staff, who came to meet him. Feng Shu spurred his horse to avoid them, but the horse refused to move forward. The four people each grabbed one of the horse's legs and suddenly arrived at the riverbank. They asked, "Do you want to cross?" Feng Shu replied, "The water is deep and unfathomable, and there are no boats. How can we cross? You are just going to kill us." The four people said, "We won't kill you; we will take you to the authorities." They then grabbed the horse's legs and crossed the river to the north. Feng Shu only heard the sound of waves but did not feel the water. Nearly reaching the shore, the four people talked to each other, saying, "This person is not clean. How can we take him away?" At that time, Feng Shu had a brother in mourning attire, and he was deeply afraid that the ghosts would leave him, fearing he would drown and die. He then whipped the horse to make it run and managed to reach the shore directly. He then thanked them, saying, "Having received your kindness, how dare I trouble you again." [36]

    安丰侯王戎，字浚冲，琅邪临沂人也。尝赴人家殡殓。主人治棺未竟，送者悉入厅事上。安丰作车中卧。忽见空中有一异物，如鸟。熟视，转大渐近，见一乘赤马车，一人在中，著帻，赤衣，手持一斧，至地下车，径入王车中。回几容之，谓王曰：“君神明清照，物无隐情。亦有事，故来相从。然当为君一言：凡人家殡殓葬送，苟非至亲，不可急往，良不获已，可乘赤车，令髯奴御之，及乘白马，则可禳之。”因谓戎：“君当致位三公。”语良久。主人内棺当殡，众客悉入，此鬼亦入。既入户，鬼便持斧行棺墙上。有一亲趋棺，欲与亡人诀。鬼便以斧正打其额，即倒地。左右扶出。鬼于棺上，视戎而笑。众悉见鬼持斧而出。 [37]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [37]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 安丰侯 - Title of Wang Rong, indicating his noble status.
    • 字浚冲 - Wang Rong's courtesy name, a common practice in ancient China.
    • 琅邪临沂人也 - Indicates Wang Rong's place of origin, Linyi, in Langya (modern-day Shandong).
    • 赴人家殡殓 - Means 'to attend a funeral at someone's home.'
    • 治棺未竟 - Means 'the coffin-making is not yet complete.'
    • 送者悉入厅事上 - Means 'all the mourners entered the hall.'
    • 作车中卧 - Means 'he lay down in his carriage.'
    • 异物 - Refers to an unusual or strange object.
    • 如鸟 - Means 'like a bird.'
    • 熟视 - Means 'to look closely.'
    • 转大渐近 - Means 'it grew larger and came closer.'
    • 赤马车 - Refers to a red horse-drawn carriage.
    • 著帻 - Means 'wearing a headband.'
    • 赤衣 - Means 'red clothing.'
    • 手持一斧 - Means 'holding an axe.'
    • 地下车 - Means 'got out of the carriage.'
    • 径入王车中 - Means 'directly entered Wang's carriage.'
    • 回几容之 - Means 'he turned and accommodated himself in the carriage.'
    • 谓王曰 - Means 'said to Wang.'
    • 君神明清照 - Means 'Your divine clarity and insight.'
    • 物无隐情 - Means 'nothing is hidden from you.'
    • 亦有事 - Means 'there is something to say.'
    • 故来相从 - Means 'so I have come to accompany you.'
    • 然当为君一言 - Means 'but I will give you one piece of advice.'
    • 凡人家殡殓葬送 - Means 'in general, when people are buried and mourned.'
    • 苟非至亲 - Means 'if they are not close relatives.'
    • 不可急往 - Means 'you should not rush to attend.'
    • 良不获已 - Means 'if you have no choice.'
    • 可乘赤车 - Means 'you can ride in a red carriage.'
    • 令髯奴御之 - Means 'let a bearded servant drive it.'
    • 及乘白马 - Means 'or ride a white horse.'
    • 则可禳之 - Means 'then you can ward off evil.'
    • 君当致位三公 - Means 'You will attain the position of one of the Three Dukes.'
    • 语良久 - Means 'they spoke for a long time.'
    • 内棺当殡 - Means 'when the coffin was placed inside for burial.'
    • 众悉见鬼持斧而出 - Means 'everyone saw the ghost holding an axe leave.'

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 安丰侯 - Wang Rong was a high-ranking official during the Jin dynasty.
    • 琅邪临沂 - Linyi, in Langya, is a historically significant place in Shandong province.
    • 殡殓葬送 - Refers to the traditional Chinese funeral rites, which involve preparing the body, mourning, and burial.
    • 三公 - Refers to the highest-ranking officials in the imperial court, such as the Grand Tutor, Grand Guardian, and Grand Administrator.
    • 鬼持斧 - The ghost holding an axe is a supernatural element, often associated with protection or punishment in Chinese folklore.

    Proper Nouns

    • 安丰侯王戎 - Wang Rong, a high-ranking official during the Jin dynasty.
    • 浚冲 - Wang Rong's courtesy name.
    • 琅邪临沂 - Linyi, in Langya, a place in Shandong province.
    • 三公 - The highest-ranking officials in the imperial court.

    Translation:

    Lord Wang Rong of Anfeng, courtesy name Junchong, was from Linyi, Langya. Once, he attended a funeral at someone's home. The host was still in the process of preparing the coffin, and all the mourners had entered the main hall. Lord Wang lay down in his carriage. Suddenly, he saw an unusual object in the sky, like a bird. Upon closer inspection, it grew larger and came closer. He saw a red horse-drawn carriage with a man inside, wearing a headband and red clothing, holding an axe. The man got out of the carriage and directly entered Lord Wang's carriage. He turned and accommodated himself in the carriage, saying to Lord Wang, "Your divine clarity and insight are such that nothing is hidden from you. I have something to say, so I have come to accompany you. I will give you one piece of advice: in general, when people are buried and mourned, if they are not close relatives, you should not rush to attend. If you have no choice, you can ride in a red carriage, let a bearded servant drive it, or ride a white horse, then you can ward off evil." He then told Lord Wang, "You will attain the position of one of the Three Dukes." They spoke for a long time. When the host placed the coffin inside for burial, all the guests entered, and this ghost also entered. After entering the house, the ghost took the axe and struck it on the wall of the coffin. One relative rushed to the coffin, wanting to bid farewell to the deceased. The ghost struck him on the forehead with the axe, and he immediately fell to the ground. The attendants helped him out. The ghost stood on the coffin, looked at Lord Wang, and laughed. Everyone saw the ghost holding the axe leave. [37]

    李子豫，少善医方，当代称其通灵。许永为豫州刺史，镇历阳。其弟得病，心腹疼痛十余年，殆死。忽一夜，闻屏风后有鬼谓腹中鬼曰：“何不速杀之。不然，李子豫当从此过。以赤丸打汝，汝其死矣。”腹中鬼对曰：“吾不畏之。”及旦，许永遂使人候子豫，果来。未入门，病者自闻中有呻吟声。及子豫入视，曰：“鬼病也。”遂于巾箱中出八毒赤丸子与服之。须臾，腹中雷鸣鼓转，大利数行，遂差。今八毒丸方是也。 [38]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [38]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 少善医方 - Young and skilled in medical formulas.
    • 当代称其通灵 - Contemporary people praised him for his supernatural insight.
    • 心腹疼痛十余年 - Chest and abdominal pain for over ten years.
    • 殆死 - Almost died.
    • 忽一夜 - One night suddenly.
    • 闻屏风后有鬼谓腹中鬼曰 - Heard a ghost behind the screen saying to the ghost in the abdomen.
    • 何不速杀之 - Why not kill it quickly?
    • 不然，李子豫当从此过 - Otherwise, Li Ziyu will pass by here.
    • 以赤丸打汝，汝其死矣 - With the red pill, I will hit you, and you will die.
    • 腹中鬼对曰 - The ghost in the abdomen responded, saying.
    • 吾不畏之 - I do not fear it.
    • 及旦，许永遂使人候子豫 - By dawn, Xu Yong then sent someone to wait for Ziyu.
    • 果来 - Indeed came.
    • 未入门，病者自闻中有呻吟声 - Not yet having entered the door, the patient heard groaning sounds inside.
    • 及子豫入视 - By the time Ziyu entered to examine.
    • 曰：“鬼病也” - Said, 'It is a ghostly illness.'
    • 遂于巾箱中出八毒赤丸子与服之 - Then took out the Eight Toxins Red Pills from the medicine box and gave them to take.
    • 须臾，腹中雷鸣鼓转 - In a moment, thundering and drumming sounds in the abdomen.
    • 大利数行，遂差 - A lot of diarrhea several times, and finally recovered.
    • 今八毒丸方是也 - Now the formula for the Eight Toxins Pills is this.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 李子豫 - A historical figure known for his skills in medicine.
    • 许永 - A historical figure who served as a governor.
    • 豫州刺史 - A high-ranking official in charge of the state of Yu.
    • 镇历阳 - The place where Xu Yong was stationed.
    • 八毒赤丸子 - A famous ancient Chinese medicine formula known for its strong effects.

    Proper Nouns

    • 李子豫 - Li Ziyu
    • 许永 - Xu Yong
    • 豫州 - State of Yu
    • 历阳 - Liyang
    • 八毒赤丸子 - Eight Toxins Red Pills

    Li Ziyu, young and skilled in medical formulas, was praised by contemporary people for his supernatural insight. Xu Yong, serving as the governor of the state of Yu, was stationed in Liyang. His younger brother had suffered from chest and abdominal pain for over ten years and was nearly dead. One night, he suddenly heard a ghost behind the screen saying to the ghost in his abdomen, "Why not kill it quickly? Otherwise, Li Ziyu will pass by here. With the red pill, I will hit you, and you will die." The ghost in the abdomen responded, "I do not fear it." By dawn, Xu Yong then sent someone to wait for Ziyu, who indeed came. Not yet having entered the door, the patient heard groaning sounds inside. By the time Ziyu entered to examine, he said, "It is a ghostly illness." He then took out the Eight Toxins Red Pills from the medicine box and gave them to take. In a moment, thundering and drumming sounds in the abdomen, a lot of diarrhea several times, and finally recovered. Now the formula for the Eight Toxins Pills is this. [38]

    宋元嘉十四年，广陵盛道儿亡，托孤女于妇弟申翼之。服阕，翼之以其女嫁北乡严齐息，寒门也，丰其礼赂，始成婚。道儿忽空中怒曰：“吾喘唾之气，举门户相托。如何昧利忘义，结婚微族。”翼之乃大惶愧。
    　　晋淮南胡茂回，能见鬼。虽不喜见，而不可止。后行至扬州，还历阳。城东有神祠，中正值民将巫祝视之。至须臾顷，有群鬼相叱曰：“上官来。”各迸走出祠去。回顾，见二沙门来入祠中。诸鬼两两三三相抱持，在祠边草中伺望。望见沙门，皆有怖惧。须臾，二沙门去后，诸鬼皆还祠中。回于是信佛，遂精诚奉事。 [39]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [39]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 喘唾之气，举门户相托 - Metaphorical expression meaning 'the breath of life, entrusting the family's future.'
    • 昧利忘义 - Expression meaning 'forgetting righteousness for the sake of gain.'
    • 结婚微族 - Expression meaning 'marrying into a lowly family.'
    • 大惶愧 - Expression meaning 'greatly frightened and ashamed.'

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 宋元嘉十四年 - The 14th year of the Yungang era during the Southern Song dynasty.
    • 广陵 - An ancient name for Yangzhou, a city in present-day Jiangsu province.
    • 妇弟 - Refers to a brother-in-law, specifically Shen Yizhi.
    • 寒门 - Refers to a low-ranking or poor family.
    • 晋淮南 - Refers to Huainan, a region in present-day Anhui province during the Jin dynasty.
    • 神祠 - Refers to a shrine or temple dedicated to a deity or spirit.
    • 巫祝 - Refers to a shaman or priest who performs rituals and communicates with spirits.
    • 沙门 - Refers to Buddhist monks.
    • 精诚奉事 - Refers to sincere and devoted worship or service.

    Proper Nouns

    • 宋元嘉十四年 - The 14th year of the Yungang era during the Southern Song dynasty.
    • 广陵 - An ancient name for Yangzhou.
    • 盛道儿 - The deceased father.
    • 申翼之 - The brother-in-law.
    • 北乡严齐息 - The husband-to-be from a low-ranking family.
    • 晋 - The Jin dynasty.
    • 淮南 - A region in present-day Anhui province.
    • 胡茂回 - The name of the person who could see ghosts.
    • 神祠 - A shrine or temple.
    • 沙门 - Buddhist monks.

    During the 14th year of the Yungang era of the Song dynasty, a man named Sheng Dao'er from Guangling died, leaving behind an orphaned daughter whom he entrusted to his brother-in-law, Shen Yizhi. After completing the mourning rites, Shen Yizhi arranged for his daughter to marry a man named Yan Qixi from the northern suburbs, who came from a low-ranking family. He lavished gifts and hospitality, and the marriage was finally consummated. Suddenly, Sheng Dao'er appeared from the air and angrily said, "With my very breath, I entrusted the future of our family to you. How could you forget righteousness for the sake of gain and marry into a lowly family?" Shen Yizhi was greatly frightened and ashamed.

    During the Jin dynasty, a man named Hu Maohui from Huainan had the ability to see ghosts. Although he did not like seeing them, he could not stop it. Later, he traveled to Yangzhou and returned to Lixia. In the east of the city, there was a shrine where a local was about to perform a ritual with a shaman. After a short while, a group of ghosts shouted at each other, "The superior official has arrived." They all fled out of the shrine. Looking back, they saw two monks enter the shrine. The ghosts, in twos and threes, embraced each other and hid in the grass near the shrine, watching and waiting. When they saw the monks, they were all filled with fear. After a short while, when the two monks left, the ghosts returned to the shrine. Hu Maohui thus came to believe in Buddhism and sincerely worshiped. [39]

    有一伧小儿，放牛野中，伴辈数人。见一鬼依诸丛草间，处处设网，欲以捕人。设网后未竟，伧小儿窃取前网，仍以罨捕，即缚得鬼。
    　　庐江杜谦为诸暨令。县西山下有一鬼，长三丈，著赭衣裤穿褶，在草中拍张。又脱褶掷草上，作“懊恼歌”。百姓皆看之。
    　　会稽朱弼为国郎中令，营立第舍，未成而卒。同郡谢子木代其事，以弼死亡，乃簿书多张功赏，长百余万，以其赃诬弼。而实自入。子木夜寝，忽闻有人道弼姓字者。俄顷而到子木堂前，谓之曰：“卿以枯骨腐专可得诬，当以某曰夜更与对证。”言终，忽然不见。
    　　夏侯综为安西参军，常见鬼骑马满道，与人无异。尝与人载行，忽牵人语，指道上有一小儿云：“此儿正须大病。”须臾，此儿果病，殆死。其母闻之，诘综。综云：“无他，此儿向于道中掷涂，误中一鬼脚。鬼怒，故病汝儿尔。得以酒饭遗鬼，即差。”母如言而愈。 [40]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [40]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 有一伧小儿 - Colloquial expression meaning 'a rustic child' or 'a country child,' often used to describe someone from a rural background.
    • 伴辈数人 - Means 'a few companions' or 'a group of friends,' indicating that the child was with others.
    • 见一鬼依诸丛草间 - Literally 'saw a ghost leaning among the grass,' describing the ghost's position.
    • 处处设网，欲以捕人 - Means 'setting traps everywhere, intending to catch people,' indicating the ghost's intention.
    • 窃取前网，仍以罨捕 - Means 'secretly took the front trap and used it to catch,' describing the child's action.
    • 缚得鬼 - Means 'caught the ghost,' indicating the outcome of the child's action.
    • 庐江杜谦为诸暨令 - Means 'Du Qian of Lujiang was the magistrate of Zhujin,' providing historical context.
    • 长三丈 - Means 'three zhang long,' a unit of measurement, indicating the size of the ghost.
    • 著赭衣裤穿褶 - Means 'wearing ochre-colored pants and a robe,' describing the ghost's attire.
    • 拍张 - Means 'to wave or flap,' describing the ghost's movements.
    • 脱褶掷草上 - Means 'took off the robe and threw it on the grass,' indicating the ghost's action.
    • 作“懊恼歌” - Means 'sang a song of annoyance,' describing the ghost's behavior.
    • 夏侯综为安西参军 - Means 'Xia Houzong was a military officer in the Western Command,' providing historical context.
    • 骑马满道 - Means 'riding horses filling the road,' describing the scene.
    • 与人无异 - Means 'no different from people,' indicating that the ghosts looked like humans.
    • 牵人语 - Means 'pulled someone to talk,' describing the ghost's interaction with the child.
    • 掷涂 - Means 'threw mud,' indicating the child's action.
    • 遗鬼 - Means 'give to the ghost,' indicating the mother's action.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 伧小儿 - A term often used to describe a rustic or unrefined person, reflecting the social hierarchy and regional distinctions of the time.
    • 庐江 - A region in modern-day Anhui Province, known for its historical significance.
    • 诸暨 - A county in Zhejiang Province, where Du Qian served as a magistrate.
    • 赭衣裤穿褶 - Ochre-colored pants and a robe, typical attire for certain social or religious figures.
    • 安西 - Refers to the Western Command, a military region in ancient China.
    • 懊恼歌 - A type of folk song expressing frustration or annoyance.

    Proper Nouns

    • 伧小儿 - A rustic child.
    • 庐江杜谦 - Du Qian of Lujiang.
    • 诸暨 - Zhujin, a county in Zhejiang Province.
    • 赭衣裤穿褶 - Ochre-colored pants and a robe.
    • 安西 - Western Command.
    • 夏侯综 - Xia Houzong, a military officer.
    • 懊恼歌 - Song of annoyance.

    A rustic child, while grazing cattle in the wild, was accompanied by several friends. They saw a ghost leaning among the grass, setting traps everywhere with the intention of catching people. Before the ghost had finished setting the traps, the child secretly took the front trap and used it to catch the ghost, thereby capturing the ghost.

    Du Qian of Lujiang served as the magistrate of Zhujin. In the west of the county, there was a ghost three zhang long, wearing ochre-colored pants and a robe, waving and flapping in the grass. The ghost also took off the robe and threw it on the grass, singing a song of annoyance. The people all watched it.

    Zhu Bi of Kuaiji served as a court official. He was building his residence but died before it was completed. His fellow townsman Xie Zimu took over his affairs. Taking advantage of Zhu Bi's death, Xie Zimu falsified records to claim many rewards, amounting to over a million coins, and used this to defame Zhu Bi. In reality, the money was for himself. One night, while sleeping, Xie Zimu suddenly heard someone mentioning Zhu Bi's name. Soon after, the person appeared before Xie Zimu's hall and said, "You can defame a dead man, but you will face me on a certain day and night for a confrontation." After speaking, the person disappeared suddenly.

    Xia Houzong served as a military officer in the Western Command. He often saw ghosts riding horses filling the road, looking no different from people. Once, while traveling with someone, he was suddenly pulled to talk by a ghost, who pointed to a child on the road and said, "This child needs to fall ill." Soon after, the child indeed fell ill and nearly died. The child's mother heard about it and questioned Xia Houzong. Xia Houzong replied, "There is nothing to worry about. The child threw mud on the road and accidentally hit a ghost's foot. The ghost was angry and thus caused your child to fall ill. If you give the ghost some food and wine, it will recover." The mother followed his advice, and the child recovered. [40]

    顺阳范启，母丧当葬。前母墓在顺阳，往视之，既至而坟垅杂沓，难可识别，不知何所。袁彦仁时为豫州，往看之，因云：“闻有一人见鬼。”范即如言，令物色觅之。比至，云：“墓中一人衣服颜状如此。”即开墓，棺物皆烂，冢中灰壤深尺余。意甚疑之。试令人以足拨灰中土，冀得旧物，果得一砖，铭云“范坚之妻”。然后信之。
    　　沙门竺法师，会稽人也，与北中郎王坦之周旋甚厚。每共论死生罪福报应之事茫昧难明，因便共要，若有先死者，当相报。语后经年，王于庙中忽见法师来，曰：“贫道以某月曰命故，罪福皆不虚，应若影响。檀越惟当勤修道德，以升跻神明耳。先与君要，先死者相报，故来相语。”言讫，忽然不见。坦之寻亦卒。 [41]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [41]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 母喪當葬 - Literally 'mother's death to be buried,' indicating the preparation for a mother's funeral.
    • 坟垅杂沓 - Describes the graves as being numerous and disorderly.
    • 罪福皆不虚 - Means 'karma and retribution are not in vain,' expressing the belief in karma.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 順陽 - A place name, referring to Shunyang, a location in ancient China.
    • 范啟 - A personal name, referring to Fan Qi, a historical figure.
    • 袁彦仁 - A historical figure, a person known for his ability to see ghosts.
    • 豫州 - A place name, referring to Yuzhou, an administrative region in ancient China.
    • 沙门 - A term for Buddhist monks, indicating the religious affiliation of the monk.
    • 竺法师 - A personal name, referring to a specific monk.
    • 会稽 - A place name, referring to Kuaiji, a region in ancient China.
    • 北中郎王坦之 - A historical figure, Wang Tan, who held the position of Northern Attendant.
    • 庙 - A term for a temple or shrine, indicating the place where the monk appeared to Wang.
    • 贫道 - A humble term used by monks to refer to themselves.
    • 檀越 - A respectful term used by monks to refer to laypeople.

    Proper Nouns

    • 順陽 - Shunyang, a place name.
    • 范啟 - Fan Qi, a personal name.
    • 袁彦仁 - Yuan Yanren, a personal name.
    • 豫州 - Yuzhou, a place name.
    • 沙门竺法师 - Monk Zhi, a personal name.
    • 会稽 - Kuaiji, a place name.
    • 北中郎王坦之 - Wang Tan, a personal name.

    Fan Qi from Shunyang was preparing to bury his mother. His mother's grave was in Shunyang, and he went to see it. Upon arrival, he found the graves to be numerous and disorderly, making it difficult to identify which one was his mother's. He did not know which grave was hers.

    At that time, Yuan Yanren was the governor of Yuzhou. He went to see Fan Qi and said, "I have heard of someone who can see ghosts." Fan Qi immediately followed his advice and had someone search for the grave. By the time they arrived, they reported, "There is a person in the tomb with such clothing and appearance." They immediately opened the tomb and found that all the coffin contents were rotten. The grave was filled with ash and soil to a depth of more than a foot. Fan Qi was very suspicious. He tried to have someone use their foot to stir the soil in the ash, hoping to find some old object. Indeed, they found a brick, and the inscription read "wife of Fan Jian." Then, he believed it.

    Monk Zhi, a monk from Kuaiji, had a very close relationship with Wang Tan, the Northern Attendant. They often discussed matters of life and death, karma, and retribution, which were vague and hard to understand. Therefore, they made a pact: if one of them died first, they would inform the other. After several years, Wang suddenly saw the monk come in the temple and said, "I died on a certain day of a certain month. Karma and retribution are not in vain; they respond like echoes. You should diligently cultivate virtue and morality to ascend to the divine. As we agreed earlier, the one who dies first will inform the other, so I came to tell you." After speaking, he suddenly disappeared. Wang soon died as well. [41]

    乐安刘池苟，家在夏口，忽有一鬼来住刘家。初因暗彷佛见形如人，著白布裤。自尔后，数曰一来，不复隐形。便不去，喜偷食。不以为患，然且难之。初不敢呵骂。吉翼子者，强梁不信鬼，至刘家，谓主人曰：“卿家鬼何在唤来，今为卿骂之。”即闻屋梁作声。时大有客，共仰视，便纷纭掷一物下，正著翼子面，视之，乃主人家妇女亵衣，恶犹著焉。众共大笑为乐。吉大惭，洗面而去。有人语刘：“此鬼偷食，乃食尽，必有形之物，可以毒药中之。”刘即于他家煮冶葛，取二升汁，密赍还家。向夜，举家作粥糜，食余一瓯，因泻葛汁著中，置于几上，以盆覆之。人定后，闻鬼从外来，发盆啖糜。既讫，便掷破瓯走去。须臾间，在屋头吐，嗔怒非常，便捧打窗户。刘先已防备，与斗。亦不敢入。至四更，然后遂绝。 [42]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [42]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 樂安劉池苟 - Proper name, 'Liu Chigou from Yue'an,' referring to a person from the Yue'an area.
    • 夏口 - Place name, 'Xia Kou,' referring to a location in Hubei Province.
    • 鬼 - Term for 'ghost' or 'spirit,' often associated with supernatural phenomena.
    • 暗彷佛見形如人 - Descriptive phrase meaning 'vaguely saw a shape resembling a human,' indicating a ghostly apparition.
    • 白布裤 - Description of 'white cloth trousers,' the clothing worn by the ghost.
    • 數曰一來 - Phrase meaning 'comes every few days,' indicating a regular but infrequent visit.
    • 不復隐形 - Phrase meaning 'no longer invisible,' suggesting the ghost has become more visible over time.
    • 喜偷食 - Phrase meaning 'likes to steal food,' indicating the ghost's mischievous behavior.
    • 不以為患 - Phrase meaning 'not considered a problem,' suggesting the family did not see the ghost as a threat.
    • 難之 - Phrase meaning 'find it difficult,' indicating the family's discomfort with the ghost's presence.
    • 吉翼子 - Proper name, 'Ji Yizi,' referring to a person named Ji Yizi.
    • 強梁不信鬼 - Descriptive phrase meaning 'strong and unafraid of ghosts,' indicating Ji Yizi's bold nature.
    • 卿家鬼何在 - Phrase meaning 'where is the ghost in your house,' a question directed at the host.
    • 今為卿罵之 - Phrase meaning 'I will curse it for you,' indicating Ji Yizi's willingness to confront the ghost.
    • 屋梁作聲 - Phrase meaning 'made a sound in the beam,' indicating the ghost's reaction to the curse.
    • 大有客 - Phrase meaning 'there were many guests,' indicating a social gathering.
    • 共仰視 - Phrase meaning 'looked up together,' indicating the guests' reaction to the sound.
    • 紛纭擲一物下 - Phrase meaning 'threw something down in a flurry,' indicating the ghost's action.
    • 正著翼子面 - Phrase meaning 'hit Ji Yizi's face,' indicating the object's impact.
    • 視之 - Phrase meaning 'looked at it,' indicating the examination of the object.
    • 乃主人家婦女亵衣 - Phrase meaning 'was the undergarment of the host's wife,' indicating the nature of the object.
    • 惡猶著焉 - Phrase meaning 'still dirty,' indicating the state of the undergarment.
    • 眾共大笑為樂 - Phrase meaning 'everyone laughed and had fun,' indicating the reaction to the incident.
    • 吉大慚 - Phrase meaning 'Ji Yizi was greatly embarrassed,' indicating his reaction to the incident.
    • 洗面而去 - Phrase meaning 'washed his face and left,' indicating Ji Yizi's departure.
    • 有人語劉 - Phrase meaning 'someone told Liu,' indicating a piece of advice.
    • 此鬼偷食 - Phrase meaning 'this ghost steals food,' indicating the ghost's behavior.
    • 乃食盡 - Phrase meaning 'eats everything,' indicating the ghost's voracity.
    • 必有形之物 - Phrase meaning 'must be a tangible object,' indicating the suggestion to use a physical trap.
    • 可以毒藥中之 - Phrase meaning 'can be trapped with poison,' indicating the suggested method.
    • 劉即於他家煮冶葛 - Phrase meaning 'Liu cooked the herb *Tianma* in another house,' indicating the preparation of the trap.
    • 取二升汁 - Phrase meaning 'took two cups of juice,' indicating the amount of the poison.
    • 密赍還家 - Phrase meaning 'secretly brought it back home,' indicating the stealthy action.
    • 向夜 - Phrase meaning 'toward night,' indicating the time of the action.
    • 舉家作粥糜 - Phrase meaning 'the whole family made porridge,' indicating the preparation of food.
    • 食餘一瓯 - Phrase meaning 'left one bowl of leftover,' indicating the remaining food.
    • 因泻葛汁著中 - Phrase meaning 'poured the *Tianma* juice into it,' indicating the setup of the trap.
    • 置於几上 - Phrase meaning 'placed it on the table,' indicating the placement of the trap.
    • 以盆覆之 - Phrase meaning 'covered it with a bowl,' indicating the concealment of the trap.
    • 人定後 - Phrase meaning 'after people settled down,' indicating the time of the ghost's appearance.
    • 聞鬼從外来 - Phrase meaning 'heard the ghost coming from outside,' indicating the ghost's arrival.
    • 發盆啖糜 - Phrase meaning 'opened the bowl and ate the porridge,' indicating the ghost's action.
    • 既讫 - Phrase meaning 'after finishing,' indicating the completion of the action.
    • 便掷破瓯走去 - Phrase meaning 'threw the broken bowl away and left,' indicating the ghost's departure.
    • 須臾間 - Phrase meaning 'in a short while,' indicating a brief period of time.
    • 在屋頭吐 - Phrase meaning 'vomited in the house,' indicating the ghost's reaction.
    • 嗔怒非常 - Phrase meaning 'extremely angry,' indicating the ghost's emotional state.
    • 便捧打窗户 - Phrase meaning 'pounded the windows,' indicating the ghost's aggressive action.
    • 劉先已防备 - Phrase meaning 'Liu had already prepared,' indicating the family's readiness.
    • 與斗 - Phrase meaning 'fought back,' indicating the family's resistance.
    • 亦不敢入 - Phrase meaning 'also dared not enter,' indicating the ghost's fear.
    • 至四更 - Phrase meaning 'until the fourth watch,' indicating the duration of the fight.
    • 然后遂绝 - Phrase meaning 'then finally died,' indicating the ghost's demise.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 樂安 - Place name, 'Yue'an,' referring to a region in Shandong Province.
    • 夏口 - Place name, 'Xia Kou,' referring to a location in Hubei Province.
    • 鬼 - Term for 'ghost' or 'spirit,' often associated with supernatural phenomena.
    • 冶葛 - Plant, 'Tianma,' known for its medicinal properties and toxicity.
    • 粥糜 - Type of porridge, a common food in ancient China.
    • 四更 - Time division in the night, corresponding to the hours of 1:00 AM to 3:00 AM.

    A man from Yue'an named Liu Chigou lived in Xia Kou. Suddenly, a ghost came to reside in Liu's house. At first, the ghost appeared vaguely as a human shape, wearing white cloth trousers. After that, it came every few days and no longer remained invisible. It would not leave and liked to steal food. The family did not consider it a problem, but they found it difficult to deal with. Initially, they dared not scold it. Ji Yizi, a strong and unafraid man who did not believe in ghosts, went to Liu's house and told the host, "Where is the ghost in your house? I will curse it for you." Immediately, there was a sound from the beam. There were many guests present, and they all looked up. The ghost then threw something down in a flurry, which hit Ji Yizi's face. He looked at it and saw that it was the undergarment of the host's wife, still dirty. Everyone laughed and had fun. Ji Yizi was greatly embarrassed, washed his face, and left. Someone told Liu, "This ghost steals food and eats everything. It must be a tangible object, and you can trap it with poison." Liu then cooked the herb Tianma in another house, took two cups of juice, and secretly brought it back home. Toward night, the whole family made porridge, and there was one bowl of leftover. Liu poured the Tianma juice into it, placed it on the table, and covered it with a bowl. After people settled down, they heard the ghost coming from outside. It opened the bowl and ate the porridge. After finishing, it threw the broken bowl away and left. In a short while, it vomited in the house, extremely angry, and pounded the windows. Liu had already prepared, and they fought back. The ghost also dared not enter. Until the fourth watch, it finally died. [42]

    ●卷七
    　　庐陵巴邱人陈济者，作州吏，其妇秦，独在家。常有一丈夫，长丈余，仪容端正，著绛碧袍，采色炫耀，来从之。后常相期于一山涧间。至于寝处，不觉有人道相感接。如是数年。比邻入观其所至，辄有虹见。秦至水侧，丈夫以金瓶引水共饮。后遂有身，生而如人，多肉。济假还，秦惧见之，乃纳儿著瓮中。此丈夫以金瓶与之，令覆儿，云：“小儿，未可得将去。不须作衣，我自衣之。”即与绛囊以裹之，令可时出与乳。于时风雨暝晦，邻人见虹下其庭，化为丈夫，复少时，将儿去，亦风雨暝晦。人见二虹出其家。数年而来省母。后秦适田，见二虹于涧，畏之。须臾见丈夫，云：“是我，无所畏也。”从此乃绝。 [43]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [43]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 作州吏 - Formal title meaning 'to serve as a state official'; '作' indicates a position or role.
    • 獨在家 - Literally 'alone at home'; interpreted as 'living alone' or 'remaining at home'.
    • 丈夫 - Refers to a man, but can also be interpreted as a supernatural figure due to the unusual circumstances.
    • 儀容端正 - Describes someone with dignified and proper appearance.
    • 著绛碧袍 - Wearing a red and green robe, which is a colorful and striking attire.
    • 采色炫耀 - The colors are dazzling or eye-catching.
    • 來從之 - Comes to her, implying a visit or interaction.
    • 期於一山涧間 - Meets at a mountain stream, indicating a specific location for their meetings.
    • 不覺有人道相感接 - Without realizing it, they engage in intimate relations, suggesting a supernatural or dream-like encounter.
    • 虹見 - Refers to a rainbow appearing, which is often associated with divine or supernatural phenomena.
    • 金瓶引水 - Using a golden bottle to draw water, which is a symbol of purity and life.
    • 有身 - Becomes pregnant, a common term for becoming pregnant in Classical Chinese.
    • 多肉 - Means 'fat' or 'plump,' describing the baby's physical appearance.
    • 假还 - A temporary return home, often used to describe a visit from a traveler.
    • 納兒著瓮中 - Hides the child in a jar, indicating a desperate attempt to conceal the child.
    • 覆兒 - Covers the child, likely with the golden bottle.
    • 兒，未可得將去 - The child cannot be taken away yet, suggesting a temporary arrangement.
    • 不須作衣，我自衣之 - Does not need to make clothes; I will clothe the child, indicating a supernatural provision.
    • 绛囊以裹之 - Wraps the child in a red bag, symbolizing protection or a special status.
    • 令可時出與乳 - Allows the child to come out for feeding, indicating a periodic care routine.
    • 风雨暝晦 - Refers to dark and stormy weather, often associated with supernatural events.
    • 化為丈夫 - Transforms into a man, indicating a supernatural transformation.
    • 來省母 - Comes to visit the mother, showing a return after a period of absence.
    • 畏之 - Feels afraid of, indicating fear or apprehension.
    • 須臾 - A short time, indicating a brief period.
    • 此丈夫以金瓶與之 - This man gives her a golden bottle, suggesting a gift or a tool for survival.
    • 將兒去 - Takes the child away, indicating the final departure.
    • 亦风雨暝晦 - Also dark and stormy, reinforcing the supernatural atmosphere.
    • 人見二虹出其家 - People see two rainbows coming out of her house, indicating a visible sign of the supernatural.
    • 數年而来省母 - Comes back to visit the mother after several years, showing a recurring pattern.
    • 后秦适田 - Later, Qin goes to the fields, indicating a change in her daily routine.
    • 畏之 - Feels afraid of, indicating continued fear or caution.
    • 須臾見丈夫 - Shortly sees the man, indicating a quick appearance.
    • 云：“是我，无所畏也。” - Says, 'It is I, there is no need to be afraid,' indicating reassurance.
    • 从此乃绝 - From then on, it ceases, indicating the end of the supernatural visits.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 庐陵巴邱 - Refers to a specific place in ancient China, likely a region known for its cultural significance.
    • 陈济 - The name of the male protagonist, a state official.
    • 秦 - The name of the female protagonist, Tchen's wife.
    • 虹 - Rainbows are often associated with divine or supernatural phenomena in Chinese culture.
    • 金瓶 - Golden bottles are symbols of purity and life, often used in rituals or as gifts.
    • 绛囊 - Red bags are often used for protection or to signify special status in Chinese culture.
    • 风雨暝晦 - Dark and stormy weather is often associated with supernatural events or omens in Chinese folklore.

    Proper Nouns

    • 庐陵巴邱 - A specific place in ancient China.
    • 陈济 - The name of the male protagonist.
    • 秦 - The name of the female protagonist.
    • 虹 - Rainbows, often associated with supernatural phenomena.
    • 金瓶 - Golden bottles, symbols of purity and life.
    • 绛囊 - Red bags, often used for protection or to signify special status.
    • 风雨暝晦 - Dark and stormy weather, often associated with supernatural events.

    Chapter Seven

    In the village of Baqiu,庐陵, there lived a man named Chen Ji, who served as a state official. His wife, Qin, lived alone at home. One day, a man appeared, over ten feet tall, with dignified and proper appearance, wearing a red and green robe, whose colors were dazzling. He came to visit her. Later, they would meet regularly at a mountain stream. They engaged in intimate relations without realizing it. This continued for several years. When neighbors entered the place where these events occurred, they would see a rainbow. Qin went to the water side, and the man used a golden bottle to draw water for them to drink. Afterward, she became pregnant and gave birth to a child who looked human but was fat. When Chen Ji returned temporarily, Qin feared seeing him, so she hid the child in a jar. This man gave her a golden bottle to cover the child and said, "This child cannot be taken away yet. You do not need to make clothes; I will clothe the child." He then wrapped the child in a red bag and allowed it to come out for feeding. During dark and stormy weather, neighbors saw a rainbow descending into her courtyard, transforming into a man, who took the child away, also during dark and stormy weather. People saw two rainbows coming out of her house. After several years, the man came back to visit the mother. Later, Qin went to the fields and saw two rainbows in the stream, feeling afraid. Shortly, she saw the man, who reassured her, "It is I, there is no need to be afraid." From then on, the supernatural visits ceased. [43]

    宋元嘉初，富阳人姓王，于穷渎中作蟹断。旦往观之，见一材长二尺许，在断中。而断裂开，蟹出都尽。乃修治断，出材岸上。明往视之，材复在断中，断败如前。王又治断出材。明晨视，所见如初。王疑此材妖异，乃取内蟹笼中，挛头担归，云：“至家，当斧砍燃之。”未至家二三里，闻笼中ヘヘ动。转头顾视，见向材头变成一物，人面猴身，一身一足。语王曰：“我性嗜蟹，比曰实入水破君蟹断，入断食蟹。相负已尔，望君见恕，开笼出我。我是山神，当相佑助，并令断得大蟹。”王曰：“如此暴人，前后非一，罪自应死。”此物恳告，苦请乞放。王回顾不应。物曰：“君何姓字，我欲知之。”频问不已，王遂不答。去家转近，物曰：“既不放我，又不告我姓字，当复何计，但应就死。”王至家，炽火焚之。后寂然无复声。土俗谓之山犭，云知人姓名，则能中伤人。所以勤勤问王，欲害人自免。 [44]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [44]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 宋元嘉初 - Indicates the early years of the reign of Emperor Wu of Song (424–453 CE), providing a historical context for the narrative.
    • 富陽人姓王 - Refers to a man named Wang from Fuyang, establishing the protagonist and his origin.
    • 蟹斷 - A specialized term for a trap used to catch crabs, indicating a specific type of fishing tool.
    • 材長二尺許 - Describes a piece of wood approximately two feet long, with '許' indicating an approximation.
    • 斷敗如前 - Means the trap was damaged again, similar to the previous instance.
    • 山神 - Refers to a mountain deity, suggesting a supernatural element in the story.
    • 山犭 - A term for a mythical creature, possibly a mix of human and animal features, often associated with causing harm.
    • 知人姓名 - Knowing someone's name, which is significant in Chinese folklore for the power it confers.
    • 中傷人 - To harm or injure someone, indicating the potential danger posed by the creature.
    • 勤勤問王 - Repeatedly asking Wang, emphasizing the persistence of the creature's request.
    • 欲害人自免 - Intends to harm others to save itself, revealing the creature's malevolent nature.
    • 土俗謂之山犭 - Local folklore refers to it as 'shan yu,' a creature that can harm people if it knows their names.
    • 無復聲 - No more sounds, indicating the end of the creature's activity after being burned.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 宋元嘉初 - The early years of the reign of Emperor Wu of Song, a period of relative peace and prosperity in Southern China.
    • 富陽 - A place name, likely referring to a region in modern-day Zhejiang Province, known for its scenic beauty and historical significance.
    • 蟹斷 - A traditional fishing tool specific to crab trapping, reflecting the local fishing practices of the time.
    • 山神 - Mountain deities are common in Chinese mythology and folklore, often associated with natural phenomena and local protection.
    • 山犭 - A mythical creature, possibly derived from local legends, symbolizing the dangers of the unknown and the importance of respecting natural forces.
    • 知人姓名 - In Chinese culture, knowing someone's name is believed to give one power over them, a concept that plays a crucial role in the story.
    • 中傷人 - The idea of harm caused by supernatural beings is prevalent in Chinese folklore, often used to caution against curiosity and greed.
    • 勤勤問王 - The persistence of the creature's requests reflects the theme of temptation and the struggle between good and evil.
    • 欲害人自免 - The creature's intentions highlight the moral lesson of the story, emphasizing the consequences of succumbing to temptation.
    • 土俗謂之山犭 - Local folklore adds a layer of cultural specificity, grounding the story in a particular region's beliefs and traditions.
    • 無復聲 - The silence after the creature's demise suggests a resolution to the conflict and a return to normalcy.

    Proper Nouns

    • 宋元嘉初 - The early years of the reign of Emperor Wu of Song.
    • 富陽 - A place name, likely referring to a region in modern-day Zhejiang Province.
    • 蟹斷 - A specialized term for a crab trap.
    • 山神 - A mountain deity.
    • 山犭 - A mythical creature.
    • 土俗 - Local folklore.

    In the early years of the reign of Emperor Wu of Song, a man named Wang from Fuyang set up a crab trap in a small stream. One morning, he went to check the trap and found a piece of wood about two feet long inside it. The trap had broken, and all the crabs had escaped. He repaired the trap and removed the wood to the bank. The next morning, he checked again and found the same piece of wood back in the trap, with the trap broken again. He repaired the trap and removed the wood once more. The following morning, he saw the same situation, with the wood and the broken trap as before.

    Wang suspected that the wood was supernatural and decided to take it home. He placed it in a crab cage, tied it up, and carried it back, saying, "When I get home, I will chop it up and burn it." As he was about two or three miles from home, he heard a noise coming from the cage. He turned around and saw that the wood had transformed into a creature with a human face and a monkey's body, standing on one leg. The creature spoke to Wang, saying, "I have a fondness for crabs. Yesterday, I broke your crab trap and ate all the crabs inside. I have wronged you, and I hope you will forgive me. Open the cage and let me go. I am a mountain deity, and I will protect you and help you catch more crabs."

    Wang replied, "You are such a violent creature, and you have done this before. Your crimes deserve death." The creature pleaded earnestly, begging Wang to release it. Wang ignored it and continued walking. The creature then asked, "What is your name? I want to know it." It repeated the question, but Wang did not answer. As they got closer to his home, the creature said, "If you won't release me and won't tell me your name, what else can I do? I will just have to die."

    When Wang reached home, he set the creature on fire. Afterward, there was no more sound. Local folklore refers to this creature as a "shan yu," a creature that can harm people if it knows their names. That is why it repeatedly asked Wang for his name, intending to harm him to save itself. [44]

    刘聪伪建元元年正月，平阳地震，其崇明观陷为池，水赤如血，赤气至天，有赤龙奋迅而去。流星起于牵牛，入紫微，龙形委蛇，其光照地，落于平阳北十里。视之则肉，臭闻于平阳，长三十步，广二十七步。肉旁尝有哭声，昼夜不止。数曰聪后刘氏，产一蛇一兽，各害人而走。寻之不得。顷之，见于陨肉之旁。俄而刘氏死，哭声自绝。
    　　晋中兴后，谯郡周子文，家在晋陵。少时喜射猎，常入山，忽山岫间有一人，长五六丈，手捉弓矢，矢镝头广二尺许，白如霜雪，忽出声唤曰：“阿鼠。”子文不觉应曰：“喏。”此人便牵弓满镝向子文，子文便失魂厌伏。 [45]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [45]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 劉聰偽建元元年 - Refers to the first year of the false reign title 'Jianyuan' used by Liu Cong, a rebel leader.
    • 平陽地震 - Refers to an earthquake in Pingyang, a city in Shanxi province.
    • 崇明觀陷為池 - Describes the collapse of the Chongming Pavilion into a pool.
    • 水赤如血 - Describes the water turning red like blood.
    • 赤氣至天 - Refers to red vapor reaching the sky.
    • 有赤龍奮迅而去 - Describes a red dragon leaping and leaving.
    • 流星起於牽牛，入紫微 - Refers to a meteor starting from the constellation of Ox (Niu) and entering the Purple Palace (Ziwei), a celestial region associated with the emperor.
    • 龍形委蛇 - Describes the shape of the meteor as winding like a dragon.
    • 其光照地 - Describes the light of the meteor illuminating the ground.
    • 落於平陽北十里 - Refers to the meteor falling ten miles north of Pingyang.
    • 視之則肉，臭聞於平陽 - Describes the appearance of the meteor as meat with a foul smell spreading throughout Pingyang.
    • 長三十步，廣二十七步 - Describes the size of the meat.
    • 肉旁嘗有哭聲，昼夜不止 - Describes a continuous crying sound near the meat.
    • 數曰聰後劉氏，產一蛇一獸 - Refers to the number of times Liu Cong's wife, Liu, gave birth to a snake and an animal.
    • 各害人而走 - Describes the snake and the animal causing harm and fleeing.
    • 尋之不得 - Describes the inability to find the snake and the animal.
    • 見於陨肉之旁 - Describes the snake and the animal appearing next to the fallen meat.
    • 俄而劉氏死，哭聲自絕 - Describes Liu's death and the cessation of the crying sound.
    • 晉中興後，谯郡周子文 - Refers to the period after the revival of the Jin dynasty and Zhou Ziwén from Qiao County.
    • 少時喜射獵 - Describes Zhou Ziwén's youth and his love for hunting.
    • 忽山岫間有一人，長五六丈 - Describes a sudden appearance of a tall figure in a mountain ravine.
    • 手捉弓矢，矢镝头广二尺许 - Describes the figure holding a bow and arrow, with the arrowhead being about two feet wide.
    • 白如霜雪 - Describes the arrowhead as white like frost or snow.
    • 忽出声唤曰：“阿鼠。” - Describes the figure suddenly calling out 'Ah, rat.'
    • 子文不觉应曰：“喏。” - Describes Zhou Ziwén unconsciously responding 'Yes.'
    • 此人便牵弓满镝向子文 - Describes the figure pulling the bowstring and aiming the arrow at Zhou Ziwén.
    • 子文便失魂厌伏 - Describes Zhou Ziwén losing his soul and collapsing.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 劉聰偽建元元年 - Liu Cong used the reign title 'Jianyuan' to legitimize his rule.
    • 平陽 - A city in Shanxi province, historically significant during the Sixteen Kingdoms period.
    • 崇明觀 - A pavilion in Pingyang, possibly a religious or cultural site.
    • 牽牛 - The constellation of Ox (Niu), one of the Twenty-Eight Mansions in Chinese astronomy.
    • 紫微 - The Purple Palace (Ziwei), a celestial region associated with the emperor.
    • 晉中興 - The revival of the Jin dynasty, referring to the Eastern Jin period.
    • 谯郡 - A county in present-day Anhui province.
    • 周子文 - A historical figure from the Eastern Jin period.

    Proper Nouns

    • 劉聰 - Liu Cong, a rebel leader during the Sixteen Kingdoms period.
    • 平陽 - Pingyang, a city in Shanxi province.
    • 崇明觀 - Chongming Pavilion, a site in Pingyang.
    • 牽牛 - The constellation of Ox (Niu).
    • 紫微 - The Purple Palace (Ziwei).
    • 晉中興 - The revival of the Jin dynasty.
    • 谯郡 - Qiao County, a county in present-day Anhui province.
    • 周子文 - Zhou Ziwén, a historical figure from the Eastern Jin period.

    In the first month of the first year of the false reign title "Jianyuan" used by Liu Cong, an earthquake struck Pingyang, causing the Chongming Pavilion to collapse into a pool. The water turned red like blood, and red vapor rose to the sky, followed by a red dragon leaping and leaving. A meteor started from the constellation of Ox (Niu) and entered the Purple Palace (Ziwei), taking on a winding dragon-like shape and illuminating the ground before falling ten miles north of Pingyang. Upon inspection, it appeared as meat with a foul smell spreading throughout Pingyang, measuring thirty steps in length and twenty-seven steps in width. There was a continuous crying sound near the meat, day and night. It was said that Liu Cong's wife, Liu, gave birth to a snake and an animal, both of which caused harm and fled. They were not found. Soon after, they appeared next to the fallen meat. Shortly thereafter, Liu died, and the crying sound ceased.

    After the revival of the Jin dynasty, Zhou Ziwén from Qiao County, whose home was in Jinling, enjoyed hunting as a youth. One day, while in the mountains, he suddenly saw a tall figure in a mountain ravine, about five or six zhang tall, holding a bow and arrow with an arrowhead about two feet wide, white like frost or snow. The figure suddenly called out, "Ah, rat." Zhou Ziwén unconsciously responded, "Yes." The figure then pulled the bowstring and aimed the arrow at Zhou Ziwén, causing him to lose his soul and collapse. [45]

    晋孝武世，宣城人秦精，常入武昌山中采茗。忽遇一人，身长丈余，遍体皆毛，从山北来。精见之，大怖，自谓必死。毛人径牵其臂，将至山曲，入大丛茗处，放之便去。精因采茗。须臾复来，乃探怀中二十枚橘与精，甘美异常。精甚怪，负茗而归。
    　　会稽盛逸，常晨兴，路未有行人，见门外柳树上有一人，长二尺，衣朱衣朱冠，俯以舌舐叶上露。良久，忽见逸，神意惊遽，即隐不见。
    　　宋永初三年，谢南康家婢，行逢一黑狗，语婢云：“汝看我背后。”婢举头，见一人长三尺，有两头。婢惊怕返走，人、狗亦随婢后，至家庭中，举家避走。婢问狗：“汝来何为”狗云：“欲吃食尔。”于是婢为设食。并食食讫，两头人出。婢因谓狗曰：“人已去矣。”狗曰：“正已复来。”良久乃没。不知所在。后家人死丧殆尽。 [46]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [46]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 身长丈余，遍体皆毛 - Describes a tall, hairy figure, which is likely a supernatural being.
    • 甘美异常 - Indicates that the oranges are unusually sweet and delicious.
    • 良久乃没 - Describes the figure disappearing after a while.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 晋孝武世 - Refers to the reign of Emperor Xiaowu of Jin, a significant period in Chinese history.
    • 武昌山 - Refers to the Wuchang Mountains, a specific location in China.
    • 宋永初三年 - Refers to the year 422 AD during the Song dynasty, a specific historical period.
    • 谢南康 - Refers to a historical figure, Xie Nankang, who was a prominent official during the Eastern Jin dynasty.
    • 会稽 - Refers to Kuaiji, a region in present-day Zhejiang Province, China.
    • 两头人 - Refers to a mythical or supernatural being with two heads, which is a common motif in Chinese folklore.
    • 黑狗 - Refers to a black dog, which in Chinese culture can have various symbolic meanings, including protection and guidance.
    • 饭牛牧羊 - Refers to the act of feeding cattle and herding sheep, indicating humble occupations.

    Proper Nouns

    • 晋孝武世 - The reign of Emperor Xiaowu of Jin.
    • 宣城 - A city in present-day Anhui Province, China.
    • 武昌山 - The Wuchang Mountains.
    • 宋永初三年 - The year 422 AD during the Song dynasty.
    • 谢南康 - Xie Nankang, a historical figure.
    • 会稽 - Kuaiji, a region in present-day Zhejiang Province, China.
    • 两头人 - A mythical or supernatural being with two heads.
    • 黑狗 - A black dog.
    • 饭牛牧羊 - Feeding cattle and herding sheep.

    During the reign of Emperor Xiaowu of Jin, a man named Qin Jing from Xuancheng often went to the Wuchang Mountains to pick tea leaves. One day, he suddenly encountered a tall, hairy figure, about ten feet tall, with hair covering his entire body, coming from the north of the mountain. Qin Jing was terrified and thought he would surely die. The hairy figure grabbed his arm and led him to a bend in the mountain, into a dense tea bush, and then left. Qin Jing continued to pick tea leaves. Shortly after, the figure returned and gave him twenty oranges from his pocket, which were unusually sweet and delicious. Qin Jing was very surprised and carried the tea leaves back home.

    In the early morning, Sheng Yi from Kuaiji often rose early and found no one on the road. Suddenly, he saw a person dressed in red clothing and a red hat, licking dew from the leaves on a willow tree outside his house. After a while, he suddenly saw Sheng Yi and became startled, then disappeared immediately.

    In the year 422 AD during the Song dynasty, a servant girl in the household of Xie Nankang was walking and encountered a black dog. The dog told her, "Look at what is behind me." She looked up and saw a person three feet tall with two heads. The servant girl was terrified and ran away, and the person and the dog followed her to her home. The entire family fled. The servant girl asked the dog, "What are you doing here?" The dog replied, "I just want to eat." The servant girl then prepared food for the dog. After they had eaten, the two-headed person appeared. The servant girl told the dog, "The person has already left." The dog said, "He is about to return." After a while, the figure disappeared. No one knew where he went. Later, the family suffered almost complete deaths. [46]

    宋襄城李颐，其父为人不信妖邪。有一宅，由来凶不可居，居者辄死。父便买居之。多年安居，子孙昌炽。为二千石，当徙家之官，临去，请会内外亲戚。酒食既行，父乃言曰：“天下竟有吉凶否此宅由来言凶，自吾居之，多年安吉，乃得迁官，鬼为何在自今已后，便为吉宅。居者住止，心无所嫌也。”语讫如厕，须臾，见壁中有一物，如卷席大，高五尺许，正白。便还取刀中之，中断，化为两人。复横斫之，又成四人。便夺取，反斫杀李。持至坐上，斫杀其子弟。凡姓李者必死，惟异姓无他。颐尚幼，在抱。家内知变，乳母抱出后门，藏他家，止其一身获免。颐字景真，位至湘东太守。 [47]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [47]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 由来凶不可居 - Fixed phrase meaning 'originally it was a place of misfortune and could not be inhabited.'
    • 居者辄死 - Literal translation of 'those who lived there died,' but it means 'whenever someone lived there, they died.'
    • 多年安居 - Literal translation of 'lived peacefully for many years.'
    • 子孙昌炽 - Literal translation of 'descendants thrived,' meaning 'the descendants flourished.'
    • 为二千石 - A title referring to a high-ranking official, specifically a governor or prefect.
    • 当徙家之官 - Literal translation of 'when he was about to move his family to serve as an official.'
    • 请会内外亲戚 - Literal translation of 'requested to gather relatives inside and outside the family.'
    • 酒食既行 - Literal translation of 'after the wine and food were served.'
    • 天下竟有吉凶否 - Literal translation of 'Is there really good and evil in the world?'
    • 此宅由来言凶 - Literal translation of 'This house has always been said to be unlucky.'
    • 自吾居之，多年安吉 - Literal translation of 'Since I moved in, it has been peaceful and prosperous for many years.'
    • 乃得迁官 - Literal translation of 'and I have been promoted.'
    • 鬼为何在 - Literal translation of 'where are the ghosts?'
    • 自今已后，便为吉宅 - Literal translation of 'from now on, it will be a lucky house.'
    • 居者住止，心无所嫌也 - Literal translation of 'those who live here will have no doubts.'
    • 语讫如厕 - Literal translation of 'after finishing speaking, he went to the toilet.'
    • 须臾 - Literal translation of 'in a moment.'
    • 见壁中有一物，如卷席大，高五尺许 - Literal translation of 'he saw something in the wall, about the size of a rolled mat, about five feet tall.'
    • 正白 - Literal translation of 'pure white.'
    • 便还取刀中之 - Literal translation of 'he immediately took a knife and stabbed it.'
    • 中断，化为两人 - Literal translation of 'it split in half, transforming into two people.'
    • 复横斫之 - Literal translation of 'he then cut it horizontally.'
    • 又成四人 - Literal translation of 'and it turned into four people.'
    • 便夺取，反斫杀李 - Literal translation of 'he seized them and struck Li back, killing him.'
    • 持至坐上，斫杀其子弟 - Literal translation of 'carried them to the seat and killed his children.'
    • 凡姓李者必死，惟异姓无他 - Literal translation of 'all who were surnamed Li would die, while others would not be harmed.'
    • 颐尚幼，在抱 - Literal translation of 'Yi was still young, being held in someone's arms.'
    • 家内知变 - Literal translation of 'the family knew of the change.'
    • 乳母抱出后门，藏他家 - Literal translation of 'the wet nurse carried him out the back door and hid him in another house.'
    • 止其一身获免 - Literal translation of 'only he escaped.'
    • 颐字景真，位至湘东太守 - Literal translation of 'Yi, whose courtesy name was Jingzhen, became the governor of Xiangdong.'

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 宋襄城 - A place name, likely referring to a county in the Song region.
    • 二千石 - A title for a high-ranking official, equivalent to a governor or prefect.
    • 湘东太守 - The title of a governor in the Xiangdong region.
    • 鬼 - Refers to supernatural beings, often associated with bad luck or malevolent forces.
    • 凶宅 - A house believed to be cursed or haunted.
    • 吉宅 - A house believed to be lucky or blessed.

    Proper Nouns

    • 宋襄城 - A place name, likely referring to a county in the Song region.
    • 李颐 - The name of the person.
    • 二千石 - A title for a high-ranking official.
    • 湘东太守 - The title of a governor in the Xiangdong region.
    • 景真 - The courtesy name of Li Yi.

    Li Yi of Song County, whose father did not believe in ghosts and spirits, lived in a house that was originally considered so unlucky that anyone who lived there died. His father bought and moved into the house. For many years, they lived peacefully, and his descendants flourished. Li Yi became a high-ranking official, and when he was about to move his family to serve as an official, he gathered his relatives inside and outside the family. After the wine and food were served, his father said, "Is there really good and evil in the world? This house has always been said to be unlucky, but since I moved in, it has been peaceful and prosperous for many years, and I have been promoted. Where are the ghosts? From now on, it will be a lucky house. Those who live here will have no doubts." After finishing speaking, he went to the toilet. In a moment, he saw something in the wall, about the size of a rolled mat, about five feet tall, pure white. He immediately took a knife and stabbed it. It split in half, transforming into two people. He then cut it horizontally, and it turned into four people. He seized them and struck Li back, killing him. He carried them to the seat and killed his children. All who were surnamed Li would die, while others would not be harmed. Li Yi was still young, being held in someone's arms. The family knew of the change. The wet nurse carried him out the back door and hid him in another house, and only he escaped. Li Yi, whose courtesy name was Jingzhen, became the governor of Xiangdong. [47]

    宋王仲文为河南郡主簿，居缑氏县北。得休，因晚行泽中。见车后有白狗，仲文甚爱之。欲取之，忽变形如人，状似方相，目赤如火，磋牙吐舌，甚可憎恶。仲文大怖，与奴共击之，不胜而走。告家人，合十余人，持刀捉火，自来视之，不知所在。月余，仲文忽复见之。与奴并走，未到家，伏地俱死。 [48]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [48]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 得休 - Classical phrase meaning 'to take a rest' or 'to be on leave.'
    • 因晚行泽中 - Means 'to go walking in the wetlands at dusk,' with '因' indicating a casual or spontaneous action.
    • 欲取之 - Means 'to want to take it,' referring to the white dog.
    • 忽变 - Means 'suddenly transformed,' with '忽' indicating suddenness.
    • 状似方相 - Means 'in appearance like the Fangxiang,' referring to a mythical figure often depicted as a mask or a ghost.
    • 目赤如火 - Means 'eyes red like fire,' describing intense redness.
    • 磋牙吐舌 - Means 'showing teeth and sticking out the tongue,' indicating a threatening or grotesque appearance.
    • 甚可憎恶 - Means 'very repulsive' or 'highly detestable.'
    • 与奴共击之 - Means 'together with the servant to attack it,' with '与' indicating joint action.
    • 不胜而走 - Means 'unable to withstand and fled,' with '不胜' indicating inability to overcome.
    • 告家人 - Means 'to inform the family,' with '告' indicating notification.
    • 合十余人 - Means 'gathered over ten people,' with '合' indicating gathering or assembling.
    • 持刀捉火 - Means 'holding knives and holding fire,' with '捉火' meaning to carry or hold a light source.
    • 自来视之 - Means 'came to see it,' with '自来' indicating self-initiated action.
    • 不知所在 - Means 'did not know where it was,' with '所在' indicating location.
    • 月余 - Means 'more than a month,' with '余' indicating excess or additional time.
    • 忽复见之 - Means 'suddenly saw it again,' with '忽' indicating suddenness.
    • 与奴并走 - Means 'with the servant ran together,' with '并' indicating together or jointly.
    • 未到家 - Means 'not yet arrived at home,' with '未' indicating negation of arrival.
    • 伏地俱死 - Means 'fell to the ground and both died,' with '俱' indicating together or jointly.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 宋王仲文 - A historical figure from the Song dynasty, likely a notable person or official.
    • 河南郡 - A specific administrative division.
    • 缑氏县 - A specific county in Henan, providing a geographical context.
    • 白狗 - A common animal, but in this context, it transforms into a supernatural entity.
    • 方相 - A mythical figure often associated with exorcism and protection against evil spirits, typically depicted with a mask and a fearsome appearance.

    Proper Nouns

    • 宋王仲文 - A historical figure.
    • 河南郡 - A specific administrative division.
    • 缑氏县 - A specific county.
    • 方相 - A mythical figure.

    During the reign of Emperor Song, Wang Zhongwen served as a minor official in the Henan Prefecture, residing north of Gou County. While on leave, he went walking in the wetlands at dusk. He saw a white dog behind his carriage and greatly loved it. He wanted to take it, but suddenly it transformed into a human-like form, resembling the Fangxiang, with eyes red like fire, showing teeth and sticking out its tongue, and was very repulsive. Wang Zhongwen was greatly terrified and, together with his servant, attacked it, but they could not withstand it and fled. He informed his family, and over ten people gathered, holding knives and carrying lights, and came to see it, but they did not know where it was. More than a month later, Wang Zhongwen suddenly saw it again. With his servant, he ran together, but before reaching home, they both fell to the ground and died. [48]

    ●卷八
    　　王机为广州刺史，人厕，忽见二人著乌衣，与机相捍。良久擒之，得二物如乌鸭。以问鲍靓，靓曰：“此物不祥。”机焚之，径飞上天。寻诛死。
    　　晋义熙中，乌伤葛辉夫，在妇家宿。三更后，有两人把火至阶前。疑是凶人，往打之。欲下杖。悉变成蝴蝶，缤纷飞散，有冲辉夫腋下。便倒地，少时死。
    　　诸葛长民富贵后，常一月中，辄十数夜眠中惊起跳踉，如与人相打。毛修之尝与同宿，见之惊愕，问其故，答曰：“正见一物，甚黑而有毛，脚不分明，奇健，非我无以制之也。”后来转数屋中柱及椽桷间，悉见有蛇头。令人以刃悬斫，应刃隐藏。去辄复出。又捣衣杵相与语，如人声，不可解。于壁见有巨手，长七八尺，臂大数围。令斫之，忽然不见。未几伏诛。 [49]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [49]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 忽見二人著烏衣 - Sudden encounter with two people wearing black clothes; '烏衣' often refers to supernatural beings or ghosts in classical literature.
    • 與機相捍 - Fought with Wáng Jī; '捍' means to resist or fight against someone.
    • 得二物如烏鴨 - Obtained two objects like black ducks; '如烏鴨' is a metaphor for something dark and unidentifiable.
    • 焚之，徑飛上天 - Burned them, and they flew straight up into the sky; '徑飛上天' suggests a supernatural or otherworldly event.
    • 三更後 - After the third watch of the night; '三更' refers to the third watch of the night, around midnight.
    • 把火至階前 - Carried a torch to the front of the steps; '把火' means to carry a torch or hold a light.
    • 疑是凶人 - Suspected them to be evil people; '凶人' refers to evil or malevolent individuals.
    • 悉變成蝴蝶 - All transformed into butterflies; '悉變' means to completely transform or change.
    • 缤纷飛散 - Flew away in a colorful and scattered manner; '缤纷' describes a colorful and scattered appearance.
    • 奇健 - Strange and powerful; '奇健' describes something that is both unusual and strong.
    • 非我無以制之 - There was no one else who could control it; '非我無以制之' emphasizes the speaker's unique ability to control the situation.
    • 轉數屋中柱及椽桷間 - Frequently appeared in the pillars and rafters of the house; '轉數' means to frequently appear or occur.
    • 悉見有蛇頭 - All saw snake heads; '悉見' means to all see or observe.
    • 應刃隐藏 - Disappeared upon being cut; '應刃隐藏' describes something that vanishes when struck by a blade.
    • 去辄复出 - Left and immediately reappeared; '去辄复出' describes a recurring pattern of disappearance and reappearance.
    • 搗衣杵相与语 - The washing sticks talked to each other; '搗衣杵' refers to tools used for pounding clothes, and '相与语' means to talk to each other.
    • 巨手 - Giant hands; '巨' means large or enormous.
    • 臂大数围 - Arms as thick as several encirclements; '臂大数围' describes arms that are extremely thick.
    • 未几伏诛 - Not long after, he was executed; '未几' means not long after, and '伏诛' means to be executed or punished.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 王机 - Wáng Jī, a historical figure who served as the governor of Guangzhou during the Eastern Jin Dynasty.
    • 鲍靓 - Bào Liàng, a famous alchemist and scholar of the Eastern Jin Dynasty.
    • 晋义熙中 - During the Yìxī period of the Eastern Jin Dynasty (405-418 AD).
    • 乌伤 - Wūshāng, an ancient county in present-day Zhejiang Province.
    • 诸葛长民 - Zhūgě Chángmín, a military leader and politician during the Eastern Jin Dynasty.
    • 毛修之 - Máo Xiūzhī, a contemporary of Zhūgě Chángmín and a military leader during the Eastern Jin Dynasty.

    Proper Nouns

    • 王机 - Wáng Jī
    • 鲍靓 - Bào Liàng
    • 诸葛长民 - Zhūgě Chángmín
    • 毛修之 - Máo Xiūzhī

    ● Chapter Eight
    　　Wáng Jī, the governor of Guangzhou, once entered the latrine and suddenly saw two people dressed in black clothing fighting with him. After a long struggle, he captured them and obtained two objects that resembled black ducks. He asked Bào Liàng about them, and Bào Liàng said, "These things are ominous." Wáng Jī burned them, and they flew straight up into the sky. Soon after, he was executed.
    　　During the Yìxī period of the Eastern Jin Dynasty, a man named Gě Huīfū from Wūshāng stayed at his wife's home. After the third watch of the night, two people carrying torches appeared at the front of the steps. Suspecting them to be evil people, Gě Huīfū went to attack them. As he was about to strike, they all transformed into butterflies, flying away in a colorful and scattered manner. One of the butterflies flew into Gě Huīfū's armpit, causing him to fall to the ground and die shortly after.
    　　After Zhūgě Chángmín became wealthy and powerful, he often experienced several nights each month where he would wake up in a panic, jumping around as if fighting with someone. Máo Xiūzhī, who had once stayed with him, witnessed this and was startled. He asked Zhūgě Chángmín why this happened, and Zhūgě Chángmín replied, "I just saw something very black with fur, whose feet were not clearly visible, and it was strangely powerful. No one else could control it." Later, strange occurrences became frequent in the house, with snake heads appearing in the pillars and rafters. When someone tried to cut them with a blade, they would disappear but reappear immediately. The washing sticks also seemed to talk to each other in a human-like voice, though the words were incomprehensible. Gě Huīfū also saw giant hands on the walls, each about seven or eight feet long and with arms as thick as several encirclements. When he tried to cut them, they suddenly vanished. Not long after, Zhūgě Chángmín was executed. [49]

    新野庾谨，母病，兄弟三人，悉在侍疾。白曰常燃火，忽见帐带自卷自舒，如此数四。须臾间，床前闻狗声异常。举家共视，了不见狗，见一死人头在地，头犹有发，两眼尚动，甚可憎恶。其家怖惧。乃不持出门，即于后园中瘗之。明曰往视，乃出土上，两眼犹尔，即又埋之。后曰复出，乃以砖头合埋之，遂不复出。他曰，其母便亡。
    　　王绥字彦猷，其家夜中，梁上无故有人头堕于床，而流血滂沱。俄拜荆州刺史，坐父愉之谋，与弟纳并被诛。
    　　晋永嘉五年，张荣为高平戍逻主。时曹嶷贼寇离乱，人民皆坞垒自保固。见山中火起，飞埃绝焰十余丈，树颠火炎，响动山谷。又闻人马铠甲声，谓嶷贼上，人皆惶恐，并戒严出，将欲击之。乃引骑到山下，无有人，但见碎火来晒人，袍铠马毛鬣皆烧。于是军人走还。明曰往视，山中无燃火处，见髑髅百头，布散山中。 [50]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [50]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 悉在侍疾 - Means 'all were attending to the illness,' indicating that all three brothers were caring for their mother.
    • 忽见帐带自卷自舒 - Means 'suddenly saw the curtain rope rolling up and down by itself,' describing an inexplicable and eerie event.
    • 两眼尚动 - Means 'the eyes were still moving,' emphasizing the unsettling nature of the apparition.
    • 他曰 - Means 'on another day,' used to indicate a subsequent day.
    • 无故 - Means 'without reason' or 'unexpectedly,' describing the sudden appearance of the head.
    • 流血滂沱 - Means 'blood flowed profusely,' describing the intensity of the bloodshed.
    • 俄拜 - Means 'shortly after being appointed,' referring to the rapid rise in Weng Sui's position.
    • 坐父愉之谋 - Means 'due to his father's plot,' explaining the reason for Weng Sui's execution.
    • 但见碎火来晒人 - Means 'only saw sparks flying towards people,' describing the eerie and dangerous phenomenon.
    • 布散山中 - Means 'scattered throughout the mountain,' indicating the widespread nature of the discovery.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 新野庾谨 - Refers to a person named Yu Jin from Xinye, a place in Henan Province during the Eastern Jin period.
    • 王绥 - Refers to a person named Wang Sui, who was a prominent official during the Eastern Jin period.
    • 永嘉五年 - Refers to the fifth year of the Yongjia era, which corresponds to 311 AD.
    • 高平戍逻主 - Refers to a military officer stationed in Gaoping, a place in Shandong Province.
    • 曹嶷贼寇 - Refers to a bandit leader named Cao Yi who was active in the region.
    • 张荣 - Refers to a person named Zhang Rong, who was a military officer during the Western Jin period.

    Syntactic Ambiguities
