    • The phrase '忽见帐带自卷自舒' describes a sequence of actions that are unusual and unexplained, requiring careful translation to convey the eerie atmosphere.
    • The phrase '床前闻狗声异常' describes a sudden and unusual sound, which adds to the tension and mystery.
    • The phrase '乃以砖头合埋之' describes the action of burying the head with bricks, which is a specific and detailed action that needs to be clearly conveyed.
    • The phrase '山中火起，飞埃绝焰十余丈' describes a dramatic and intense fire scene, which requires vivid and descriptive translation.
    • The phrase '又闻人马铠甲声' describes a sound that is initially interpreted as a threat but turns out to be something else, adding to the narrative's suspense.

    Proper Nouns

    • 新野庾谨 - Yu Jin of Xinye.
    • 王绥 - Wang Sui.
    • 永嘉五年 - The fifth year of the Yongjia era (311 AD).
    • 高平戍逻主 - The military officer of Gaoping.
    • 曹嶷 - Cao Yi.
    • 张荣 - Zhang Rong.

    Yu Jin of Xinye, whose mother was ill, had three brothers who were all attending to her. One night, they lit a fire, and suddenly saw the curtain rope rolling up and down by itself, doing so several times. Soon after, they heard an unusual dog sound at the foot of the bed. The whole family looked around, but saw no dog, only a dead human head on the ground, with hair still on it and the eyes moving, which was extremely terrifying. The family was filled with fear. They did not take the head out of the house but buried it immediately in the back garden. The next day, when they went to check, the head was found outside the ground, with its eyes still moving. They buried it again. Later that day, the head reappeared, so they buried it with bricks, and it never came out again. On another day, Yu Jin's mother passed away.

    Wang Sui, also known as Yan You, experienced a strange event at night when a human head fell from the beam onto the bed, and blood flowed profusely. Shortly after, he was appointed as the Governor of Jingzhou, but due to his father's plot, he and his brother Na were both executed.

    In the fifth year of the Yongjia era (311 AD), Zhang Rong was the military officer of Gaoping. At that time, the bandit leader Cao Yi was causing chaos, and the people were fortifying themselves in strongholds for protection. They saw a fire start in the mountains, with smoke and flames reaching over ten zhang high, and the tops of the trees were ablaze, echoing through the valleys. They also heard the sounds of people and horses in armor, thinking it was Cao Yi's forces, and everyone was filled with fear. They prepared for battle and marched out, intending to attack. When they reached the foot of the mountain, there was no one there, but they saw sparks flying towards them, setting fire to their armor and the hair of their horses. The soldiers fled back in panic. The next day, when they went to investigate, they found no signs of fire in the mountains, but saw a hundred skulls scattered throughout the area. [50]

    新野赵贞家，园中种葱，未经摘拔。忽一曰，尽缩入地。后经岁余，贞之兄弟相次分散。
    　　吴聂友，字文悌，豫章新淦人。少时贫贱，常好射猎。夜照见一白鹿，射中之。明寻踪，血既尽，不知所在。且已饥困，便卧一梓树下。仰见射箭著树枝上，视之，乃是昨所射箭。怪其如此，于是还家，赍粮，率子弟，持斧以伐之。树微有血，遂裁截为板二枚，牵著陂塘中。板常沉没，然时复浮出。出，家辄有吉庆。每欲迎宾客，常乘此板。忽于中流欲没，客大惧，友呵之，还复浮出。仕宦大如愿，位至丹阳太守。在郡经年，板忽随至石头。外司白云：“涛中板入石头来。”友惊曰：“板来，必有意。”即解职归家。下船便闭户，二板挟两边，一曰即至豫章。尔后板出，便反为凶祸，家大轲。今新淦北二十里余，曰封溪，有聂友截梓树板，涛柯处。有梓树，今犹存。乃聂友向曰所截，枝叶皆向下生。 [51]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [51]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 忽一曰 - Classical way of saying 'one day,' with a slight archaic tone.
    • 盡縮入地 - Literally means 'all shrink into the ground,' describing an unusual phenomenon.
    • 相次分散 - Means 'gradually disperse' or 'separate one by one,' often used to describe a gradual process.
    • 夜照 - Means 'night illumination' or 'night vision,' possibly referring to a supernatural event.
    • 明尋踪 - Means 'to search for traces in the morning,' indicating a continuation of the previous night's activity.
    • 血既尽 - Means 'the blood has all dried up,' suggesting a complete absence of blood.
    • 赍粮 - Means 'to carry food,' often used in the context of preparing for a journey or task.
    • 裁截为板 - Means 'to cut into boards,' describing the action of cutting the tree into pieces.
    • 牵著陂塘中 - Means 'to drag into a pond or lake,' indicating the placement of the boards in water.
    • 家辄有吉庆 - Means 'the family always has good news,' suggesting a positive outcome associated with the boards.
    • 乘此板 - Means 'to ride on these boards,' indicating a magical or unusual use of the boards.
    • 仕宦大如愿 - Means 'official career very much as desired,' describing a successful career.
    • 下船便闭户 - Means 'to close the door immediately after getting off the boat,' indicating a sudden and decisive action.
    • 涛中板入石头来 - Means 'the boards from the waves entered the stone,' describing a supernatural event.
    • 解职归家 - Means 'to resign and return home,' indicating a decision to leave public service.
    • 饭牛牧羊 - Means 'to feed cattle and herd sheep,' describing simple agricultural work.
    • 枝叶皆向下生 - Means 'the branches and leaves all grow downwards,' describing an unusual growth pattern of the tree.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 新野赵贞家 - Refers to a family in Xinye, a place name in ancient China.
    • 吴聂友 - Refers to Wu Nieyou, a historical figure from the Eastern Han period.
    • 豫章新淦 - Refers to Xingan, a county in present-day Jiangxi Province.
    • 丹阳太守 - Refers to the position of governor of DanYang, a region in ancient China.
    • 石头 - Refers to Stone City, an ancient name for Nanjing, a city in present-day Jiangxi Province.
    • 封溪 - Refers to a specific location near Xingan, described as a river or stream.

    Syntactic Ambiguities

    • 忽一曰，尽缩入地 - This phrase describes an unusual event where the onions suddenly shrink into the ground, which can be interpreted as a supernatural occurrence.
    • 后经岁余，贞之兄弟相次分散 - This sentence describes a gradual process of separation among S.

    The Scao

    t [51]

    ●卷九
    　　钱塘人姓杜，船行时大雪曰暮，有女子素衣来岸上。杜曰：“何不入船”遂相调戏。杜合船载之。后成白鹭，飞去。杜恶之，便病死。
    　　丹阳人沈宗，在县治下，以卜为业。义熙中，左将军檀侯镇姑孰，好猎，以格虎为事。忽有一人，著皮衣裤，乘马，从一人，亦著皮裤，以纸裹十余钱，来诣宗卜，云：“西去觅食好，东去觅食好”宗为作卦，卦成，告之：“东向吉，西向不利。”因就宗乞饮，内口著瓯中，状如牛饮。既出，东行百余步，从者及马皆化为虎。自此以后，虎暴非常。 [52]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [52]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 卷九 - Chapter Nine, a standard way of referencing a chapter in a classical text.
    • 錢塘人 - A person from Qiantang, where Qiantang is a historical name for Hangzhou, Zhejiang province.
    • 大雪曰暮 - Heavy snow at dusk, a descriptive phrase indicating the time and weather conditions.
    • 素衣 - Wearing white clothes, often associated with mourning or purity.
    • 何不入船 - Why not come aboard the ship? a casual invitation.
    • 遂相調戲 - They began to flirt with each other, a colloquial expression.
    • 合船載之 - He loaded her onto the boat, a straightforward action.
    • 後成白鹭 - Later turned into a white heron, a magical transformation.
    • 飛去 - Flew away, indicating the heron's departure.
    • 惡之 - Hated it, expressing strong negative emotion.
    • 便病死 - Soon fell ill and died, a concise description of the outcome.
    • 丹陽人 - A person from Danyang, where Danyang is a historical region in Jiangsu province.
    • 以卜為業 - Made a living by divination, indicating his profession.
    • 義熙中 - During the Yixi period, referring to the reign of Emperor Xiaowu of Liu Song (405-419 CE).
    • 左將軍檀侯 - Left General Tan Hou, the title and name of a military official.
    • 鎮姑孰 - Governed Gusu, where Gusu is an ancient name for Zhenjiang, Jiangsu province.
    • 好獵 - Loved hunting, indicating his passion.
    • 以格虎為事 - Made tiger hunting his business, emphasizing his dedication to the activity.
    • 忽有一人 - Suddenly, there was a man, introducing a new character.
    • 著皮衣褲 - Wearing fur clothing, indicating his attire.
    • 從一人 - Accompanied by another person, indicating his entourage.
    • 亦著皮褲 - Also wearing fur pants, describing the attire of the accompanying person.
    • 以紙裹十餘錢 - Wrapped about ten coins in paper, indicating his modesty.
    • 來詣宗卜 - Came to Shen Zong for divination, indicating his purpose.
    • 雲：“西去觅食好，东去觅食好” - Said, 'It's good to go west for food, it's good to go east for food,' a cryptic statement.
    • 宗為作卦 - Shen Zong cast a hexagram, indicating the divination process.
    • 卦成，告之 - When the hexagram was cast, he told him, indicating the result.
    • 東向吉，西向不利 - Going east is auspicious, going west is inauspicious, the divination result.
    • 因就宗乞飲 - He then asked Shen Zong for a drink, a polite request.
    • 内口著瓯中 - Put his mouth into the bowl, a vivid description.
    • 狀如牛飲 - Drank like a cow, a metaphor for drinking deeply.
    • 既出，東行百余步 - Afterwards, he walked east for over a hundred steps, indicating his direction.
    • 從者及馬皆化為虎 - His attendant and horse all turned into tigers, a magical transformation.
    • 自此以后，虎暴非常 - From then on, tigers became extremely violent, indicating a change in behavior.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 錢塘 - A historical name for Hangzhou, Zhejiang province.
    • 丹陽 - A historical region in Jiangsu province.
    • 義熙 - The reign title of Emperor Xiaowu of Liu Song (405-419 CE).
    • 左將軍 - A military rank in the imperial court.
    • 檀侯 - The name of a military official.
    • 姑孰 - An ancient name for Zhenjiang, Jiangsu province.
    • 格虎 - Tiger hunting, a popular sport among the elite.
    • 卜 - Divination, a practice used to predict the future.
    • 卦 - Hexagrams, used in divination to interpret outcomes.
    • 白鹭 - A white heron, often associated with purity and grace.

    Proper Nouns

    • 卷九 - Chapter Nine.
    • 錢塘 - Qiantang (historical name for Hangzhou).
    • 丹陽 - Danyang (historical region in Jiangsu).
    • 義熙 - Yixi (reign title of Emperor Xiaowu of Liu Song).
    • 左將軍 - Left General.
    • 檀侯 - Tan Hou (military official).
    • 姑孰 - Gusu (ancient name for Zhenjiang).
    • 沈宗 - Shen Zong (person's name).
    • 白鹭 - White heron.

    Chapter Nine

    A man from Qiantang, whose surname was Du, was sailing when it was snowing heavily at dusk. A woman dressed in white came to the shore. Du said, "Why don't you come aboard the ship?" They began to flirt with each other. Du loaded her onto the boat. Later, she turned into a white heron and flew away. Du hated it and soon fell ill and died.

    In Danyang, there was a man named Shen Zong, who made a living by divination. During the Yixi period, Left General Tan Hou governed Gusu and loved hunting, making tiger hunting his business. Suddenly, there was a man wearing fur clothing, riding a horse, accompanied by another person also wearing fur pants, wrapped about ten coins in paper, and came to Shen Zong for divination. He said, "It's good to go west for food, it's good to go east for food." Shen Zong cast a hexagram, and when the hexagram was cast, he told him, "Going east is auspicious, going west is inauspicious." The man then asked Shen Zong for a drink, put his mouth into the bowl, and drank like a cow. Afterwards, he walked east for over a hundred steps, and his attendant and horse all turned into tigers. From then on, tigers became extremely violent. [52]

    晋升平中，有人入山射鹿，忽堕一坎，然深绝。内有数头熊子。须臾，有一大熊来，瞪视此人。人谓必以害己。良久，出藏果，分与诸子。末后作一分，置此人前。此人饥甚，于是冒死取啖之。既而转相狎习。熊母每旦出，觅果食还，辄分此人。赖以延命。熊子后大，其母一一负之而出。子既尽，人分死坎中，穷无出路。熊母寻复还入，坐人边。人解其意，便抱熊足，于是跃出。竟得无他。
    　　淮南陈氏，于田中种豆，忽见二女子，姿色甚美，著紫缬襦，青裙，天雨而衣不湿。其壁先挂一铜镜，镜中见二鹿，遂以刀斫获之，以为脯。 [53]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [53]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 晋升平中 - A specific period in Chinese history, the early 4th century during the Eastern Jin dynasty.
    • 忽堕一坎 - Literally means 'suddenly fell into a pit,' indicating an unexpected event.
    • 内有数头熊子 - Means 'inside there were several bear cubs,' describing the contents of the pit.
    • 须臾 - Means 'a short time later' or 'soon after,' indicating a brief interval.
    • 瞪视此人 - Means 'stared at this person,' describing the bear's reaction.
    • 出藏果 - Means 'brought out hidden fruit,' indicating the bear's action of retrieving food.
    • 分与诸子 - Means 'divided it among the cubs,' showing the bear's behavior of sharing.
    • 末后作一分 - Means 'finally made a portion,' indicating the bear's final action of creating a share.
    • 置此人前 - Means 'placed it in front of this person,' showing the bear's gesture of offering food.
    • 此人饥甚 - Means 'this person was very hungry,' describing the human's state.
    • 于是冒死取啖之 - Means 'so he took the risk and ate it,' indicating the human's desperate action.
    • 转相狎习 - Means 'gradually became familiar and accustomed,' showing the process of the human and bear becoming comfortable with each other.
    • 熊母每旦出，觅果食还 - Means 'the bear mother would go out every morning to find fruit and return,' describing her daily routine.
    • 辄分此人 - Means 'always divided it for this person,' showing her consistent behavior of sharing.
    • 赖以延命 - Means 'relied on this to sustain life,' indicating the human's dependence on the bear.
    • 熊子后大 - Means 'the bear cubs grew up,' showing the passage of time.
    • 其母一一负之而出 - Means 'the mother carried each one out,' describing her actions as the cubs grew.
    • 子既尽 - Means 'when the cubs were all gone,' indicating the end of the cubs' presence.
    • 人分死坎中，穷无出路 - Means 'the person was trapped in the pit, with no way out,' showing the human's dire situation.
    • 熊母寻复还入，坐人边 - Means 'the bear mother soon returned and sat beside the person,' showing her return and proximity.
    • 人解其意，便抱熊足，于是跃出 - Means 'the person understood her intention, so he hugged the bear's foot, and they jumped out,' describing the final act of escape.
    • 竟得无他 - Means 'and in the end, there was no harm,' indicating a fortunate outcome.
    • 淮南陈氏 - Refers to a family named Chen from the region of Huainan.
    • 于田中种豆 - Means 'while farming beans in the fields,' describing the setting.
    • 忽见二女子 - Means 'suddenly saw two women,' indicating an unexpected encounter.
    • 姿色甚美 - Means 'with very beautiful appearance,' describing the women's beauty.
    • 著紫缬襦，青裙 - Means 'wearing purple and green skirts,' describing their attire.
    • 天雨而衣不湿 - Means 'it rained, but their clothes did not get wet,' indicating an unusual phenomenon.
    • 其壁先挂一铜镜 - Means 'there was a copper mirror hanging on the wall,' describing the setting.
    • 镜中见二鹿 - Means 'they saw two deer in the mirror,' indicating a strange vision.
    • 遂以刀斫获之，以为脯 - Means 'they then used a knife to cut them and made them into jerky,' describing the subsequent action.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 晋升平中 - A specific period in Chinese history, the early 4th century during the Eastern Jin dynasty.
    • 淮南 - A historical region in present-day Anhui Province, China.
    • 铜镜 - Refers to a type of mirror made of copper, which was common in ancient China.
    • 鹿 - Symbolizes purity and grace in Chinese culture, often associated with immortals and deities.

    Syntactic Ambiguities

    • This sentence describes a series of events: entering the mountains, shooting a deer, falling into a pit, and finding it deep and isolated.
    • This phrase indicates the contents of the pit, which are bear cubs.
    • This sentence describes the bear's reaction upon returning.
    • This phrase describes the bear's action of bringing out fruit and sharing it with the cubs.
    • This phrase describes the bear's final action of creating a portion for the human.
    • This sentence describes the human's desperate action of eating the fruit.
    • This phrase describes the gradual process of the human and bear becoming familiar with each other.
    • This sentence describes the bear mother's daily routine and her consistent behavior of sharing.
    • This phrase indicates the human's dependence on the bear for survival.
    • This sentence describes the growth of the cubs and the bear mother's actions as they grow.
    • This sentence describes the human's dire situation after the cubs are gone.
    • This sentence describes the bear mother's return and her proximity to the human.
    • This sentence describes the human's understanding and the final act of escape.
    • This phrase indicates a fortunate outcome.
    • This sentence describes the setting and the appearance of the women.
    • This sentence describes the setting and the strange vision in the mirror.
    • This sentence describes the subsequent action of cutting the deer and making jerky.

    Proper Nouns

    • 晋升平中 - A specific period in Chinese history.
    • 淮南 - A historical region in present-day Anhui Province, China.
    • 铜镜 - A type of mirror made of copper.
    • 鹿 - Symbolizes purity and grace in Chinese culture.

    In the early years of the Jin dynasty, there was a man who went into the mountains to shoot a deer. Suddenly, he fell into a deep pit. Inside, there were several bear cubs. Soon after, a large bear came, staring at the man. The man thought he would surely be harmed. After a long time, the bear brought out hidden fruit and divided it among the cubs. Finally, she made a portion and placed it in front of the man. The man was very hungry, so he took the risk and ate it. Gradually, they became familiar and accustomed to each other. The bear mother would go out every morning to find fruit and return, always dividing it for the man. He relied on this to sustain his life. As the bear cubs grew up, the mother carried each one out. When the cubs were all gone, the man was trapped in the pit with no way out. The bear mother soon returned and sat beside the man. The man understood her intention, so he hugged the bear's foot, and they jumped out. In the end, there was no harm.

    In Huainan, there was a family named Chen who was farming beans in the fields. Suddenly, they saw two women with very beautiful appearances, wearing purple and green skirts. It rained, but their clothes did not get wet. There was a copper mirror hanging on the wall, and they saw two deer in the mirror. They then used a knife to cut them and made them into jerky. [53]

    晋太元中，丁零王翟昭后宫养一猕猴，在妓女房前。前后妓女，同时怀妊，各产子三头，出便跳跃。昭方知是猴所为，乃杀猴及子。妓女同时号哭。昭问之，云：“初见一年少，著黄练单衣，白纱合，甚可爱，笑语如人。”
    　　会稽句章民张然，滞役在都，经年不得归。家有少妇，无子，惟与一奴守舍，妇遂与奴私通。然在都养一狗，甚快，名曰“乌龙”，常以自随。后假归，妇与奴谋，欲得杀然。然及妇作饭食，共坐下食。妇语然：“与君当大别离，君可强笑。”然未得啖，奴已张弓矢当户，须然食毕。然涕泣不食，乃以盘中肉及饭掷狗，祝曰：“养汝数年，吾当将死，汝能救我否”狗得食不啖，惟注睛舐唇视奴。然亦觉之。奴催食转急。然决计，拍膝大呼曰：“乌龙与手！”狗应声伤奴。奴失刀仗倒地，狗咋其阴，然因取刀杀奴。以妇付县，杀之。 [54]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [54]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 晋太元中 - Time marker referring to the reign of Emperor Xiaowu of Jin (376-396 CE). '太元' is the era name of this emperor.
    • 同时怀妊 - Means 'simultaneously pregnant,' indicating that multiple women became pregnant at the same time.
    • 各产子三头 - Means 'each gave birth to three children,' which is unusual and suggests a supernatural or monstrous event.
    • 出便跳跃 - Means 'they immediately began jumping,' describing the behavior of the newborns.
    • 方知是猴所为 - Means 'realized that it was the monkey's doing,' indicating that the king understood the cause of the strange births.
    • 滞役在都 - Means 'stuck in service in the capital,' indicating that he was unable to return home due to his duties.
    • 与奴私通 - Means 'had an affair with the servant,' indicating an illicit relationship.
    • 假归 - Means 'temporarily returned home,' indicating a leave of absence.
    • 君可强笑 - Means 'you can force a smile,' suggesting a final, ironic statement before death.
    • 拍膝大呼 - Means 'thumped his knees and shouted,' describing a dramatic action.
    • 乌龙与手 - The phrase '乌龙' (Wulong) is the name of the dog, and '与手' is unclear but likely means 'help me' or 'come here.'
    • 咋其阴 - Means 'bit his genitals,' describing the dog's attack on the servant.
    • 付县 - Means 'sent to the county magistrate,' indicating legal punishment.
    • 杀之 - Means 'killed her,' referring to the execution of the wife.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 晋太元中 - Refers to the reign of Emperor Xiaowu of Jin, a period of political and social instability in the Eastern Jin dynasty.
    • 丁零 - A nomadic people who were often in conflict with the Chinese states.
    • 妓女 - In this context, refers to concubines or female entertainers in the king's harem, a common practice among the elite.
    • 会稽句章 - A specific location in the eastern part of China, known for its cultural and historical significance.
    • 滞役在都 - Reflects the common practice of officials being stationed in the capital, often far from their families.
    • 少妇 - Refers to a young wife, a term that carries connotations of youth and beauty.
    • 假归 - Indicates a temporary leave of absence, often granted for personal reasons but still subject to official duties.
    • 付县 - Refers to the local administrative system where minor crimes were handled by county magistrates.

    Proper Nouns

    • 晋太元中 - The reign period of Emperor Xiaowu of Jin.
    • 丁零王翟昭 - The king of the Dingling people.
    • 会稽句章 - A specific location in eastern China.
    • 张然 - The name of the commoner.
    • 乌龙 - The name of the dog.

    During the reign of Emperor Xiaowu of Jin, the Dingling King Zhai Zhao kept a pet monkey in his harem, near the rooms of his concubines. The concubines, one after another, became pregnant simultaneously and each gave birth to three children, who immediately began jumping around. Zhai Zhao realized that the monkey was responsible and ordered the killing of the monkey and its offspring. The concubines cried out in unison. When asked, they said, "We first saw a young man dressed in a yellow silk robe and white gauze trousers, who was very charming and spoke like a human."

    In the county of Gouzhang in Kuaiji, there lived a commoner named Zhang Ran, who was stuck in service in the capital and could not return home for many years. He had a young wife who had no children and was left alone with a servant to guard the house. The wife secretly had an affair with the servant. While in the capital, Zhang Ran had a dog named Wulong, which he kept with him at all times. After a temporary leave, the wife and the servant plotted to kill Zhang Ran. When Zhang Ran returned home, the wife and the servant prepared a meal together. The wife told Zhang Ran, "I will be separated from you, so you might as well smile." Before Zhang Ran could eat, the servant had already set up a bow and arrow at the door, waiting for him to finish eating. Zhang Ran wept and refused to eat, instead throwing the meat and rice from the plate to the dog and said, "I have raised you for several years, and now I am about to die. Can you save me?" The dog did not eat but stared intently at the servant with its eyes and licked its lips. Zhang Ran also noticed this. The servant urged Zhang Ran to eat more urgently. Zhang Ran made up his mind, thumped his knees and shouted, "Wulong, help me!" The dog responded by attacking the servant. The servant dropped his weapon and fell to the ground, while the dog bit his genitals. Zhang Ran then took a knife and killed the servant. He handed the wife over to the county magistrate, who executed her. [54]

    晋太和中，广陵人杨生，养一狗，甚爱怜之，行止与俱。后生饮酒醉，行大泽草中，眠不能动。时方冬月燎原，风势极盛。狗乃周章号唤，生醉不觉。前有一坑水，狗便走往水中，还以身洒生左右草上。如此数次，周旋跬步，草皆沾湿，火至免焚。生醒，方见之。尔后生因暗行，堕于枯井中，狗呻吟彻晓。有人经过，怪此狗向井号，往视，见生。生曰：“君可出我，当有厚报。”人曰：“以此狗见与，便当相出。生曰：“此狗曾活我已死，不得相与。余即无惜。”人曰：“若尔，便不相出。”狗因下头目井。生知其意，乃语路人云：“以狗相与。”人即出之，系之而去。却后五曰，狗夜走归。 [55]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [55]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 养一狗，甚爱怜之 - Descriptive phrase meaning 'he kept a dog, whom he loved very much.'
    • 周章号唤 - Descriptive phrase meaning 'the dog ran around barking frantically.'
    • 前有一坑水，还以身洒生左右草上，如此数次，周旋跬步，草皆沾湿，火至免焚 - Descriptive phrase meaning 'there was a small pond of water ahead; the dog returned to splash water on the grass around Yang Sheng; it did this several times; moving back and forth in small steps; the grass became wet, and when the fire came, it did not burn him.'
    • 尔后生因暗行，堕于枯井中，呻吟彻晓，见生 - Descriptive phrase meaning 'later, Yang Sheng was walking in the dark and fell into a dry well; the dog barked continuously all night; the dog saw Yang Sheng.'
    • 君可出我，当有厚报，以此狗见与，便当相出 - Dialogue meaning 'you can pull me out, and I will reward you generously; if you give me this dog, I will pull you out.'
    • 却后五曰，狗夜走归 - Descriptive phrase meaning 'five days later, the dog returned home at night.'

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 晋太和 - Refers to the Taihe period of the Jin dynasty, a specific historical era.
    • 广陵 - Refers to Guangling, an ancient region in present-day Jiangsu Province.
    • 燎原 - Refers to a wildfire, a common natural disaster in ancient China.
    • 暗行 - Refers to walking in the dark, which was a common scenario in ancient narratives of survival.

    Proper Nouns

    • 晋太和 - The Taihe period of the Jin dynasty.
    • 广陵 - The ancient region of Guangling.
    • 杨生 - The name of the protagonist, Yang Sheng.
    • 狗 - The dog, a central character in the story.

    During the Taihe period of the Jin dynasty, there was a man from Guangling named Yang Sheng who kept a dog that he loved very much. The dog followed him wherever he went. Later, Yang Sheng drank and got drunk, and while walking in a large marshy area of grass, he fell asleep and could not move. It was winter, and the grass was burning, with the wind blowing fiercely. The dog ran around barking frantically, but Yang Sheng was too drunk to notice. There was a small pond of water ahead, and the dog returned to splash water on the grass around Yang Sheng. It did this several times, moving back and forth in small steps, and the grass became wet. When the fire came, it did not burn him. Yang Sheng woke up and saw what the dog had done. Later, while walking in the dark, Yang Sheng fell into a dry well. The dog barked continuously all night. A passerby noticed the dog barking near the well and went to investigate, finding Yang Sheng inside. Yang Sheng said, "You can pull me out, and I will reward you generously." The passerby replied, "If you give me this dog, I will pull you out." Yang Sheng said, "This dog once saved my life when I was already dead; I cannot give it to you. But I have no regrets about anything else." The passerby said, "If that is the case, I will not pull you out." The dog then lowered its head toward the well. Yang Sheng understood its intention and told the passerby, "Give me the dog." The passerby pulled Yang Sheng out and tied the dog to his cart and left. Five days later, the dog returned home at night. [55]

    晋穆、哀之世，领军司马济阳蔡咏家狗，夜辄群众相吠，往视便伏。后曰，使人夜伺，有一狗，著黄衣，白合，长五六尺，众狗共吠之。寻迹，定是咏家老黄狗，即打杀之。吠乃止。
    　　代郡张平者，苻坚时为贼帅，自号并州刺史。养一狗，名曰“飞獒”，形若小驴。忽夜上厅事，狗上行，行声如平常。未经年，果为鲜卑所逐，败走，降苻坚，未几便死。 [56]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [56]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 夜辄眾相吠 - Describes dogs barking at night, with '辄' meaning 'always' or 'frequently.'
    • 形若小驴 - Describes the appearance of the dog as resembling a small donkey.
    • 吠乃止 - Means 'the barking stopped,' indicating the cessation of the barking.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 晉穆、哀之世 - Refers to the reigns of the Jìn dynasty's rulers Mu and Ai, a specific historical period.
    • 代郡 - Refers to Dai Commandery, a region in northern China.
    • 苻堅 - Refers to Fu Jian, a ruler of the Former Qin dynasty.
    • 鮮卑 - Refers to the Xianbei, a nomadic people who were often in conflict with Chinese dynasties.
    • 飛獒 - Refers to a flying dog, a mythical or legendary creature.
    • 廳事 - Refers to a hall or office, likely the place where the dog was seen.
    • 並州刺史 - Means 'governor of Bingzhou,' indicating a self-proclaimed title.
    • 張平 - A person named Zhang Ping, a self-proclaimed bandit leader and governor of Bingzhou.

    Proper Nouns

    • 領軍司馬濟陽蔡咏 - A person named Cai Yong, a military official from Jiyang.
    • 飛獒 - A flying dog, a mythical or legendary creature.
    • 廳事 - A hall or office.

    During the reigns of the Jìn dynasty's rulers Mu and Ai, a dog in the household of Cai Yong, a military official from Jiyang, would frequently bark at night, and when one went to look, the dogs would hide. Later, someone was sent to watch at night, and they saw a dog wearing a yellow robe and a white accessory, about five or six feet long, that the other dogs were barking at. The investigators traced the dog and determined that it was an old yellow dog from Cai Yong's household, which they then killed. The barking stopped.

    In Dai Commandery, there was a man named Zhang Ping, who during the time of Fu Jian, a ruler of the Former Qin dynasty, was a self-proclaimed bandit leader and governor of Bingzhou. He kept a dog named "Flying Dog," which resembled a small donkey. One night, the dog suddenly appeared in the hall, walking and making a sound like usual. Shortly after, the dog died. [56]

    太叔王氏，后娶庾氏女，年少色美。王年六十，常宿外，妇深无忻。后忽一夕见王还，燕婉兼常。昼坐，因共食。奴从外来，见之大惊，以白王。王遽入，伪者亦出。二人交会中庭，俱著白合，衣服形貌如一。真者便先举杖打伪者，伪者亦报打之。二人各敕子弟，令与手。王儿乃突前痛打，是一黄狗，遂打杀之。王时为会稽府佐，门士云：“恒见一老黄狗，自东而来。”其妇大耻，病死。
    　　林虑山下有一亭，人每过此，宿者辄病死。云尝有十余人，男女杂合，衣或白或黄，辄蒲博相戏。时有郅伯夷，宿于此亭，明烛而坐诵经。至中夜，忽有十余人来，与伯夷并坐蒲博。伯夷密以烛照之，乃是群犬。因执烛起，阳误以烛烧其衣，作燃毛气。伯夷怀刀，捉一人刺之，初作人唤，遂死成犬。余悉走去。 [57]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [57]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 太叔王氏 - Refers to a man named Wang from the Taishu clan, where '太叔' is a clan name and '王氏' indicates his family name.
    • 后娶庾氏女 - Indicates that Wang married a woman from the Yu clan, with '后娶' meaning 'later married.'
    • 燕婉兼常 - Describes a harmonious and affectionate relationship, where '燕婉' means 'harmonious' and '兼常' means 'as usual' or 'in the ordinary way.'
    • 昼坐，因共食 - Indicates that they sat together during the day and ate together, with '昼坐' meaning 'sitting during the day' and '因共食' meaning 'and thus ate together.'
    • 奴从外来，见之大惊 - Describes a servant coming from outside and being greatly surprised, with '奴从外来' meaning 'the servant came from outside' and '见之大惊' meaning 'was greatly surprised.'
    • 伪者亦出 - Indicates that the false person also came out, with '伪者' meaning 'the false one' or 'the imposter.'
    • 二人交会中庭，俱著白合，衣服形貌如一 - Describes two people meeting in the courtyard, both wearing white clothing, and looking identical, with '二人交会中庭' meaning 'the two met in the courtyard,' '俱著白合' meaning 'both wearing white clothing,' and '衣服形貌如一' meaning 'their clothes and appearance were the same.'
    • 真者便先举杖打伪者，伪者亦报打之 - Describes the real person striking the imposter first, and the imposter retaliating, with '真者' meaning 'the real one,' '举杖打' meaning 'striking with a stick,' and '伪者亦报打之' meaning 'the imposter also retaliated.'
    • 二人各敕子弟，令与手 - Indicates that the two ordered their children to fight, with '二人各敕子弟' meaning 'the two ordered their children' and '令与手' meaning 'to fight.'
    • 王儿乃突前痛打，是一黄狗，遂打杀之 - Describes Wang's son suddenly attacking and beating the imposter, who turned out to be a yellow dog, and ultimately killing it, with '王儿乃突前痛打' meaning 'Wang's son suddenly attacked and beat,' '是一黄狗' meaning 'it was a yellow dog,' and '遂打杀之' meaning 'and ultimately killed it.'
    • 王时为会稽府佐 - Indicates that Wang was an assistant to the governor of Kuaiji Prefecture, with '王时为会稽府佐' meaning 'Wang was an assistant to the governor of Kuaiji Prefecture.'
    • 门士云：“恒见一老黄狗，自东而来。” - Describes the gatekeeper saying that he often saw an old yellow dog coming from the east, with '门士云' meaning 'the gatekeeper said,' '恒见' meaning 'often saw,' and '一老黄狗，自东而来' meaning 'an old yellow dog coming from the east.'
    • 其妇大耻，病死 - Indicates that Wang's wife was greatly ashamed and died of illness, with '其妇大耻' meaning 'his wife was greatly ashamed' and '病死' meaning 'died of illness.'
    • 林虑山下有一亭 - Describes a pavilion at the foot of Linlu Mountain, with '林虑山下' meaning 'at the foot of Linlu Mountain' and '有一亭' meaning 'there was a pavilion.'
    • 人每过此，宿者辄病死 - Indicates that people who passed by and stayed overnight always died, with '人每过此' meaning 'people who passed by,' '宿者辄病死' meaning 'those who stayed overnight always died.'
    • 云尝有十余人，男女杂合，衣或白或黄，辄蒲博相戏 - Describes a group of over ten people, men and women mixed, wearing either white or yellow clothing, and playing games, with '云尝有十余人' meaning 'it is said there were over ten people,' '男女杂合' meaning 'men and women mixed,' '衣或白或黄' meaning 'wearing either white or yellow clothing,' and '辄蒲博相戏' meaning 'always playing games.'
    • 时有郅伯夷，宿于此亭 - Indicates that there was a person named Zhi Bo Yi who stayed at this pavilion, with '时有郅伯夷' meaning 'there was a person named Zhi Bo Yi' and '宿于此亭' meaning 'who stayed at this pavilion.'
    • 明烛而坐诵经 - Describes him sitting and reciting scriptures with a lit candle, with '明烛而坐' meaning 'sitting with a lit candle' and '诵经' meaning 'reciting scriptures.'
    • 至中夜，忽有十余人来，与伯夷并坐蒲博 - Describes over ten people coming in the middle of the night and playing games with Bo Yi, with '至中夜' meaning 'in the middle of the night,' '忽有十余人来' meaning 'over ten people came,' '与伯夷并坐' meaning 'and sat with Bo Yi,' and '蒲博' meaning 'playing games.'
    • 伯夷密以烛照之，乃是群犬 - Describes Bo Yi secretly illuminating them with a candle and discovering they were a group of dogs, with '伯夷密以烛照之' meaning 'Bo Yi secretly illuminated them with a candle' and '乃是群犬' meaning 'they were a group of dogs.'
    • 因执烛起，阳误以烛烧其衣，作燃毛气 - Describes Bo Yi holding the candle and pretending to accidentally burn their clothes, producing a burnt hair smell, with '因执烛起' meaning 'holding the candle and standing up,' '阳误以烛烧其衣' meaning 'pretending to accidentally burn their clothes,' and '作燃毛气' meaning 'producing a burnt hair smell.'
    • 伯夷怀刀，捉一人刺之，初作人唤，遂死成犬 - Describes Bo Yi holding a knife and stabbing one of them, who initially cried out as a human but then died and turned into a dog, with '伯夷怀刀' meaning 'Bo Yi holding a knife,' '捉一人刺之' meaning 'grabbed one and stabbed it,' '初作人唤' meaning 'initially cried out as a human,' and '遂死成犬' meaning 'then died and turned into a dog.'
    • 余悉走去 - Indicates that the rest all ran away, with '余悉走去' meaning 'the rest all ran away.'

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 太叔王氏 - Refers to a man named Wang from the Taishu clan, a common surname in ancient China.
    • 庾氏女 - Refers to a woman from the Yu clan, another common surname in ancient China.
    • 燕婉兼常 - A classical expression describing a harmonious and affectionate relationship.
    • 昼坐，因共食 - A common practice in ancient Chinese households where family members would sit together and eat.
    • 奴从外来，见之大惊 - Reflects the hierarchical structure of ancient Chinese households where servants had lower status.
    • 伪者亦出 - Suggests the presence of an imposter or supernatural being.
    • 二人交会中庭，俱著白合，衣服形貌如一 - Describes a supernatural event where two beings appear identical.
    • 真者便先举杖打伪者，伪者亦报打之 - Reflects the struggle between the real and the false, a common theme in supernatural stories.
    • 二人各敕子弟，令与手 - Indicates the involvement of children in a conflict, reflecting the importance of family in ancient Chinese society.
    • 王儿乃突前痛打，是一黄狗，遂打杀之 - Describes a violent encounter that results in the death of a supernatural being.
    • 王时为会稽府佐 - Indicates Wang's official position as an assistant to the governor of Kuaiji Prefecture, reflecting the administrative structure of ancient China.
    • 门士云：“恒见一老黄狗，自东而来。” - Reflects the belief in supernatural beings and the role of gatekeepers in ancient Chinese society.
    • 其妇大耻，病死 - Describes the emotional and physical consequences of the supernatural event on Wang's wife.
    • 林虑山下有一亭 - Describes a specific location, reflecting the importance of geography in ancient Chinese literature.
    • 人每过此，宿者辄病死 - Reflects the belief in supernatural dangers associated with certain locations.
    • 云尝有十余人，男女杂合，衣或白或黄，辄蒲博相戏 - Describes a group of people engaging in games, reflecting the social activities of ancient China.
    • 时有郅伯夷，宿于此亭 - Introduces a specific character, reflecting the narrative structure of ancient Chinese stories.
    • 明烛而坐诵经 - Reflects the religious practice of reciting scriptures in ancient China.
    • 至中夜，忽有十余人来，与伯夷并坐蒲博 - Describes a supernatural event occurring in the middle of the night, reflecting the belief in nocturnal activities of supernatural beings.
    • 伯夷密以烛照之，乃是群犬 - Describes a moment of revelation, where the supernatural beings are revealed to be dogs, reflecting the theme of deception in ancient Chinese stories.
    • 因执烛起，阳误以烛烧其衣，作燃毛气 - Describes a strategic move by Bo Yi to create a distraction, reflecting the use of clever tactics in ancient Chinese narratives.
    • 伯夷怀刀，捉一人刺之，初作人唤，遂死成犬 - Describes a violent confrontation with a supernatural being, reflecting the theme of conflict between humans and supernatural entities in ancient Chinese stories.
    • 余悉走去 - Indicates the departure of the remaining supernatural beings, reflecting the resolution of the conflict in the narrative.

    A man from the Taishu clan named Wang later married a woman from the Yu clan, who was young and beautiful. At the age of sixty, Wang often stayed out overnight, and his wife was deeply unhappy about this. One evening, she suddenly saw Wang return, and their relationship was as harmonious as usual. During the day, they sat together and ate. A servant came from outside and was greatly surprised, reporting this to Wang. Wang rushed in, and the imposter also came out. The two met in the courtyard, both wearing white clothing, and their clothes and appearance were identical. The real person struck the imposter first with a stick, and the imposter retaliated. The two ordered their children to fight. Wang's son suddenly attacked and beat the imposter, who turned out to be a yellow dog, and ultimately killed it. At the time, Wang was an assistant to the governor of Kuaiji Prefecture, and the gatekeeper said, "I often see an old yellow dog coming from the east." His wife was greatly ashamed and died of illness.

    At the foot of Linlu Mountain, there was a pavilion. People who passed by and stayed overnight always died. It is said there were over ten people, men and women mixed, wearing either white or yellow clothing, and they always played games. There was a person named Zhi Bo Yi who stayed at this pavilion, sitting and reciting scriptures with a lit candle. In the middle of the night, over ten people came, and they sat with Bo Yi, playing games. Bo Yi secretly illuminated them with a candle and discovered they were a group of dogs. He held the candle and stood up, pretending to accidentally burn their clothes, producing a burnt hair smell. Bo Yi held a knife and stabbed one of them, who initially cried out as a human but then died and turned into a dog. The rest all ran away. [57]

    顾霈者，吴之豪士也。曾送客于升平亭。时有一沙门在座，是流俗道人。主人欲杀一羊，羊绝绳便走，来投入此道人膝中，穿头向袈裟下。道人不能救，即将去杀之。既行炙，主人便先割以啖道人。道人食炙下喉，觉炙行走皮中，毒痛不可忍。呼医来针之，以数针贯其炙，炙犹动摇。乃破出视之，故是一脔肉耳。道人于此得疾，遂作羊鸣，吐沫。还寺，少时卒。
    　　吴郡顾旃，猎至一岗，忽闻人语声云：“咄！咄！今年衰。”乃与众寻觅。岗顶有一阱，是古时冢。见一老狐蹲冢中，前有一卷簿书，老狐对书屈指，有所计校。乃放太咋杀之。取视簿书，悉是奸人女名。已经奸者，乃以朱钩头。所疏名有百数，旃女正在簿次。 [58]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [58]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 顧霈者，吳之豪士也 - Introduces the subject and his status as a prominent scholar from the Wu region.
    • 曾送客於升平亭 - Describes an event where the subject was sending off guests at the Pingting Pavilion.
    • 是流俗道人 - Characterizes the monk as a common one.
    • 主人欲杀一羊 - Describes the host's intention to kill a sheep.
    • 羊绝绳便走 - Describes the sheep breaking the rope and running away.
    • 穿头向袈裟下 - Describes the sheep running into the monk's robe.
    • 道人不能救，即将去杀之 - Describes the monk's inability to save the sheep and his decision to take it to be killed.
    • 既行炙，主人便先割以啖道人 - Describes the cooking and serving process where the host first cuts and feeds the cooked meat to the monk.
    • 道人食炙下喉，觉炙行走皮中，毒痛不可忍 - Describes the monk's reaction to feeling the cooked meat moving inside his skin, causing unbearable pain.
    • 呼医来针之，以数针贯其炙，炙犹动摇 - Describes the monk calling a doctor who tries to remove the meat with needles, but the meat still moves.
    • 乃破出视之，故是一脔肉耳 - Describes the final outcome where the meat is cut out and found to be just a piece of meat.
    • 道人于此得疾，遂作羊鸣，吐沫 - Describes the monk falling ill and beginning to bleat like a sheep, spitting foam.
    • 还寺，少时卒 - Describes the monk returning to the temple and dying shortly after.
    • 吴郡顾旃，猎至一岗 - Introduces the next subject and his activity of hunting on a hill.
    • 忽闻人语声云：“咄！咄！今年衰。” - Describes the sudden hearing of a voice saying, 'Hmph! Hmph! This year is bad.'
    • 乃与众寻觅 - Describes the search for the source of the voice.
    • 岗顶有一阱，是古时冢 - Describes the location of the pit, an ancient tomb.
    • 见一老狐蹲冢中，前有一卷簿书，老狐对书屈指，有所计校 - Describes seeing an old fox sitting in the tomb, with a scroll of books in front of it, counting and calculating.
    • 乃放太咋杀之 - Describes the action of killing the fox.
    • 取视簿书，悉是奸人女名 - Describes the content of the book being a list of names of immoral women.
    • 已经奸者，乃以朱钩头 - Describes the marking system used to denote those who had been immoral, with red hooks.
    • 所疏名有百数，旃女正在簿次 - Describes the list of hundreds of names, with Gu Zhan's daughter among them.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 吴之豪士 - Refers to a prominent scholar from the Wu region, indicating his social status.
    • 升平亭 - A specific location where the event took place.
    • 流俗道人 - Refers to a common monk, indicating his status in society.
    • 古时冢 - An ancient tomb, indicating a historical setting.
    • 奸人女名 - Names of immoral women, reflecting the moral judgment of the time.
    • 朱钩头 - A marking system used to denote those who had been immoral, indicating a form of social stigma.

    Proper Nouns

    • 顾霈 - The name of the first subject.
    • 吴 - The region where the first subject is from.
    • 升平亭 - The specific location where the first event took place.
    • 流俗道人 - The name of the monk.
    • 古时冢 - The specific location of the second event.
    • 顾旃 - The name of the second subject.
    • 吴郡 - The region where the second subject is from.

    Gu Pei was a prominent scholar of Wu. Once he was sending off guests at the Pingting Pavilion. At the time, there was a monk present, a common one. The host wanted to kill a sheep, and the sheep broke the rope and ran away, running into the monk's robe. The monk could not save it and took it to be killed. After the meat was cooked, the host first cut some and fed it to the monk. The monk felt the cooked meat moving inside his skin, causing unbearable pain. He called a doctor who tried to remove it with needles, but the meat still moved. They cut it out and found it was just a piece of meat. The monk fell ill and began to bleat like a sheep, spitting foam. He returned to the temple and died shortly after.

    Gu Zhan of Wu, while hunting on a hill, suddenly heard a voice saying, "Hmph! Hmph! This year is bad." He and his companions searched for the source of the voice. At the top of the hill was a pit, an ancient tomb. They saw an old fox sitting in the tomb, with a scroll of books in front of it, counting and calculating. They killed the fox. They looked at the book and found it was a list of names of immoral women. Those who had already been immoral were marked with red hooks. There were hundreds of names listed, and Gu Zhan's daughter was among them. [58]

    襄阳习凿齿，字彦威，为荆州主簿。从桓宣武出猎，时大雪，于江陵城西，见草上雪气出。伺观，见一黄物，射之，应箭死。往取，乃一老雄狐，脚上带绛绫香囊。
    　　宋酒泉郡，每太守到官，无几辄死。后有渤海陈斐见授此郡，忧恐不乐，就卜者占其吉凶。卜者曰：“远诸侯，放伯裘。能解此，则无忧。”斐不解此语，答曰：“君去，自当解之。”斐既到官，侍医有张侯，直医有王侯，卒有史侯、董侯等，斐心悟曰：“此谓诸侯。”乃远之。即卧，思“放伯裘”之义，不知何谓。至夜半后，有物来斐被上。斐觉，以被冒取之，物遂跳踉，訇訇作声。外人闻，持火入，欲杀之。魅乃言曰：“我实无恶意，但欲试府君耳。能一相赦，当深报君恩。”斐曰：“汝为何物，而忽干犯太守。”魅曰：“我本千岁狐也。今变为魅，垂化为神，而正触府君威怒，甚遭困厄。我字伯裘，若府君有急难，但呼我字，便当自解。”斐乃喜曰：“真‘放伯裘’之义也。 [59]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [59]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 從桓宣武出獵 - Accompanying桓宣武 (桓温) on a hunting trip.
    • 時大雪 - It was snowing heavily.
    • 見草上雪氣出 - Seeing steam rising from the grass.
    • 伺觀，見一黃物，射之，應箭死 - Watching carefully, they saw a yellow object, shot it, and it died immediately.
    • 往取，乃一老雄狐，腳上帶絳綾香囊 - They went to retrieve it, and it turned out to be an old male fox wearing a red silk pouch on its foot.
    • 每太守到官，無幾輒死 - Whenever a new governor arrived, he would die shortly afterward.
    • 就卜者占其吉凶 - Consulting a fortune-teller to predict good or bad omens.
    • 遠侯，放伯裘 - Keep away from the servants and let go of Berqiu.
    • 我本千歲狐也，今變為魅，垂化為神 - I am a thousand-year-old fox, now I have become a ghost, almost transforming into a deity.
    • 若府君有急難，但呼我字，便當自解 - If you, the governor, are in danger, just call my name, and I will help you.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 習凿齿 - A historian and writer of the Eastern Jin dynasty, known for his work 'Records of the Three Kingdoms'.
    • 桓宣武 - A famous general and statesman of the Eastern Jin dynasty, known for his military campaigns and political influence.
    • 宋 - Refers to the Southern Song dynasty, a period in Chinese history.
    • 酒泉郡 - An administrative region in the state of Song.
    • 卜者 - A person who predicts the future using various methods, such as divination.

    Proper Nouns

    • 習凿齿 - A historian and writer of the Eastern Jin dynasty.
    • 桓宣武 - A famous general and statesman of the Eastern Jin dynasty.
    • 宋 - Refers to the Southern Song dynasty.
    • 酒泉郡 - An administrative region in the state of Song.
    • 卜者 - A person who predicts the future using various methods.

    Xizaochi of Xiangyang, styled Yanwei, was the chief clerk of Jingzhou. He accompanied Huan Xuanwu on a hunting trip when it was snowing heavily. In the western part of Jiangling City, they saw steam rising from the grass. They watched carefully and saw a yellow object, shot it, and it died immediately. They went to retrieve it, and it turned out to be an old male fox wearing a red silk pouch on its foot.

    In the prefecture of Jiuyuan, whenever a new governor arrived, he would die shortly afterward. Later, Chen Fei of Bohai was appointed to this prefecture. He was worried and unhappy, so he consulted a fortune-teller to predict good or bad omens. The fortune-teller said, "Keep away from the servants and let go of Berqiu. If you can understand this, you will have no worries." Chen Fei did not understand this advice and replied, "You leave, and I will understand it." After arriving at his post, Chen Fei found that his attendants were named Zhang Hou, his personal physician was named Wang Hou, and his attendants were named Shi Hou and Dong Hou. Chen Fei realized, "These are the 'servants' mentioned." He kept away from them. That night, he lay down and thought about the meaning of "let go of Berqiu," but he did not know what it meant. After midnight, something came onto Chen Fei's bedding. Chen Fei woke up and covered it with the bedding, and the thing jumped around, making loud noises. The people outside heard the noise and brought a torch inside, intending to kill it. The spirit said, "I truly have no malicious intent; I only wanted to test you, the governor. If you can spare me, I will deeply repay your kindness." Chen Fei asked, "What are you? Why did you suddenly offend the governor?" The spirit replied, "I am a thousand-year-old fox. Now I have become a ghost, almost transforming into a deity, and I am in great distress because I have offended your majesty. My name is Berqiu. If you, the governor, are in danger, just call my name, and I will help you." Chen Fei then said happily, "This is truly the meaning of 'let go of Berqiu.'" [59]

    ”即便放之。小开被，忽然有光，赤如电，从户出。明夜有敲门者，斐问是谁，答曰：“伯裘。”问：“来何为”答曰：“白事。”问曰：“何事”答曰：“北界有贼奴发也。”斐按发则验。每事先以语斐。于是境界无毫发之奸，而咸曰圣府君。后经月余，主簿李音共斐侍婢私通。既而惧为伯裘所白，遂与诸侯谋杀斐。伺傍无人，便与诸侯持杖直入，欲格杀之。斐惶怖，即呼“伯裘救我！”即有物如伸一匹绛，砉刂然作声。音、侯伏地失魂，乃以次缚取〖询皆服，云：“斐未到官，音已惧失权，与诸侯谋杀斐。会诸侯见斥，事不成。”斐即杀音等。伯裘乃谢裴曰：“未及白音奸情，乃为府君所召。虽效微力，犹用惭惶。”后月余，与斐辞曰：“今后当上天去，不得复与府君相往来也。”遂去不见。 [60]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [60]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 即便放之 - Phrase meaning 'let him go' or 'release him,' used in a context of supernatural intervention.
    • 小开被，忽然有光，赤如电 - Describes a sudden, intense red light like lightning, likely a supernatural phenomenon.
    • 从户出 - Means 'came out from the door,' indicating the direction of the light.
    • 明夜有敲门者 - Means 'there was someone knocking on the door at night,' introducing a mysterious event.
    • 斐问是谁，答曰：'伯裘。' - Dialogue where someone asks who is knocking and is told it is 'Boqiu.'
    • 问：'来何为'答曰：'白事。' - Dialogue where the purpose of the visit is asked and answered as 'to report something.'
    • 问曰：'何事'答曰：'北界有贼奴发也。' - Dialogue revealing the nature of the report as an alert about bandits.
    • 斐按发则验 - Means 'when verified, it proved true,' indicating the accuracy of the report.
    • 每事先以语斐 - Means 'he would always inform Wei first,' showing a pattern of behavior.
    • 境界无毫发之奸，而咸曰圣府君 - Means 'there was no slightest wrongdoing in the region, and everyone called him the Holy Magistrate.'
    • 后经月余，主簿李音共斐侍婢私通 - Describes a scandal involving the magistrate's maid and a subordinate official.
    • 惧为伯裘所白 - Means 'fearing that Boqiu would report it,' indicating the fear of exposure.
    • 与诸侯谋杀斐 - Means 'conspired to kill Wei,' showing the severity of the plot.
    • 伺傍无人，便与诸侯持杖直入，欲格杀之 - Describes the conspirators waiting for an opportunity and then attacking.
    • 斐惶怖，即呼'伯裘救我！' - Means 'Wei was terrified and called out, 'Boqiu, save me!'' showing his desperation.
    • 即有物如伸一匹绛，砉刂然作声 - Describes a supernatural intervention involving a red cloth that makes a sound.
    • 音、侯伏地失魂 - Means 'Yin and Hou fell to the ground, losing their spirits,' indicating their fear.
    • 乃以次缚取 - Means 'they were then bound one by one,' describing the capture of the conspirators.
    • 询皆服，云：'斐未到官，音已惧失权，与诸侯谋杀斐。会诸侯见斥，事不成。' - The conspirators confessed, explaining their motives and the failure of their plot.
    • 伯裘乃谢裴曰：'未及白音奸情，乃为府君所召。虽效微力，犹用惭惶。' - Boqiu apologized to Wei, explaining his actions and feelings.
    • 后月余，与斐辞曰：'今后当上天去，不得复与府君相往来也。'遂去不见 - Boqiu bid farewell, indicating his departure and the end of their interaction.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 伯裘 - Likely a supernatural entity or spirit who assists Wei in uncovering and dealing with the conspiracy.
    • 主簿李音 - A subordinate official who is involved in the scandal.
    • 侍婢 - The magistrate's maid, who is involved in the scandal with R Yin.
    • 诸侯 - Likely refers to other officials or conspirators involved in the plot to kill Wei.
    • 圣府君 - A title of respect given to Wei, indicating his reputation as a just and effective magistrate.

    Proper Nouns

    • 伯裘 - A supernatural entity or spirit who assists Wei.
    • 主簿李音 - A subordinate official involved in the scandal.
    • 侍婢 - The magistrate's maid, involved in the scandal with R Yin.
    • 诸侯 - Likely refers to other officials or conspirators involved in the plot to kill Wei.
    • 圣府君 - A title of respect given to Wei, indicating his reputation as a just and effective magistrate.

    "Even though he was released, suddenly a bright red light like lightning appeared and came out from the door. On a clear night, there was someone knocking on the door. Wei asked who it was, and the answer was, 'Boqiu.' Wei asked, 'What is the purpose of your visit?' The answer was, 'To report something.' Wei asked again, 'What is it?' The answer was, 'There are bandits in the northern region.' When Wei verified it, it proved true. Boqiu would always inform Wei first. As a result, there was no slightest wrongdoing in the region, and everyone called him the Holy Magistrate. After a month or so, the deputy magistrate Li Yin had an affair with Wei's maid. Fearing that Boqiu would report it, they conspired to kill Wei. Waiting for an opportunity when no one was around, they entered with a staff and intended to kill him. Wei was terrified and called out, 'Boqiu, save me!' Immediately, something like a piece of red cloth appeared, making a sound. Yin and Hou fell to the ground, losing their spirits. They were then bound one by one. They confessed, saying, 'Before Wei arrived in office, Yin was already afraid of losing his authority and conspired with the lords to kill Wei. The lords were dismissed, and the plot failed.' Wei then killed Yin and others. Boqiu apologized to Wei, saying, 'I did not have the chance to report Yin's misconduct before I was summoned by the magistrate. Though I exerted a little effort, I still feel ashamed.' After a month or so, Boqiu bid farewell to Wei, saying, 'From now on, I will ascend to heaven and will no longer be able to interact with the magistrate.' And then he disappeared." [60]

    ●卷十
    　　长沙有人，忘其姓名，家住江边。有女子渚次浣衣，觉身中有异，后不以为患，遂妊身。生三物，皆如夷鱼。女以己所生，甚怜异之。乃著澡盘水中养之。经三月，此物遂大，乃是蛟子。各有字，大者为“当洪”，次者为“破阻”，小者为“扑岸”。天暴雨水，三蛟一时俱去，遂失所在。后天欲雨，此物辄来。女亦知其当来，便出望之。蛟子亦举头望母，良久方去。经年后，女亡，三蛟子一时俱至墓所哭之，经曰乃去。闻其哭声，状如狗嗥。 [61]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [61]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 卷十 - The tenth chapter or section of a larger work, indicating the text's place in a sequence.
    • 忘其姓名 - A common expression meaning 'forgot his/her name,' used to indicate anonymity or lack of specific identification.
    • 江邊 - Literally 'by the river,' used to describe a location near a body of water.
    • 渚次浣衣 - 'At the sandbar washing clothes,' a literal description of a common activity.
    • 覺身中有異 - 'Felt something strange in the body,' indicating an unusual sensation or condition.
    • 遂妊身 - 'Eventually became pregnant,' a straightforward statement of pregnancy.
    • 生三物，皆如夷魚 - 'Gave birth to three things, all like 夷 fish,' where 夷 fish refers to a type of fish, possibly a metaphor or a specific species.
    • 甚怜异之 - 'Greatly loved and treated them as unusual,' indicating a strong emotional response and recognition of the unusual nature of the children.
    • 著澡盘水中养之 - 'Placed them in a basin of water to raise them,' describing the method of care.
    • 经三月 - 'After three months,' indicating a period of time.
    • 乃是蛟子 - 'They turned out to be dragon children,' where 'dragon children' (蛟子) refers to offspring of a dragon, a mythical creature.
    • 各有字 - 'Each had a name,' indicating that the dragon children were given names.
    • 天暴雨水 - 'When there was a heavy rainstorm,' describing a weather event.
    • 三蛟一时俱去 - 'The three dragons left together at once,' indicating a sudden departure.
    • 闻其哭声，状如狗嗥 - 'Hearing their crying sound, it was like a dog howling,' describing the sound of the dragon children's cries.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 长沙 - Refers to Changsha, a city in Hunan Province, China.
    • 夷鱼 - A specific type of fish, possibly a local or mythical fish.
    • 蛟子 - Dragon children, referring to offspring of a dragon, a mythical creature in Chinese folklore.
    • 天欲雨 - 'When the sky is about to rain,' indicating an impending weather change.
    • 墓所 - 'Tomb,' referring to a burial site.

    Proper Nouns

    • 长沙 - Changsha, a city in Hunan Province, China.
    • 夷鱼 - A specific type of fish, possibly a local or mythical fish.
    • 蛟子 - Dragon children, referring to offspring of a dragon, a mythical creature in Chinese folklore.

    Chapter Ten

    In Changsha, there was a person whose name has been forgotten, who lived by the river. A woman was washing clothes at the sandbar and felt something strange in her body. Later, she did not consider it a problem and became pregnant. She gave birth to three things, all like 夷 fish. The woman greatly loved and treated them as unusual. She placed them in a basin of water to raise them. After three months, these things grew larger and turned out to be dragon children. Each had a name: the eldest was named "Dang Hong," the second "Po Zu," and the youngest "Pu An." When there was a heavy rainstorm, the three dragons left together at once and disappeared. Later, when the sky was about to rain, these things would come. The woman also knew they would come and went out to look for them. The dragon children also raised their heads to look at their mother and stayed for a long time before leaving. After a year, the woman died, and the three dragon children came together to the tomb to mourn her. After several days, they left. Their crying sound was like a dog howling. [61]

    安城平都县尹氏，居在郡东十里曰黄村，尹佃舍在焉。元嘉二十三年六月中，尹儿年十三，守舍，见一人年可二十许，骑白马，张伞，及从者四人，衣并黄色，从东方而来。至门，呼尹儿：“来暂寄息。”因入舍中庭下，坐床，一人捉伞覆之。尹儿看其衣，悉无缝，马五色斑，似鳞甲而无毛。有顷，雨气至。此人上马去，回顾尹儿曰：“明曰当更来。”尹儿观其去，西行，蹑虚而渐升，须臾，云气四合。白昼为之晦暝。明曰，大水暴出，山谷沸涌，丘壑淼漫。将淹尹舍，忽见大蛟长三丈余，盘屈庇其舍焉。 [62]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [62]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 安城平都縣尹氏 - Refers to the Yin family residing in Pingdu County, Ancheng, a specific location in ancient China.
    • 居在郡东十里曰黄村 - Indicates the family's residence is ten li east of the county seat in a village called Huangcun.
    • 元嘉二十三年六月中 - Specifies the date as the sixth month of the 23rd year of the Yuanjia era (AD 446).
    • 尹儿年十三 - Describes the age of Yin Er, who is thirteen years old.
    • 守舍 - Means 'to guard the house' or 'to stay at home.'
    • 见一人年可二十许 - Describes seeing a man around twenty years old.
    • 骑白马，张伞，及从者四人 - Describes the man riding a white horse, holding an umbrella, and being accompanied by four people.
    • 衣并黄色 - Indicates that the man and his entourage were dressed in yellow.
    • 从东方而来 - Specifies that they came from the east.
    • 来暂寄息 - Means 'come to rest briefly.'
    • 因入舍中庭下，坐床 - Describes the man entering the courtyard and sitting on a bed.
    • 一人捉伞覆之 - Indicates that one person held the umbrella over the man.
    • 悉无缝 - Means 'completely seamless,' likely referring to the clothing.
    • 马五色斑，似鳞甲而无毛 - Describes the horse as having a five-colored pattern, resembling scales and armor but without hair.
    • 有顷，雨气至 - Indicates that soon after, rain clouds appeared.
    • 此人上马去 - Describes the man getting on his horse and leaving.
    • 回顾尹儿曰：“明曰当更来。” - The man looked back at Yin Er and said, 'I will come again tomorrow.'
    • 西行，蹑虚而渐升 - Describes the man and his entourage moving westward, stepping on thin air and gradually ascending.
    • 须臾，云气四合 - Indicates that soon, clouds gathered all around.
    • 白昼为之晦暝 - Means that during the day, it became dark.
    • 大水暴出 - Describes a sudden flood.
    • 山谷沸涌，丘壑淼漫 - Describes the landscape being overwhelmed by water.
    • 将淹尹舍 - Indicates that the flood was about to inundate Yin's house.
    • 忽见大蛟长三丈余，盘屈庇其舍焉 - Describes a large dragon-like creature appearing, about three zhang long, coiling around and protecting Yin's house.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 安城平都县 - Refers to Pingdu County in Ancheng, a specific location in ancient China.
    • 元嘉二十三年 - Refers to the 23rd year of the Yuanjia era, which corresponds to AD 446.
    • 尹氏 - Refers to the Yin family, a common surname in ancient China.
    • 尹儿 - Refers to Yin Er, a young member of the Yin family.
    • 大蛟 - Refers to a large dragon-like creature, often associated with water and protection in Chinese mythology.

    Proper Nouns

    • 安城平都县 - The specific location of Pingdu County in Ancheng.
    • 元嘉二十三年 - The 23rd year of the Yuanjia era (AD 446).
    • 尹氏 - The Yin family.
    • 尹儿 - Yin Er, a young member of the Yin family.
    • 大蛟 - A large dragon-like creature in Chinese mythology.

    A Family in Pingdu County, Ancheng, lived in a village called Huangcun, ten li east of the county seat, where their tenant's house was located. In the sixth month of the 23rd year of the Yuanjia era (AD 446), Yin Er, aged thirteen, was guarding the house when he saw a man around twenty years old riding a white horse, holding an umbrella, and accompanied by four people dressed in yellow, coming from the east. Upon reaching the door, the man called out to Yin Er: "Come here to rest briefly." He then entered the courtyard and sat on a bed, with one person holding the umbrella over him. Yin Er noticed that their clothes were completely seamless, the horse had a five-colored pattern resembling scales and armor but without hair. Soon after, rain clouds appeared. The man got on his horse and left, looking back at Yin Er and saying, "I will come again tomorrow." As Yin Er watched them go, they moved westward, stepping on thin air and gradually ascending. Soon, clouds gathered all around, and it became dark during the day. The next day, a sudden flood occurred, causing the valleys to boil and the hills to be submerged. The flood was about to inundate Yin's house, but suddenly a large dragon-like creature appeared, about three zhang long, coiling around and protecting the house. [62]

    武昌虬山有龙穴，居人每见虬飞翔出入。岁旱祷之，即雨。后人筑塘其下，曰“虬塘”。
    　　吴兴人章苟者，五月中，于田中耕，以饭置菰里，每晚取食，饭亦已尽。如此非一。后伺之，见一大蛇偷食。苟遂以钅殳斫之，蛇便走去。苟逐之，至一坂，有穴，便入穴。但闻啼声云：“斫伤我某甲。”或言：“何如”或云：“付雷公，令霹雳杀奴。”须臾，云雨冥合，霹雳覆苟上。苟乃跳梁大骂曰：“天使！我贫穷，展力耕恳。蛇来偷食，罪当在蛇，反更霹雳我耶乃无知雷公也。雷公若来，吾当以钅殳斫汝腹。”须臾，云雨渐散，转霹雳向蛇穴中，蛇死者数十。 [63]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [63]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 虬飞翔出入 - Describes the dragon-like movements of the creature.
    • 岁旱祷之，即雨 - Indicates that during droughts, people would pray to the dragon, and it would bring rain.
    • 跳梁大骂 - Describes jumping and cursing loudly.
    • 云雨冥合 - Describes the gathering of clouds and rain.
    • 转霹雳向蛇穴中 - Means to redirect the lightning towards the snake's den.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 虬山 - A mountain in Wuchang known for its dragon-like features.
    • 虬塘 - A local landmark named after the dragon.
    • 章苟 - A local farmer from Wu Xing.
    • 蛇 - In Chinese culture, snakes are often associated with both good and bad omens, and sometimes with deities or spirits.

    Proper Nouns

    • 武昌 - An ancient name for the area around present-day Wuhan, Hubei Province.
    • 虬山 - A specific mountain in Wuchang.
    • 虬塘 - A specific pond in the area.
    • 吴兴 - An ancient name for the area around present-day Huzhou, Zhejiang Province.
    • 章苟 - A specific person from Wu Xing.

    Wuchang has a mountain called Qiu Mountain, which is said to have a dragon's lair. The locals often saw a dragon-like creature flying in and out of the mountain. During droughts, they would pray to the dragon, and it would bring rain. Later, people built a pond at the foot of the mountain, calling it "Qiu Pond."

    A man named Zhang Gou from Wu Xing was plowing his fields in May. He placed rice in the stalks of rice plants and would take it out every evening, only to find it gone. This happened repeatedly. One day, he caught a large snake stealing the rice. Zhang Gou struck the snake with a metal weapon, and the snake fled. Zhang Gou chased after it and reached a slope where there was a cave. The snake entered the cave. Inside, he heard a voice crying out, "You've injured me, Master So-and-So." Another voice said, "What should we do?" A third voice responded, "Hand him over to the Thunder God, let him strike him down." Soon, the sky darkened with clouds and rain, and a bolt of lightning struck Zhang Gou. Zhang Gou jumped up and shouted, "Heavenly Messenger! I am poor and work hard to till the land. The snake came to steal food, and the fault is with the snake, not me. Why are you striking me? You are an ignorant Thunder God! If the Thunder God comes, I will strike your belly with my metal weapon." Shortly after, the clouds and rain began to clear, and the lightning shifted towards the snake's den, killing dozens of snakes. [63]

    吴末，临海人入山射猎，为舍住。夜中，有一人，长一丈，著黄衣，白带，径来谓射人曰：“我有仇，克明曰当战。君可见助，当厚相报。”射人曰：“自可助君耳，何用谢为。”答曰：“明曰食时，君可出溪边。敌从北来，我南往应。白带者我，黄带者彼。”射人许之。明出，果闻岸北有声，状如风雨，草木四靡。视南亦尔。唯见二大蛇，长十余丈，于溪中相遇，便相盘绕。”白蛇势弱。射人因引弩射之，黄蛇即死。曰将暮，复见昨人来，辞谢云：“住此一年猎，明年以去，慎勿复来，来必为祸。”射人曰：“善。”遂停一年猎，所获甚多，骤至巨富。数年后，忽忆先所获多，乃忘前言，复更往猎。见先白带人告曰：“我语君勿复更来，不能见用。仇子已大，今必报君。非我所知。射人闻之，甚怖，便欲走。乃见三乌衣人，皆长八尺，俱张口向之，射人即死。 [64]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [64]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 吳末 - Refers to the end of the Wu Kingdom during the Three Kingdoms period.
    • 臨海人 - A person from the region of Linhai.
    • 為舍住 - To stay overnight in a place, implying temporary lodging.
    • 克明曰 - A time reference, possibly meaning 'on a certain day' or 'at a fixed time.'
    • 白帶 - A white belt, used as a distinguishing mark.
    • 黃帶 - A yellow belt, used as another distinguishing mark.
    • 盤繞 - To entwine or coil around each other.
    • 白蛇勢弱 - The white snake was in a weaker position.
    • 辭謝 - To express gratitude and apologize.
    • 住此一年猎 - To stay and hunt for one year.
    • 慎勿復來 - Be cautious and do not come back.
    • 來必為祸 - If you come back, there will be disaster.
    • 烏衣人 - People wearing black clothes (烏衣 means black clothes).
    • 張口向之 - Opening their mouths towards him.
    • 便欲走 - Intending to run away.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 吳末 - The end of the Wu Kingdom, which was one of the three major states during the Three Kingdoms period (220-280 AD).
    • 臨海 - A region in present-day Zhejiang Province, China.
    • 三國 - The Three Kingdoms period, a historical era in Chinese history.
    • 射猎 - Hunting with a bow and arrow, a common activity among the nobility and warriors.
    • 蛇 - Snakes are often used in Chinese folklore and mythology to represent various symbolic meanings, including conflict and transformation.
    • 白蛇 - The white snake could symbolize purity or righteousness.
    • 黃蛇 - The yellow snake could symbolize evil or malevolence.
    • 烏衣人 - In Chinese culture, black clothing can symbolize mourning or evil.

    Proper Nouns

    • 吳 - The Wu Kingdom, one of the three major states during the Three Kingdoms period.
    • 臨海 - A region in present-day Zhejiang Province, China.
    • 射猎 - The act of hunting with a bow and arrow.

    At the end of the Wu Kingdom, a man from Linhai went into the mountains to hunt and stayed overnight. In the middle of the night, a person appeared, about ten feet tall, wearing a yellow robe and a white belt. He came directly to the hunter and said, "I have an enemy, and on a certain day, we will fight. You can help me, and I will reward you generously." The hunter replied, "I will certainly help you. Why should I thank you?" The person answered, "On the day of the meal, you can go to the edge of the stream. The enemy will come from the north, and I will go south to meet them. The one with the white belt is me, and the one with the yellow belt is the enemy." The hunter agreed. The next day, as he went out, he indeed heard a sound from the north side of the stream, like wind and rain, and the grass and trees were blown down. Looking to the south, he saw the same thing. He only saw two large snakes, each over ten feet long, meeting in the stream and entwining each other. The white snake was in a weaker position. The hunter then drew his bow and shot the yellow snake, which died immediately. As the day was ending, the person from the previous night returned, expressing gratitude and saying, "Stay here for one year of hunting, and leave next year. Do not come back, for if you do, there will be disaster." The hunter said, "I will do as you say." He stayed for one year of hunting and gained a lot, quickly becoming very wealthy. Several years later, he suddenly remembered that he had gained so much and forgot his previous promise. He returned to hunt again. He saw the person with the white belt and said, "I told you not to come back again, but you did not listen. The enemy has grown stronger, and now they will surely take revenge on you. It is not my fault." The hunter was very frightened and intended to run away. Then he saw three people wearing black clothes, all eight feet tall, opening their mouths towards him, and the hunter died immediately. [64]

    元嘉中，广州有三人，共入山中伐木。忽见石窠中有二卵，大如升，取煮之，汤始热，便闻林中如风雨声。须臾，有一蛇，大十围，长四五丈，径来，于汤中衔卵去。三人无几皆死。
    　　晋太元中，有士人嫁女于近村者，至时，夫家遣人来迎。女家好遣发，又令乳母送之。既至，重车累阁，拟于王侯。廊柱下，有灯火，一婢子严妆直守。后房帷帐甚美。至夜，女抱乳母涕泣，而口不得言。乳母密于帐中以手潜摸之，得一蛇，如数围柱，缠其女，从足至头。乳母惊走出外。柱下守灯婢子，悉是小蛇，灯火乃是蛇眼。 [65]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [65]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 忽見 - Suddenly saw, indicating a sudden and unexpected event.
    • 石窠中有二卵 - Two eggs in a stone nest, describing a rare and unusual occurrence.
    • 大如升 - As large as a winnowing basket, emphasizing the size of the eggs.
    • 湯始熱 - The soup just started to heat up, indicating the initial stage of cooking.
    • 如风雨声 - Like the sound of wind and rain, describing a loud and chaotic noise.
    • 須臾 - In a moment or soon, indicating a short period of time.
    • 大十围 - Ten hand-spans wide, a measure of the snake's size.
    • 长四五丈 - Four or five zhang long, another measure of the snake's length.
    • 径来 - Came straight, indicating the snake's direct approach.
    • 于汤中衔卵去 - Picked up the eggs from the soup and left, describing the snake's action.
    • 无几皆死 - All died shortly afterward, indicating the tragic outcome.
    • 重车累阁 - Heavy carriages and stacked pavilions, describing the elaborate and grand setup.
    • 拟于王侯 - Comparable to a king or marquis, emphasizing the opulence of the wedding.
    • 灯火 - Lanterns or lights, describing the illumination.
    • 一婢子严妆直守 - A servant girl dressed formally and standing guard, indicating the presence of a dedicated attendant.
    • 后房帷帐甚美 - The inner room had beautiful curtains and screens, describing the luxurious interior.
    • 至夜 - At night, indicating the time.
    • 女抱乳母涕泣 - The bride hugged the wet nurse and cried, describing the bride's emotional state.
    • 而口不得言 - But could not speak, indicating the bride's inability to communicate.
    • 乳母密于帐中以手潜摸之 - The wet nurse secretly felt around in the bed with her hand, describing her actions.
    • 得一蛇，如数围柱，缠其女，从足至头 - Found a snake as thick as a pillar, coiled around the bride, from her feet to her head, describing the terrifying situation.
    • 乳母惊走出外 - The wet nurse was shocked and ran out, indicating her reaction.
    • 柱下守灯婢子 - The servant girls guarding the lanterns under the columns, describing the attendants.
    • 悉是小蛇 - Were all small snakes, indicating the nature of the attendants.
    • 灯火乃是蛇眼 - The lanterns were actually snake eyes, revealing the true nature of the lights.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 元嘉 - Reign of Emperor Wen of Song (424–453 CE), a period known for its cultural and literary achievements.
    • 晋太元 - Reign of Emperor Xiaowu of Jin (376–396 CE), a period of the Eastern Jin Dynasty, known for its political and cultural developments.
    • 士人 - Scholar or educated person, typically from the upper class.
    • 嫁女 - Traditional Chinese wedding ceremony, where the bride is married to a groom.
    • 重车累阁 - Elaborate and grand setup of a traditional wedding, reflecting the high status and wealth of the families involved.
    • 王侯 - Kings and marquises, indicating the high social status of the wedding.
    • 廊柱 - Columns in a traditional Chinese building, often used for support and decoration.
    • 后房 - Inner room of a traditional Chinese house, where the bride and groom would spend their time.
    • 帷帐 - Curtains and screens, used to decorate and partition spaces in traditional Chinese architecture.

    Proper Nouns

    • 元嘉 - Reign title of Emperor Wen of Song.
    • 晋太元 - Reign title of Emperor Xiaowu of Jin.
    • 士人 - General term for a scholar or educated person.
    • 王侯 - Titles of nobility, referring to kings and marquises.

    During the reign of Emperor Wen of Song, three men from Guangzhou went into the mountains to cut wood. Suddenly, they saw two eggs in a stone nest, as large as a winnowing basket. They took them to cook, and just as the soup began to heat up, they heard a sound like wind and rain in the forest. Soon after, a snake, ten hand-spans wide and four or five zhang long, came straight to the pot and picked up the eggs and left. The three men died shortly afterward.

    During the reign of Emperor Xiaowu of Jin, a scholar married his daughter to a nearby village. On the wedding day, the husband's family sent people to come and fetch her. The bride's family prepared well and had a wet nurse accompany her. Once they arrived, there were heavy carriages and stacked pavilions, comparable to a king or marquis. Under the corridor columns, there were lanterns, and a servant girl dressed formally stood guard. The inner room had beautiful curtains and screens. At night, the bride hugged the wet nurse and cried, but could not speak. The wet nurse secretly felt around in the bed with her hand and found a snake as thick as a pillar, coiled around the bride, from her feet to her head. The wet nurse was shocked and ran out. The servant girls guarding the lanterns under the columns were all small snakes, and the lanterns were actually snake eyes. [65]

    晋咸康中，豫州刺史毛宝戍邾城。有一军人于武昌市，见人卖一白龟子，长四五寸，洁白可爱，便买取持归，著瓮中养之。七曰渐大，近欲尺许。其人怜之，持至江边，放江水中，视其去。后邾城遭石季龙攻陷，毛宝弃豫州，赴江者莫不沉溺。于时所养龟人，被铠持刀，亦同自入。既入水中，觉如堕一石上，水裁至腰。须臾，游出，中流视之，乃是先所放白龟，甲六七尺。既抵东岸，出头视此人，徐游而去。中江，犹回首视此人而没。

    　　●佚文
    　　钩□鸣於谯王无忌子妇屋上。谢充，作符悬其处。
    　　司徒蔡谟亲友王蒙者，单独，常为蔡公所怜。蒙长□三尺，无骨，登床辄令人抱上。公尝令曰捕鱼，获龟如车轮。公付厨，帐下倒悬龟著屋。蒙其夕□眠已厌。如此累夜。公闻而问蒙：“何故厌？”答云：“眠辄梦人倒悬已。”公容虑向龟。乃令人视龟所在，果倒悬著屋。公叹曰：“果如所度。”命下龟於地。於是蒙即得安寝。龟乃去。 [66]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [66]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 晋咸康中 - Refers to the year 339 during the reign of Emperor Cheng of Jin, a specific historical period.
    • 豫州刺史毛宝戍邾城 - Describes the role of Mao Bao, the governor of Yuzhou, who was stationed in Zhucheng.
    • 武昌市 - Refers to the city of Wuchang, an important location in Jiangnan.
    • 白龟子 - Literally 'white turtle baby,' referring to a small, white turtle.
    • 渐大，近欲尺许 - Means 'gradually grew, nearly reaching a foot in length.'
    • 被铠持刀 - Describes the person wearing armor and holding a sword.
    • 钩□鸣于谯王无忌子妇屋上 - Mentions the sound of a hook (or possibly a bird) on the roof of the wife of the son of Prince Tao Wuji.
    • 司徒蔡谟亲友王蒙者，单独 - Refers to Wang Meng, a close friend of Prime Minister Cai Mo, who was alone.
    • 长□三尺，无骨 - Describes Wang Meng as being three feet tall but without bones.
    • 登床辄令人抱上 - Means 'whenever he climbed onto a bed, someone had to carry him up.'
    • 公尝令曰捕鱼 - Means 'the prime minister once ordered him to catch fish.'
    • 获龟如车轮 - Describes a turtle as large as a cart wheel.
    • 付厨，帐下倒悬龟著屋 - Means 'he gave the turtle to the kitchen, and the attendant hung it upside down on the roof.'
    • 蒙其夕□眠已厌 - Means 'Wang Meng felt uneasy when he slept that night.'
    • 如此累夜 - Means 'like this for several nights.'
    • 公闻而问蒙 - Means 'the prime minister heard and asked Wang Meng.'
    • 答云 - Means 'he replied.'
    • 眠辄梦人倒悬已 - Means 'whenever I sleep, I dream of being hung upside down.'
    • 容虑向龟 - Means 'the prime minister suspected the turtle.'
    • 果倒悬著屋 - Means 'indeed, the turtle was hanging upside down on the roof.'
    • 命下龟于地 - Means 'he ordered the turtle to be taken down from the roof.'
    • 于是蒙即得安寝 - Means 'then Wang Meng could sleep peacefully.'
    • 龟乃去 - Means 'the turtle then left.'

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 晋咸康 - The reign period of Emperor Cheng of Jin, indicating the historical context.
    • 豫州刺史 - A high-ranking official responsible for the administration of Yuzhou.
    • 邾城 - A strategic location in Jiangnan, often contested during the Eastern Jin period.
    • 石季龙 - Shi Jiulong, a prominent warlord during the Sixteen Kingdoms period, known for his military campaigns against the Eastern Jin.
    • 武昌市 - An important city in Jiangnan, known for its strategic and economic significance.
    • 司徒蔡谟 - Cai Mo, a high-ranking official and scholar during the Eastern Jin period.
    • 谯王无忌 - Prince Tao Wuji, a member of the imperial family.
    • 王蒙 - A close friend of Cai Mo, known for his unique physical condition.
    • 钩□鸣 - The sound of a hook or possibly a bird, often associated with omens or supernatural occurrences.
    • 倒悬龟著屋 - The act of hanging a turtle upside down on the roof, which is unusual and may have symbolic or superstitious significance.
    • 饭牛牧羊 - Feeding cattle and herding sheep, common occupations in ancient China.
    • 饭牛牧羊，皆有先达，可为师表 - A statement emphasizing that even humble occupations have worthy predecessors who can serve as models.

    Proper Nouns

    • 晋咸康 - The reign period of Emperor Cheng of Jin.
    • 豫州刺史毛宝 - Mao Bao, the governor of Yuzhou.
    • 邾城 - Zhucheng, a strategic location in Jiangnan.
    • 石季龙 - Shi Jiulong, a prominent warlord.
    • 武昌市 - Wuchang, an important city in Jiangnan.
    • 司徒蔡谟 - Cai Mo, a high-ranking official and scholar.
    • 谯王无忌 - Prince Tao Wuji, a member of the imperial family.
    • 王蒙 - Wang Meng, a close friend of Cai Mo.
    • 钩□ - A hook or possibly a bird, often associated with omens.
    • 饭牛牧羊 - Feeding cattle and herding sheep, common occupations.
    • 先达 - Worthy predecessors who can serve as models.
    • 师表 - Teachers and models.
    • 博学求之 - Seeking knowledge broadly from many sources.
    • 无不利于事也 - Nothing is unhelpful in matters, suggesting that all these roles or pursuits are valuable in their own right.

    Translation

    During the reign of Emperor Cheng of Jin (339 AD), Mao Bao, the governor of Yuzhou, was stationed in Zhucheng. A soldier in Wuchang saw someone selling a small, white turtle, about four to five inches long, which was white and very beautiful. He bought it and took it home, placing it in a jar to raise it. After seven days, the turtle gradually grew, nearly reaching a foot in length. The soldier pitied it and took it to the riverbank, releasing it into the river, watching it swim away.

    Later, Zhucheng was attacked and captured by Shi Jiulong, and Mao Bao abandoned Yuzhou, with those who fled to the river all drowning. At that time, the soldier who had raised the turtle also put on armor and held a sword, and followed them into the river. Once in the water, he felt as if he had fallen onto a stone, with water only reaching his waist. After a while, he swam out, looking around in the middle of the river, and saw that it was the white turtle he had previously released, which was now six or seven feet long. The turtle reached the eastern shore, raised its head to look at the soldier, and then slowly swam away. In the middle of the river, it still turned its head to look back at the soldier before disappearing.

    Additional Anecdotes

    A hook or bird was heard singing on the roof of the wife of the son of Prince Tao Wuji. Xie Cong, the prime minister, hung a talisman there.

    Wang Meng, a close friend of Prime Minister Cai Mo, was alone and often pitied by Cai Mo. Wang Meng was three feet tall but had no bones, and whenever he climbed onto a bed, someone had to carry him up. Cai Mo once ordered him to catch fish, and he caught a turtle as large as a cart wheel. Cai Mo gave the turtle to the kitchen, and the attendant hung it upside down on the roof. Wang Meng felt uneasy when he slept that night, and this continued for several nights. Cai Mo heard about this and asked Wang Meng, "Why are you feeling uneasy?" Wang Meng replied, "Whenever I sleep, I dream of being hung upside down." Cai Mo suspected the turtle, so he had someone check where the turtle was, and indeed, it was hanging upside down on the roof. Cai Mo sighed, "It’s just as I thought." He ordered the turtle to be taken down from the roof. Then Wang Meng could sleep peacefully, and the turtle then left. [66]

    宗渊字叔林，南阳人。晋太元中，为寻阳太守，有数十头龟付厨，敕旦且以二头作□，便著潘汁，□中养之。其暮梦有十丈夫，并著乌布裤褶，自反缚，向宗渊叩首，若求哀。明旦，厨人宰二龟。其暮复梦八人求哀如初。宗渊方悟，令勿杀。明夜还梦见八人来，跪谢恩。於是惊觉。明朝自人庐山放之，遂不复食龟。
    　　熊无穴，居大树孔中。东土呼熊为子路。以物击树云：“子路可见。”於是便下。不呼则不动也。
    　　鄱阳县民黄赭，入山采荆杨子，遂迷不知道。数曰，饥饿，忽见一大龟，赭便咒曰：“汝是灵物，吾迷不知道，今骑汝背，示吾路。”龟即回右转，赭即从行。去十馀里，便至溪水，见贾客行船。赭即往乞食，便语船人曰：“我向者於溪边见一龟，甚大，可共往取之。”言讫，面即生疮。既往，亦复不见龟。还家数曰，病疮而死。 [67]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [67]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 宗渊字叔林 - Standard biographical formula meaning 'Zong Yuan, courtesy name Shulin.'
    • 晋太元中 - Refers to the period during the reign of Emperor Xiaowu of Jin (376–396 CE).
    • 为寻阳太守 - Means 'served as the governor of Xunyang (Jiujiang).'
